text_content:
is the base information unit in DITA. Every piece of content lives inside a topic. A topic is a standalone, self-contained unit that answers ONE question or covers ONE subject. What is ? It's the root element for all DITA content. Every DITA file has exactly one root topic (or a specialization like , , ). Topics are designed to be reused across multiple maps and outputs. Structure: - (required) — the topic heading - <shortdesc> (optional) — brief summary shown in search results and link previews - <prolog> (optional) — metadata: author, copyright, audience, keywords - <body> (required) — main content with sections, paragraphs, lists, tables - <related-links> (optional) — cross-references to other topics Example: ```xml <topic id="install-guide" xml:lang="en"> <title>Installing AEM Guides Steps to install AEM Guides on your AEM instance. Adobe Documentation Team
Prerequisites

Ensure you have AEM 6.5 SP18 or later.

 ``` Key rules: - @id attribute is required and must be unique within the file - One topic per file is the recommended best practice - Topics should be self-contained — avoid assumptions about reading order In AEM Guides: Topics are the primary authoring unit in the Web Editor. Each .dita file appears in the Repository panel and can be added to maps via drag-and-drop.
usage_contexts: Base topic type — use when content doesn't fit concept, task, or reference
Generic information units that combine explanation and procedure

text_content:
is a topic specialization for conceptual/explanatory information. Use it when you need to explain WHAT something is, not HOW to do it. What is ? It's a specialized topic type designed for background information, definitions, overviews, and explanations. The body element is (not ), which allows sections, examples, and block content. When to use: - Explaining what a feature is and why it exists - Providing background knowledge before a procedure - Defining terms, architectures, or workflows Example: ```xml Understanding Content Reuse in DITA Content reuse lets you write once and publish everywhere.
What is content reuse?

Content reuse is the practice of writing content once and referencing it from multiple locations using mechanisms like conref, keyref, and conkeyref.

Benefits
  • Consistency across documents
  • Reduced translation costs
  • Single-source updates
 ``` Comparison with other topic types: | Type | Purpose | Body element | Use when... | |------|---------|-------------|-------------| | concept | Explain what/why | | Explaining a feature or idea | | task | Step-by-step how-to | | Giving instructions | | reference | Lookup data | | API docs, parameter tables | In AEM Guides: When you create a new topic in the Web Editor, you can choose 'Concept' from the template list. The editor enforces the structure.
usage_contexts: Explanatory/conceptual information — what something is, why it matters
Background knowledge, overviews, definitions

text_content:
is a topic specialization for step-by-step procedures. Use it when you need to tell someone HOW to do something. What is ? It's a structured topic type with a strict content model: prerequisites → context → steps → result. This enforced structure ensures consistent, scannable procedures across your documentation. Structure: - contains: - — what the user needs before starting - — brief intro explaining why/when to do this - — ordered procedure with / pairs - — what happens after completing all steps - — follow-up actions Example: ```xml Creating a PDF Output Preset Create a preset to generate PDF output from your DITA map.

You must have Editor or Administrator role in AEM Guides.

Output presets define how your DITA content is transformed into deliverables.

 Open your DITA map in the Map Dashboard. Click the Output Presets tab. Click Add and select PDF.

You can customize the PDF template in the preset settings.

 Click Generate to produce the PDF.

The PDF appears in the Outputs panel when generation completes.

Your PDF output preset is saved and can be reused for future publishing.

 ``` Key rules: - Each must have exactly one (the instruction) - Use for supplementary details within a step - Use to describe what happens after a step - For single-step tasks, use or a simplified structure In AEM Guides: The Web Editor provides a Task template with the full structure pre-populated. Steps are rendered as numbered lists in all output formats.
usage_contexts: Step-by-step procedures — how to do something
Installation, configuration, troubleshooting steps

Missing <cmd> inside <step> — every <step> MUST have exactly one <cmd>

text_content:
<reference> is a topic specialization for lookup/reference material. Use it for API docs, parameter tables, command references, and specifications.

What is <reference>?
It's a structured topic designed for content that users scan rather than read linearly — tables of properties, lists of parameters, syntax definitions.

Example:
```xml
<reference id="output-preset-params">
  <title>PDF Output Preset Parameters</title>
  <shortdesc>Configuration parameters for PDF output generation.</shortdesc>
  <refbody>
    <section><title>General Settings</title>
      <simpletable>
        <sthead><stentry>Parameter</stentry><stentry>Description</stentry><stentry>Default</stentry></sthead>
        <strow><stentry>paper-size</stentry><stentry>Page dimensions for PDF</stentry><stentry>A4</stentry></strow>
        <strow><stentry>orientation</stentry><stentry>Portrait or landscape</stentry><stentry>portrait</stentry></strow>
      </simpletable>
    </section>
    <properties>
      <property><proptype>String</proptype><propvalue>template-name</propvalue><propdesc>Name of the PDF template to use.</propdesc></property>
    </properties>
  </refbody>
</reference>
```

In AEM Guides: Reference topics work well for documenting AEM Guides configuration parameters, API endpoints, and DITA element catalogs.
usage_contexts: Lookup/reference material — API docs, parameter tables, command references
Property tables, configuration options, glossary-style content
common_mistakes: Using <body> instead of <refbody> — reference topics require <refbody>
Putting content directly inside <reference> without <refbody>

text_content:
<map> is the root element of a DITA map (.ditamap file). It organizes topics into a navigable structure — like a table of contents for your documentation.

What is <map>?
A map does NOT contain content itself. Instead, it references topics via <topicref> and defines their hierarchy, order, and relationships. Maps control navigation (TOC), key definitions, and linking.

Key children:
- <title> — map title
- <topicref> — reference to a topic (the workhorse)
- <topichead> — visible heading without a topic file
- <topicgroup> — invisible grouping container
- <mapref> — reference to another map (submap)
- <keydef> — key definition for indirect references
- <reltable> — relationship table for cross-linking

Example:
```xml
<map id="aem-guides-admin">
  <title>AEM Guides Administration Guide</title>

  <!-- Key definitions for reuse -->
  <keydef keys="product-name">
    <topicmeta><linktext>AEM Guides</linktext></topicmeta>
  </keydef>

  <!-- Visible section heading -->
  <topichead navtitle="Installation">
    <topicref href="topics/prerequisites.dita"/>
    <topicref href="topics/install-steps.dita"/>
  </topichead>

  <!-- Silent grouping (no heading in output) -->
  <topicgroup>
    <topicref href="topics/config-general.dita"/>
    <topicref href="topics/config-advanced.dita"/>
  </topicgroup>

  <!-- Include a submap -->
  <mapref href="submaps/troubleshooting.ditamap"/>

  <!-- Relationship table for cross-linking -->
  <reltable>
    <relrow>
      <relcell><topicref href="topics/prerequisites.dita"/></relcell>
      <relcell><topicref href="topics/install-steps.dita"/></relcell>
    </relrow>
  </reltable>
</map>
```

In AEM Guides: Maps are opened in the Map Dashboard for publishing. The Web Editor's map view shows the hierarchy as a tree. You can drag-and-drop topics to reorder.

<topicref> is the core building block of a DITA map. It creates a reference to an actual topic file (.dita) and says: 'include this topic in the output.'

What is <topicref>?
Every topic you want in your published output needs a <topicref> in the map. It's the workhorse element that builds your TOC, defines navigation order, and controls which topics get processed.

Key attributes:
- @href — path to the .dita topic file
- @navtitle — override title for TOC (instead of using the topic's <title>)
- @type — topic type hint (topic, concept, task, reference)
- @scope — local (default), peer, or external
- @format — dita (default), html, pdf
- @collection-type — unordered, sequence (prev/next), family (sibling links)
- @chunk — controls output splitting/merging

Example:
```xml
<map>
  <title>User Guide</title>

  <!-- Basic topicref -->
  <topicref href="getting-started.dita"/>

  <!-- Nested hierarchy (creates parent-child in TOC) -->
  <topicref href="configuration.dita">
    <topicref href="config-general.dita"/>
    <topicref href="config-advanced.dita"/>
  </topicref>

  <!-- With navtitle override -->
  <topicref href="api-reference.dita" navtitle="API Docs"/>

  <!-- Sequential (adds prev/next navigation) -->
  <topicref href="tutorials.dita" collection-type="sequence">
    <topicref href="tutorial-01.dita"/>
    <topicref href="tutorial-02.dita"/>
    <topicref href="tutorial-03.dita"/>
  </topicref>
</map>
```

Comparison: topicref vs topichead vs topicgroup
| Element | Points to a file? | Visible in TOC? | Purpose |
|---------|-------------------|-----------------|----------|
| <topicref> | Yes (href) | Yes | Include a topic in the build |
| <topichead> | No | Yes | Visible section heading only |
| <topicgroup> | No | No | Silent/invisible grouping |

In AEM Guides: In the Web Editor map view, topicrefs show the linked topic's title. You can right-click to open the referenced topic, check its status, or remove it from the map.
usage_contexts: Core building block of DITA maps — references a topic file
Creates a visible, clickable entry in the table of contents
common_mistakes: Using key= (singular) instead of keys= (PLURAL): <topicref href='x.dita' key='x'/> is WRONG
navtitle on topicref without locktitle='yes' — processor ignores navtitle without locktitle='yes'
Forgetting that topicref needs href to point to a topic file
correct_examples:
<map>
  <title>User Guide</title>
  <!-- keys= is PLURAL, not key= -->
  <topicref href="install.dita" keys="install-topic"/>
  <!-- navtitle requires locktitle="yes" to take effect -->
  <topicref href="configure.dita" navtitle="Setup" locktitle="yes"/>
</map>

text_content:
<topichead> is a title-only container in a DITA map. It creates a visible heading in the TOC/navigation but does NOT point to any topic file.

What is <topichead>?
Use it when you want a chapter or section heading in your output's table of contents without creating a separate topic file just for that heading. It's purely organizational — it groups child topicrefs under a labeled node.

Key difference from <topicref>:
- <topicref> points to a .dita file → produces a page in the output
- <topichead> has NO href → produces a heading only, no content page

Example:
```xml
<map>
  <title>AEM Guides Admin Guide</title>

  <!-- topichead = visible heading, NO linked topic file -->
  <topichead navtitle="Installation">
    <topicref href="prerequisites.dita"/>
    <topicref href="install-steps.dita"/>
    <topicref href="verify-install.dita"/>
  </topichead>

  <!-- Compare with topicref = actual topic file -->
  <topicref href="release-notes.dita"/>
</map>
```

Output TOC:
  Installation              ← topichead (heading only, no page)
    ├── Prerequisites       ← topicref (clickable page)
    ├── Install Steps       ← topicref (clickable page)
    └── Verify Install      ← topicref (clickable page)
  Release Notes             ← topicref (clickable page)

Comparison:
| Element | Visible in TOC? | Has a page? | Title source |
|---------|----------------|-------------|---------------|
| <topicref> | Yes | Yes | From topic's <title> or @navtitle |
| <topichead> | Yes | No | From @navtitle attribute only |
| <topicgroup> | No | No | None |

When to use <topichead> vs a parent <topicref>:
- Use <topichead> when there's no meaningful content for the heading — it's just organizational
- Use a parent <topicref> when the heading itself has content (e.g., an overview page)

In AEM Guides: In the Web Editor map view, topicheads appear as folder-like nodes. In PDF output, they generate chapter/section headings in the TOC but no content page. In AEM Sites output, they create navigation nodes.

<topicgroup> is a non-titled grouping element in a DITA map. It lets you organize topics together WITHOUT creating a visible heading or label in the output.

What is <topicgroup>?
It's an invisible container. Unlike <topichead> (which shows a heading) or <topicref> (which includes a topic), <topicgroup> is completely silent in the output. It exists only for authoring convenience — to apply shared metadata, attributes, or conditional processing to a batch of topics.

Example:
```xml
<map>
  <title>AEM Guides Documentation</title>

  <!-- This group has NO title in the output -->
  <topicgroup>
    <topicref href="install.dita"/>
    <topicref href="configure.dita"/>
    <topicref href="deploy.dita"/>
  </topicgroup>

  <!-- Compare with topichead, which DOES have a visible title -->
  <topichead navtitle="Troubleshooting">
    <topicref href="errors.dita"/>
    <topicref href="faq.dita"/>
  </topichead>
</map>
```

Comparison of all three grouping elements:
| Element | Visible in TOC? | Has a title? | Links to a file? | Purpose |
|---------|----------------|-------------|------------------|----------|
| <topicref> | Yes | Yes | Yes | Include a topic |
| <topichead> | Yes | Yes (navtitle) | No | Visible section label |
| <topicgroup> | No | No | No | Silent/invisible grouping |

Common uses:
- Apply shared metadata (audience, platform, product) to a batch of topics at once
- Apply conditional processing (@props, @audience) to a group without affecting navigation structure
- Organize the map source for authors without impacting the reader-facing output

Nested topicgroups:
```xml
<topicgroup>
  <topicref href="admin-overview.dita"/>
  <topicgroup>
    <topicref href="admin-users.dita"/>
    <topicref href="admin-roles.dita"/>
  </topicgroup>
</topicgroup>
```

In AEM Guides: In the Web Editor map view, topicgroups appear as structural containers but won't generate headings in PDF or AEM Sites output. They are useful for applying DITAVAL filtering to groups of topics.

<topicset> is a named, reusable group of topicrefs in a DITA map. Think of it as a 'chapter module' that can be referenced from other maps.

What is <topicset>?
Like <topicgroup>, it groups topics — but with a key identity (@id). Other maps can reference an entire topicset using <topicsetref>, enabling modular map architectures where chapters are assembled from reusable building blocks.

Example:
```xml
<!-- In shared-chapters.ditamap -->
<map>
  <topicset id="install-chapter">
    <topicref href="prerequisites.dita"/>
    <topicref href="install-steps.dita"/>
    <topicref href="verify.dita"/>
  </topicset>
</map>

<!-- In product-guide.ditamap -->
<map>
  <title>Product Guide</title>
  <topicsetref href="shared-chapters.ditamap#install-chapter"/>
</map>
```

Comparison:
| Element | Reusable by ID? | Visible in TOC? | Purpose |
|---------|----------------|-----------------|----------|
| <topicgroup> | No | No | Silent grouping |
| <topicset> | Yes (@id) | Depends | Named, reusable chapter module |
| <topichead> | No | Yes | Visible heading |

In AEM Guides: Topicsets enable modular documentation architectures where shared chapters (like 'Legal Notices' or 'Getting Started') are maintained once and included in multiple guides.

<navref> references another map purely for navigation purposes. Unlike <mapref>, it only pulls in the navigation structure — not key definitions or relationship tables.

What is <navref>?
Use it when you want to include another map's TOC entries in your navigation without importing its keys or reltables. This prevents key collisions when combining maps.

Example:
```xml
<map>
  <title>Master Guide</title>
  <topicref href="intro.dita"/>
  <!-- Pull in sub-guide navigation only -->
  <navref mapref="admin-guide.ditamap"/>
  <navref mapref="developer-guide.ditamap"/>
</map>
```

Comparison: mapref vs navref
| Element | Imports navigation? | Imports keys? | Imports reltables? |
|---------|-------------------|--------------|--------------------|
| <mapref> | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| <navref> | Yes | No | No |

In AEM Guides: Use navref when assembling a master guide from sub-guides but you want each sub-guide's keys to remain isolated.

<mapref> references another DITA map, pulling it into the current map as a submap. It imports everything: navigation, key definitions, and relationship tables.

What is <mapref>?
It's how you build modular documentation. A master map references submaps, each covering a section or guide. All keys, topicrefs, and reltables from the submap become part of the parent map's scope.

Example:
```xml
<map>
  <title>AEM Guides Complete Documentation</title>

  <!-- Each submap is a self-contained guide -->
  <mapref href="submaps/installation-guide.ditamap"/>
  <mapref href="submaps/user-guide.ditamap"/>
  <mapref href="submaps/admin-guide.ditamap"/>
  <mapref href="submaps/api-reference.ditamap"/>
</map>
```

Key rules:
- The submap's <keydef> elements become available in the parent scope
- Use @keyscope on the mapref to namespace the submap's keys and prevent collisions
- format='ditamap' is the default

In AEM Guides: Maprefs are shown as expandable submap nodes in the Web Editor map view. You can open the submap directly from the parent map.

<bookmap> is a map specialization for book-like publications with frontmatter, chapters, appendices, and backmatter.

What is <bookmap>?
While a regular <map> is flexible and flat, <bookmap> enforces a book structure: title page → preface → chapters → appendices → index. Use it when your output is a formal document (PDF book, printed manual).

Structure:
- <booktitle> / <booktitlealt> — book title and subtitle
- <bookmeta> — book-level metadata (authors, publishers, ISBN)
- <frontmatter> — preface, TOC, notices
- <chapter> — main content sections (each contains topicrefs)
- <part> — groups chapters (for very large books)
- <appendices> — supplementary material
- <backmatter> — index, glossary, colophon

Example:
```xml
<bookmap id="aem-guides-book">
  <booktitle>
    <mainbooktitle>AEM Guides Administrator Guide</mainbooktitle>
    <booktitlealt>Version 4.6</booktitlealt>
  </booktitle>
  <bookmeta>
    <author>Adobe Documentation Team</author>
  </bookmeta>

  <frontmatter>
    <topicref href="preface.dita"/>
  </frontmatter>

  <chapter href="installation.dita">
    <topicref href="prerequisites.dita"/>
    <topicref href="install-steps.dita"/>
  </chapter>

  <chapter href="configuration.dita">
    <topicref href="config-general.dita"/>
    <topicref href="config-advanced.dita"/>
  </chapter>

  <appendices>
    <topicref href="troubleshooting.dita"/>
  </appendices>

  <backmatter>
    <topicref href="glossary.dita"/>
  </backmatter>
</bookmap>
```

Comparison: map vs bookmap
| Feature | <map> | <bookmap> |
|---------|-------|-----------|
| Structure | Flexible, flat or nested | Book-like: chapters, parts, appendices |
| Metadata | Basic <topicmeta> | Rich <bookmeta> (ISBN, publisher, etc.) |
| Best for | Web help, site output | PDF books, printed manuals |
| Frontmatter/backmatter | No | Yes |

In AEM Guides: Bookmaps are fully supported. The PDF output engine uses the bookmap structure to generate title pages, chapter numbering, and appendix letters automatically.
common_mistakes: Using <topicref> directly under <bookmap> - use <chapter>
Missing <booktitle>/<mainbooktitle>
Confusing bookmap with ditamap structure
correct_examples:
<bookmap id="admin_guide">
  <booktitle><mainbooktitle>Admin Guide</mainbooktitle></booktitle>
  <frontmatter><booklists><toc/></booklists></frontmatter>
  <chapter href="install.dita">
    <topicref href="prereqs.dita"/>
  </chapter>
  <backmatter><booklists><indexlist/></booklists></backmatter>
</bookmap>
usage_contexts: Book-style publications with chapters and parts
Supports frontmatter/backmatter

is a topic specialization for glossary definitions. Each glossentry defines ONE term. What is ? It provides a structured way to define terminology. Each entry has a term () and a definition (), plus optional variants like acronyms, abbreviations, and usage notes. Example: ```xml conref

Content reference — a DITA mechanism for reusing content by referencing another element's ID. The referenced content replaces the referencing element at build time.

 content reference (conref) conref ``` Building a glossary: ```xml ``` In AEM Guides: Glossary entries can be linked via keyref so that hovering over a term in the Web Editor shows its definition. This enables tooltip-style glossary popups in web output.
common_mistakes: Using <body> or <conbody> instead of <glossdef>
Wrong child structure - expects <glossterm> then <glossdef>
Missing id on <glossentry>
Putting definition directly in <glossentry>

<glossentry id="gl_conref">
  <glossterm>conref</glossterm>
  <glossdef>A DITA attribute for content reuse.</glossdef>

text_content:
(content reference) is DITA's primary content reuse mechanism. It pulls content from one element into another — write once, reuse everywhere. What is conref? The @conref attribute on any DITA element points to a source element. At build time, the source content replaces the referencing element. The source and target must be the SAME element type (or the target must specialize from the source type). Syntax: conref="file.dita#topicId/elementId" Example: ```xml

Always back up your content before making bulk changes.

 ``` Conref range (conrefend): ```xml ``` Key rules: - Source and target must be the same element type - The referencing element must be valid (have required children like in ) - The conref path is: filename#topicId/elementId - Use conkeyref for indirect references via keys (more maintainable) Comparison: | Mechanism | Direct/Indirect | Syntax | Best for | |-----------|----------------|--------|----------| | conref | Direct (by path) | conref="file.dita#id/id" | Simple, single-source reuse | | conkeyref | Indirect (by key) | conkeyref="keyname/elementId" | Multi-product reuse, variable content | | keyref | Indirect (by key) | keyref="keyname" | Text variables, link targets | In AEM Guides: The Web Editor shows conref'd content with a blue border. You can right-click a conref to navigate to the source. Broken conrefs are flagged in the validation panel.
attributes: {"conref": "required \u2014 path to source element. Format: file.dita#topicId/elementId", "conrefend": "optional \u2014 end of range. Format: file.dita#topicId/lastElementId. Source and end must be siblings", "conaction": "optional \u2014 pushconref action: pushafter|pushbefore|pushreplace|mark. For PUSH conref (reverse direction reuse)"}
usage_contexts: Reuse content by pulling it from a source element into the current element
Share warnings, notes, steps, paragraphs across multiple topics
Write once, reuse everywhere — single source of truth
common_mistakes: Writing <conref .../> as if conref is an ELEMENT — it is NOT. There is NO <conref> element in DITA
conref is an ATTRIBUTE placed on the element to be replaced: <note conref='...'/>
Using conref='file.dita#elementId' — WRONG: missing topicId. Correct: conref='file.dita#topicId/elementId'
Using conref='file.dita#topicId' — WRONG: missing /elementId after topicId. Must point to specific element
Source and target element types must match — cannot conref a into a
Forgetting to give the source element an @id attribute — conref target MUST have id

Using conref inside

to insert inline text — conref replaces the WHOLE element, not just text
Conref to an element in a different topic type without matching structure — specialization rules apply
Not providing required child elements in the referencing element — e.g. <step conref='...'> MUST have <cmd/> as placeholder
Using conref for text variables — use keyref instead. Conref is for structural reuse, keyref for text substitution
correct_examples:
Shared Warnings

Always back up your content before making bulk changes.

Do not attempt to repair the device yourself.

text_content:
<conkeyref> is an indirect content reference that resolves through a key. It combines the reuse power of conref with the flexibility of keys.

What is conkeyref?
Instead of hardcoding a file path (like conref), conkeyref uses a key name defined in the map. This means you can swap the source file by changing the keydef — without touching every topic that reuses the content.

Syntax: conkeyref="keyname/elementId"

Example:
```xml
<!-- In your map: define the key -->
<keydef keys="shared-warnings" href="shared/warnings-v2.dita"/>

<!-- In any topic: reference by key -->
<note conkeyref="shared-warnings/backup-warning"/>
```

Why use conkeyref over conref?
- Change the source file in ONE place (the keydef) instead of updating every conref
- Different maps can point the same key to different files (product variants)
- Easier to manage in large documentation sets

In AEM Guides: Conkeyrefs resolve based on the root map's key definitions. The Web Editor resolves and displays conkeyref content when a root map is selected.
attributes: {"conkeyref": "required \u2014 indirect reference. Format: keyname/elementId", "conrefend": "optional \u2014 end of range for indirect conref range. Format: keyname/lastElementId"}
usage_contexts: Indirect content reuse via keys — more maintainable than conref
Multi-product documentation where the same key resolves to different sources
common_mistakes: conkeyref='keyname' without /elementId — MUST be conkeyref='keyname/elementId'
Using <conkeyref .../> as an element — conkeyref is an ATTRIBUTE, not an element
Forgetting to define the key in the ditamap via <keydef keys='keyname' href='source.dita'/>
Using conkeyref='keyname#topicId/elementId' — WRONG: conkeyref uses key/elemId, NOT key#topic/elem
Missing root map selection in AEM Guides — conkeyref won't resolve without a root map
Not providing placeholder content — element must still be valid DITA (e.g. <step> needs <cmd/>)
correct_examples:
<!-- In ditamap -->
<keydef keys="shared-warnings" href="shared/warnings.dita"/>

<!-- In topic — conkeyref on <note> -->
<note conkeyref="shared-warnings/backup-warning">placeholder</note>

<!-- conkeyref on <step> (placeholder <cmd> required) -->
<step conkeyref="shared-steps/step-save"><cmd/></step>

<!-- Multi-product setup: same key, different source per map -->
<!-- Product A map -->
<keydef keys="install-steps" href="product-a/install.dita"/>
<!-- Product B map -->
<keydef keys="install-steps" href="product-b/install.dita"/>

<!-- Shared topic — works for both products -->
<step conkeyref="install-steps/download-step"><cmd/></step>

text_content:
references content or a link target by key name, defined in the map via . It's DITA's variable system. What is keyref? Keys are indirect references. Instead of hardcoding a file path or text value, you reference a key name. The map's resolves the key to its actual value at build time. This enables: - Text variables (product names, version numbers) - Indirect links (swap link targets without editing topics) - Conditional content (different keydefs per product/audience) Example — text variable: ```xml AEM Guides 4.6

Welcome to version .

``` Example — indirect link: ```xml

Visit the support portal for help.

``` Comparison: | Mechanism | What it references | Defined in | Use case | |-----------|-------------------|-----------|----------| | @href | Direct file path | Topic | Static links | | @keyref | Key name | Map () | Variables, indirect links | | @conref | Element by path | Topic | Content reuse (by path) | | @conkeyref | Element by key | Map () | Content reuse (by key) | In AEM Guides: Keys resolve based on the selected root map. The Web Editor shows resolved keyref values inline. You can see all key definitions in the Map Dashboard > Key Definitions panel.
attributes: {"keyref": "required \u2014 key name defined in the map via <keydef>. Resolves to text, link, or resource at build time"}
usage_contexts: Text variables — product name, version, company name via <keyword keyref='...'/>
Indirect links — swap link targets by changing keydef in map
Image references via key — <image keyref='logo'/>
Glossary term resolution — <keyword keyref='gl-conref'/> shows glossterm with tooltip in AEM Guides
common_mistakes: Confusing keyref with conkeyref — keyref creates LINKS or inserts TEXT, conkeyref COPIES CONTENT from elements
Using keyref to try to copy structured content — keyref does NOT copy element trees, use conkeyref
Forgetting to define the key in the ditamap via <keydef>
Using keyref on <keyword> without a matching <keyword> in the keydef's <topicmeta> — text won't resolve
Missing root map selection in AEM Guides — keys don't resolve without root map context
Using keyref='key#id' syntax — WRONG: keyref takes just the key name, not a path
correct_examples:
AEM Guides 4.6

Welcome to version .
Visit Adobe Support for help.

text_content:
<keydef> defines a key in a DITA map. Keys enable indirect references — the foundation of DITA's variable and linking system.

What is <keydef>?
It's a specialized <topicref> that defines a key name (@keys) and optionally points to a resource (@href). Other elements reference this key via @keyref, @conkeyref, or @conref.

Example — text variable:
```xml
<keydef keys="product-name">
  <topicmeta>
    <linktext>Adobe Experience Manager Guides</linktext>
  </topicmeta>
</keydef>
```

Example — link target:
```xml
<keydef keys="admin-guide" href="admin-guide.dita" type="topic"/>
```

Example — image key:
```xml
<keydef keys="company-logo" href="images/adobe-logo.png" format="png"/>
```

Key scoping:
```xml
<!-- Different product variants -->
<topicref keyscope="cloud">
  <keydef keys="product-name"><topicmeta><linktext>AEM Guides Cloud</linktext></topicmeta></keydef>
</topicref>
<topicref keyscope="on-prem">
  <keydef keys="product-name"><topicmeta><linktext>AEM Guides On-Premise</linktext></topicmeta></keydef>
</topicref>
```

In AEM Guides: Key definitions are visible in the Map Dashboard. The Web Editor auto-completes key names when you type keyref attributes.
usage_contexts: Define a key in a DITA map — invisible in TOC, used for indirect references
Variable text (product names, versions), resource links, conkeyref targets
common_mistakes: Using key= (singular) instead of keys= (PLURAL): <keydef key='x'/> is WRONG
Expecting keydef to appear in the TOC — keydef is always invisible in output
correct_examples:
<!-- keys= is PLURAL -->
<keydef keys="product-name">
  <topicmeta><keywords><keyword>AEM Guides</keyword></keywords></topicmeta>
</keydef>
<keydef keys="shared-warnings" href="shared/warnings.dita"/>

text_content:
creates a namespace for key resolution. Child keydefs are scoped to that namespace, preventing key collisions across submaps. What is keyscope? When you have multiple submaps that each define a key named 'product-name' with different values, keyscope disambiguates them. You reference scoped keys as 'scopename.keyname'. Example: ```xml AEM Guides Cloud Service AEM Guides 4.6

This guide covers .

``` In AEM Guides: Keyscopes are resolved during output generation. Each map/submap can define its own scope, and the root map's scope takes precedence.

<reltable> (relationship table) defines cross-linking relationships between topics in a map. It's the preferred way to create related-topic links without embedding links inside topics.

What is <reltable>?
Instead of adding <xref> or <related-links> inside topics (which creates tight coupling), reltables define relationships centrally in the map. This keeps topics self-contained and reusable.

Structure:
- Each row (<relrow>) defines a set of related topics
- Each cell (<relcell>) contains topicrefs to topics of a specific type
- Common pattern: 3 columns for concept, task, reference

Example:
```xml
<reltable title="Installation Relationships">
  <relheader>
    <relcolspec type="concept"/>
    <relcolspec type="task"/>
    <relcolspec type="reference"/>
  </relheader>
  <relrow>
    <relcell><topicref href="what-is-aem-guides.dita"/></relcell>
    <relcell><topicref href="install-aem-guides.dita"/></relcell>
    <relcell><topicref href="system-requirements.dita"/></relcell>
  </relrow>
  <relrow>
    <relcell><topicref href="content-reuse-overview.dita"/></relcell>
    <relcell><topicref href="create-conref.dita"/></relcell>
    <relcell><topicref href="conref-reference.dita"/></relcell>
  </relrow>
</reltable>
```

How it works:
- Topics in the SAME row are linked to each other
- Topics in the SAME cell are NOT linked to each other
- Links appear in the 'Related Topics' section of each topic's output

Best practices:
- Put reltables at the END of the map (after all topicrefs)
- Use descriptive @title for author reference
- Prefer reltables over topic-level <related-links> for maintainability

In AEM Guides: Reltable links appear in the 'Related Topics' section in both PDF and AEM Sites output. The Web Editor has a visual reltable editor for managing relationships.
common_mistakes: Placing reltable inside a topic - goes in the map
Missing <relrow> wrapper
Not understanding reltable is invisible in TOC
correct_examples:
<map>
  <reltable>
    <relheader>
      <relcolspec type="concept"/>
      <relcolspec type="task"/>
    </relheader>
    <relrow>
      <relcell><topicref href="about.dita"/></relcell>
      <relcell><topicref href="configure.dita"/></relcell>
    </relrow>
  </reltable>
</map>
usage_contexts: Cross-linking related topics in a map
Creates related-links sections in output
Invisible in TOC

text_content:
divides body content into logical parts. Each section can have an optional title. What is
? It's a structural division within . Use sections to organize content into labeled groups. Sections CANNOT nest inside other sections — use or for nested grouping. Example: ```xml
Overview

This topic covers DITA map structure.

Map Elements

A map contains topicref, topichead, and topicgroup elements.

  • topicref — references a topic file
  • topichead — visible heading only
  • topicgroup — silent grouping
``` Key rule: Sections cannot nest inside sections. This keeps topic structure flat and scannable. In AEM Guides: Sections are rendered with their titles as sub-headings. In PDF output, section titles appear as bold headings within the topic.

text_content:
contains illustrative sample content — code samples, configurations, demonstrations. What is ? It's a semantic container that tells processors 'this is an example.' It gets special rendering in output (often indented or boxed). Unlike
, examples can appear inside sections. Example: ```xml
Using conref

To reuse a warning note across topics:

Conref Example <note conref="shared/warnings.dita#shared/backup-warning"/>

This pulls in the warning from the shared file at build time.
``` In AEM Guides: Examples are visually distinguished in both the Web Editor and output formats.

<shortdesc> is a brief description of the topic. It appears in search results, link previews, and hover tooltips.

What is <shortdesc>?
It's a one-or-two sentence summary that helps users decide whether to read the full topic. It appears directly after the title in many output formats and is used as the description in link previews and search results.

Example:
```xml
<topic id="install">
  <title>Installing AEM Guides</title>
  <shortdesc>Step-by-step instructions for installing AEM Guides on your AEM instance.</shortdesc>
  <body>...</body>
</topic>
```

Best practices:
- Keep it under 50 words
- Make it self-contained (don't start with 'This topic...')
- Focus on what the reader will learn or accomplish
- Don't repeat the title

In AEM Guides: Shortdescs appear as tooltips when hovering over links in web output. They are also used in search result snippets.
common_mistakes: Placing <shortdesc> after the body element
Making shortdesc too long (>50 words)
Using multiple paragraphs in shortdesc
Omitting shortdesc entirely
correct_examples:
<concept id="about">
  <title>About Maps</title>
  <shortdesc>DITA maps organize topics into a hierarchy.</shortdesc>
  <conbody>...</conbody>
</concept>
usage_contexts: Brief summary shown in search results and link previews
Must appear between <title> and body element
Keep to 1-2 sentences

text_content:
<prolog> contains topic metadata — author, copyright, audience, keywords. This metadata is NOT displayed in the output but is used for filtering, search, and processing.

Example:
```xml
<prolog>
  <author>Adobe Documentation Team</author>
  <copyright><copyryear year="2024"/><copyrholder>Adobe Systems</copyrholder></copyright>
  <metadata>
    <audience type="administrator" experiencelevel="expert"/>
    <keywords><keyword>AEM Guides</keyword><keyword>installation</keyword></keywords>
  </metadata>
</prolog>
```

In AEM Guides: Prolog metadata is searchable in the repository. Audience and platform metadata enable conditional filtering via DITAVAL profiles.
common_mistakes: Placing prolog after body
Using wrong metadata children
Duplicating prolog in nested topics
correct_examples:
<concept id="ov">
  <title>Overview</title>
  <prolog>
    <author>Jane</author>
    <metadata><keywords><keyword>DITA</keyword></keywords></metadata>
  </prolog>
  <conbody>...</conbody>
</concept>
usage_contexts: Topic metadata: author, keywords, permissions
Placed between shortdesc and body

text_content:
<topicmeta> holds metadata for a topicref or mapref — navtitle overrides, link text, search titles, and keywords.

What is <topicmeta>?
It provides metadata that overrides or supplements what's in the referenced topic. Most commonly used for navtitle (TOC title override) and linktext (link display text).

Example:
```xml
<topicref href="api-reference.dita">
  <topicmeta>
    <navtitle>API Docs</navtitle>
    <linktext>API Reference Guide</linktext>
    <searchtitle>AEM Guides API Documentation</searchtitle>
  </topicmeta>
</topicref>
```

Also used in keydefs for variable text:
```xml
<keydef keys="product-name">
  <topicmeta><linktext>AEM Guides</linktext></topicmeta>
</keydef>
```

In AEM Guides: The navtitle from topicmeta overrides the topic's title in the TOC and breadcrumbs.

<navtitle> overrides a topic's title in the TOC/navigation. Use when the topic title is too long or technical for navigation.

CRITICAL: navtitle has TWO usage contexts with different rules:

1. As an ATTRIBUTE on <topicref>: navtitle="Short Label"
   - REQUIRES locktitle="yes" to take effect!
   - Without locktitle="yes", the DITA processor IGNORES navtitle and uses the topic's <title> instead.
   - Example: <topicref href="install.dita" navtitle="Setup" locktitle="yes"/>
   - WRONG: <topicref href="install.dita" navtitle="Setup"/> (navtitle ignored without locktitle)

2. As an ATTRIBUTE on <topichead>: navtitle="Section Label"
   - DITA 1.3 made locktitle="yes" the DEFAULT for topichead, so no explicit locktitle needed.
   - Example: <topichead navtitle="Getting Started">

3. As an ELEMENT inside <topicmeta>: <navtitle>Short Label</navtitle>
   - Alternative to the attribute form. Same locktitle rules apply for topicref.
   - Example:
```xml
<topicref href="configuring-cross-origin-resource-sharing-policies.dita" locktitle="yes">
  <topicmeta>
    <navtitle>CORS Configuration</navtitle>
  </topicmeta>
</topicref>
```

Common mistake: Setting navtitle on topicref without locktitle="yes" — the navtitle is silently ignored by spec-compliant processors.

When to use navtitle:
- Topic title is too long for TOC ("Configuring Cross-Origin Resource Sharing Policies" → "CORS Setup")
- Topic title is too technical for readers ("ssl-cert-rotation-procedure" → "Rotate SSL Certificates")
- You want different TOC labels for different maps reusing the same topic

In AEM Guides: The navtitle appears in the left-nav, breadcrumbs, and TOC instead of the topic's full <title>. The Web Editor shows navtitle in the Map View when set. locktitle="yes" is required for topicref overrides to render correctly in PDF and Sites output.
attributes: {"locktitle": "yes|no (default: no for topicref, yes for topichead in DITA 1.3)"}

text_content:
<xref> creates a cross-reference link to another topic, element, or external URL.

SYNTAX PATTERNS:
1. Link to topic: <xref href="install.dita">Installation Guide</xref>
2. Link to element in topic: <xref href="install.dita#install_topic/prereqs">prerequisites</xref>
3. Link by key (RECOMMENDED): <xref keyref="install-guide">Installation Guide</xref>
4. External URL: <xref href="https://example.com" scope="external" format="html">Example</xref>
5. Email link: <xref href="mailto:support@example.com" scope="external" format="mailto">Contact Support</xref>

HREF FORMAT BREAKDOWN:
- file.dita — link to topic (processor picks first topic)
- file.dita#topicId — link to specific topic by ID
- file.dita#topicId/elementId — link to specific element within a topic
- #./elementId — link to element in SAME topic (relative)
- https://... — external URL (requires scope='external' format='html')

KEY RULES:
- For external URLs: BOTH scope='external' AND format='html' are required
- For PDF links: format='pdf' scope='external'
- For same-topic links: use href='#./elementId' (dot = current topic)
- Prefer keyref over href for all internal links
- Always provide link text between the tags for accessibility
- The type attribute helps processors generate better link text when none is provided

AEM GUIDES WEB EDITOR:
- Xrefs are clickable in Author view — click to navigate to target
- Broken xrefs show red highlight and appear in Validation panel
- Insert xref via toolbar: Insert > Cross Reference
- Key-based xrefs resolve based on the selected root map
attributes: {"href": "optional \u2014 direct path to target topic/element/URL. Format: file.dita, file.dita#topicId, file.dita#topicId/elementId, or https://...", "keyref": "optional \u2014 indirect reference via key defined in map. Preferred over href for maintainability", "type": "optional \u2014 topic|concept|task|reference|fig|table|section \u2014 hints at target type for processing", "scope": "optional \u2014 local (default, same ditamap), peer (same system), external (outside DITA system, e.g. web URLs)", "format": "optional \u2014 dita (default), html, pdf, mailto \u2014 format of target resource. MUST be 'html' for web URLs", "outputclass": "optional \u2014 custom CSS class for styling the link in output"}
common_mistakes: Missing scope='external' for web URLs — link won't open in browser correctly
Missing format='html' for web URLs — processor treats it as DITA link and fails
Using href='file.dita#elementId' without topicId — WRONG: must be file.dita#topicId/elementId
Using href when keyref is available — keyref is more maintainable across renames
Empty xref with no link text: <xref href='file.dita'/> — always provide text for accessibility
Using scope='external' for internal DITA links — external is only for non-DITA targets
Forgetting format='pdf' when linking to PDF files
Using <xref> for inline images — use <image> instead
correct_examples:
Internal link by key:
<xref keyref="install-guide">Installation Guide</xref>

Internal link by path:
<xref href="admin/install.dita#install_topic">Installation Guide</xref>

Link to specific section:
<xref href="admin/install.dita#install_topic/prereqs">prerequisites section</xref>

External web URL (BOTH scope and format required):
<xref href="https://experienceleague.adobe.com" scope="external" format="html">Experience League</xref>

Email link:
<xref href="mailto:docs-team@adobe.com" scope="external" format="mailto">Email the docs team</xref>

PDF link:
<xref href="release-notes.pdf" scope="external" format="pdf">Release Notes (PDF)</xref>
usage_contexts: Cross-references to other topics within the same DITA documentation set
External web URL links (with scope='external' format='html')
Deep links to specific elements within topics (sections, tables, figures)
Key-based indirect links for maintainability
Email and PDF links with appropriate format attribute

text_content:
locktitle is an attribute on <topicref> and <topichead> that controls whether navtitle overrides the referenced topic's <title> in navigation.

Values:
- locktitle="yes" — USE the navtitle. The processor will display the navtitle in TOC/navigation instead of the topic's <title>.
- locktitle="no" (default for topicref) — IGNORE the navtitle. The processor will pull the title from the referenced topic's <title> element.

Critical behavior:
- For <topicref>: locktitle defaults to "no" in DITA 1.2 and DITA 1.3. You MUST explicitly set locktitle="yes" for navtitle to work.
- For <topichead>: DITA 1.3 changed the default to "yes" (since topichead has no linked topic to pull a title from).

Example — correct usage:
```xml
<!-- navtitle IS used (locktitle=yes) -->
<topicref href="install.dita" navtitle="Quick Setup" locktitle="yes"/>
<!-- TOC shows: Quick Setup -->

<!-- navtitle is IGNORED (locktitle=no, the default) -->
<topicref href="install.dita" navtitle="Quick Setup"/>
<!-- TOC shows: whatever <title> says in install.dita -->
```

This is one of the most common DITA mistakes. Many authors set navtitle on topicref and wonder why the TOC still shows the original topic title.

In AEM Guides: locktitle="yes" is required for navtitle overrides to appear in PDF, Sites, and Web Editor Map View navigation.

references a graphic file. It can appear inline (within text) or as a block (within ). Example: ```xml AEM Guides Architecture Diagram showing the AEM Guides components

Click the icon.

``` Key attributes: - @href — path to the image file (required) - @alt — accessibility text (required) - @placement — inline or break (block) - @scale — percentage scaling In AEM Guides: Images are uploaded to the DAM and referenced by path. The Web Editor shows image previews inline.
common_mistakes: Missing href attribute
Using src instead of href - DITA uses href
Missing <alt> text
Wrong path format
correct_examples:
<image href="images/screenshot.png" placement="break" width="400px">
  <alt>Screenshot of the dialog</alt>
</image>
usage_contexts: Inline or block images
placement="break" for block, "inline" for inline
Use <fig> wrapper for captioned images

text_content:
<fig> is a figure container with an optional title and description. It wraps images, code blocks, or other content that needs a caption.

Example:
```xml
<fig id="arch-diagram">
  <title>System Architecture</title>
  <desc>High-level view of the AEM Guides publishing pipeline.</desc>
  <image href="images/architecture.png" alt="Architecture diagram"/>
</fig>
```

In AEM Guides: Figures are numbered automatically in PDF output. The title appears as a caption below the image.
common_mistakes: Missing <title> for figure captions
Putting <fig> inside inline content
Using <fig> without meaningful content
correct_examples:
<fig id="fig_arch">
  <title>System Architecture</title>
  <image href="images/arch.png">
    <alt>Architecture diagram</alt>
  </image>
</fig>
usage_contexts: Captioned figures with images or codeblocks
Title becomes figure caption in output

text_content:
is a formal CALS table with thead, tbody, rows, and entries. Use for complex tables with headers, spanning, alignment, and column width control. Structure:
→ (optional) → <tgroup cols="N"> → <colspec> (one per column) → <thead> → <tbody> → <row> → <entry>. Example — basic table with column widths and spanning: ```xml <table id="output-formats" dir="ltr"> <title>Supported Output Formats FormatEngineNotes PDFNative PDFCustomizable via PDF templates All formats support conditional processing via @props and @audience.
``` Example — RTL table for Arabic/Hebrew content: ```xml Arabic Terminology المصطلحالتعريفمحتوىأي نوع من المعلومات المكتوبة
``` For simple tables, use instead (simpler markup, no spanning). Key attributes: - @id — unique identifier for conref/reuse - @dir — text direction: ltr (default), rtl, lro, rlo — essential for bidirectional content - @xml:lang — language of table content, inherits from parent if not set - @frame — border rendering: all, top, bottom, topbot, sides, none - @colsep / @rowsep — column/row separator lines (0 or 1) - @pgwide — 1 for page-wide table in PDF output In AEM Guides: The Web Editor provides a visual table editor. You can add/remove rows and columns, merge cells, set alignment, and control column widths. RTL tables render correctly in both PDF and Sites output when @dir is set.
usage_contexts: Complex data tables requiring column width control, spanning, or alignment
RTL/bidirectional content tables with @dir='rtl' and @xml:lang
Specification tables with merged header cells
Comparison tables where simpletable cannot express the required layout
common_mistakes: Forgetting <tgroup cols="N"> — <table> MUST contain <tgroup>, not direct <thead>/<tbody>
Not specifying @cols on <tgroup> — the cols attribute is required and must match the number of <colspec> elements
Using <simpletable> markup inside <table> — <table> uses <row>/<entry>, not <strow>/<stentry>
Omitting @dir on tables with RTL content — causes incorrect column rendering order
Using namest/nameend without matching colspec @colname — spanning references must match defined column names
correct_examples: Header 1Header 2DataValue
שלוםעולם

text_content:
<simpletable> is a lightweight table for simple grids. Easier markup than <table> (no tgroup/colspec).

Structure: <simpletable> → <sthead> (optional, one header row) → <strow> (data rows) → <stentry> (cells).

Example:
```xml
<simpletable dir="ltr">
  <sthead>
    <stentry>Parameter</stentry>
    <stentry>Default</stentry>
    <stentry>Description</stentry>
  </sthead>
  <strow>
    <stentry>output.format</stentry>
    <stentry>pdf</stentry>
    <stentry>Output format for generation</stentry>
  </strow>
  <strow>
    <stentry>toc.depth</stentry>
    <stentry>3</stentry>
    <stentry>Maximum TOC depth level</stentry>
  </strow>
</simpletable>
```

When to use simpletable vs table:
| Feature | <simpletable> | <table> |
|---------|-------------|--------|
| Markup complexity | Simple | Complex (tgroup, colspec) |
| Cell spanning | No | Yes (namest/nameend) |
| Column width control | No | Yes (colspec @colwidth) |
| Multiple header rows | No (1 sthead only) | Yes |
| @dir support | Yes | Yes |
| Best for | Key-value pairs, simple grids | Complex data with spanning |

Key attributes:
- @id — for conref/reuse
- @dir — text direction (ltr, rtl, lro, rlo)
- @xml:lang — language override
- @relcolwidth — relative column widths (e.g., "1* 2* 1*")

In AEM Guides: Both table types are supported. The Web Editor renders simpletables as clean grids. Use @relcolwidth for column proportions.
usage_contexts: Key-value pair tables (parameter/value, term/definition)
Simple comparison grids without spanning needs
Quick reference tables in concept or reference topics
common_mistakes: Using <row>/<entry> inside <simpletable> — simpletable uses <strow>/<stentry>, not <row>/<entry>
Adding <tgroup> inside <simpletable> — simpletable has no tgroup; children are <sthead> and <strow> directly
Trying to span cells in <simpletable> — use <table> if you need cell spanning
correct_examples: <simpletable><sthead><stentry>Key</stentry><stentry>Value</stentry></sthead><strow><stentry>Name</stentry><stentry>AEM Guides</stentry></strow></simpletable>
<simpletable relcolwidth="1* 3*" dir="ltr"><sthead><stentry>Code</stentry><stentry>Description</stentry></sthead><strow><stentry>200</stentry><stentry>Success</stentry></strow></simpletable>

text_content:
<codeblock> contains preformatted code or command-line text. Whitespace and line breaks are preserved.

Example:
```xml
<codeblock outputclass="language-xml">
<topicref href="install.dita"/>
<topicref href="configure.dita"/>
</codeblock>
```

Use @outputclass for syntax highlighting hints (language-xml, language-java, language-json, etc.).

In AEM Guides: Codeblocks are rendered in monospace font with optional syntax highlighting in web output.
common_mistakes: Not escaping XML special chars (< > &)
Missing outputclass for syntax highlighting
Using <pre> when <codeblock> is more semantic
correct_examples:
<codeblock outputclass="language-xml"><topic id="example">
  <title>Example</title>
</topic></codeblock>
usage_contexts: Code samples and configuration snippets
Use outputclass for syntax highlighting hints
Must escape XML chars

text_content:
is a callout element for tips, warnings, cautions, and important information. The @type attribute controls the label and styling. Available types: - note (default) — general information - tip — helpful suggestion - important — critical information - caution — potential data loss or errors - warning — safety risk - danger — immediate safety hazard - remember — reminder - restriction — limitation - trouble — troubleshooting Example: ```xml

Use keyrefs instead of direct hrefs for easier maintenance.

Deleting a map also removes all output presets. Back up presets before deleting.

This operation cannot be undone. Ensure you have a backup.

 ``` In AEM Guides: Notes are rendered with colored icons and labels in all output formats. The Web Editor shows note types with distinct icons.

The @chunk attribute on <topicref> controls how topics are split or merged in output. It is defined in the OASIS DITA 1.3 specification and applies to ALL DITA output types including AEM Sites, HTML5, and PDF.

IMPORTANT: 'to-navigation' is NOT a valid chunk value. The only valid DITA 1.3 chunk values are listed below.

Valid values (combinable with space separator):
- to-content — merge all child topics referenced under this topicref into ONE output document/page. The referenced topic becomes the container.
- by-topic — split: each nested <topic> element becomes its own separate output document/page, even if they share a source file.
- by-document — each source DITA file produces exactly one output document (default DITA-OT behavior).
- select-topic — include ONLY the single topic referenced by href, ignoring any nested child topics in the same file.
- select-branch — include the referenced topic AND all its nested child topics from the same file.
- select-document — include ALL topics from the referenced source file, regardless of nesting.

Common combinations:
- chunk="to-content" — merge children into parent page (most common)
- chunk="by-topic" — force one page per topic
- chunk="select-branch to-content" — select a branch and merge it

Behavior by output type:

1. AEM Sites output:
   - to-content: child topicrefs are merged into a single AEM page. The parent topicref's topic becomes the page, with children rendered as sections within it.
   - by-topic: each topic gets its own AEM page, even if they were grouped under one topicref.
   - Default (no chunk): each topicref with an href creates one AEM page.

2. HTML5 output:
   - to-content: produces fewer, larger HTML files by combining topics.
   - by-topic: produces more, smaller HTML files (one per topic element).
   - by-document: one HTML file per source DITA file (default).

3. Native PDF output:
   - Chunking has limited effect on PDF because PDF is inherently a single continuous document.
   - to-content may affect how topics are grouped into chapters/sections.
   - Page breaks are primarily controlled by CSS page-break-before/after, not chunking.

4. DITA-OT processing:
   - Chunk is processed during the map-reader preprocessing stage.
   - It physically rewrites the temporary topic files before transformation.
   - select-* values filter which topics from a multi-topic file are included.

Example:
```xml
<!-- Merge all child topics into one page -->
<topicref href="overview.dita" chunk="to-content">
  <topicref href="section-a.dita"/>
  <topicref href="section-b.dita"/>
</topicref>

<!-- Force each topic to its own page -->
<topicref href="guide.dita" chunk="by-topic">
  <topicref href="chapter1.dita"/>
  <topicref href="chapter2.dita"/>
</topicref>

<!-- Select only one topic from a multi-topic file -->
<topicref href="multi-topics.dita#specific-topic" chunk="select-topic"/>
```
attributes: {"to-content": "merge children into one output", "by-topic": "split into one output per topic", "by-document": "one output per source file", "select-topic": "include only referenced topic", "select-branch": "include topic and descendants", "select-document": "include all topics from file"}

text_content:
The @collection-type attribute on <topicref> defines how child topics relate to each other and generates automatic links.

Values:
- unordered (default) — parent-to-child links, no sibling links
- sequence — adds previous/next navigation between siblings (for tutorials, procedures)
- family — all siblings link to each other (for closely related topics)
- choice — one-of selection (rarely used)

Example:
```xml
<!-- Sequential tutorial with prev/next links -->
<topicref href="tutorial.dita" collection-type="sequence">
  <topicref href="tutorial-01-intro.dita"/>
  <topicref href="tutorial-02-setup.dita"/>
  <topicref href="tutorial-03-build.dita"/>
  <topicref href="tutorial-04-deploy.dita"/>
</topicref>
```

In AEM Guides: collection-type=sequence generates 'Previous' and 'Next' links in AEM Sites output. PDF output may show sequential navigation in footers.

<relrow> is a row in a relationship table. Topics in the same row are linked to each other in the output.

Example:
```xml
<relrow>
  <relcell><topicref href="what-is-conref.dita"/></relcell>
  <relcell><topicref href="create-conref.dita"/></relcell>
  <relcell><topicref href="conref-reference.dita"/></relcell>
</relrow>
```

Rule: Topics in the SAME cell are NOT linked to each other. Only topics in DIFFERENT cells of the same row get linked.

<relcell> is a cell in a relationship table row. It contains topicrefs. Topics in different cells of the same row are linked.

Example:
```xml
<relrow>
  <relcell><!-- Concepts -->
    <topicref href="content-reuse-overview.dita"/>
  </relcell>
  <relcell><!-- Tasks -->
    <topicref href="create-conref.dita"/>
    <topicref href="create-keyref.dita"/>
  </relcell>
</relrow>
```

<relcolspec> defines a column in a relationship table header. Sets the default type and linking behavior for all cells in that column.

Example:
```xml
<relheader>
  <relcolspec type="concept"/>
  <relcolspec type="task"/>
  <relcolspec type="reference" linking="targetonly"/>
</relheader>
```

@linking values:
- normal — bidirectional links (default)
- sourceonly — links only FROM this column's topics
- targetonly — links only TO this column's topics (useful for reference material)

<subjectScheme> defines controlled vocabularies and taxonomies for conditional processing. It lets you restrict what values authors can use for @audience, @platform, @product, and @otherprops.

Example:
```xml
<subjectScheme>
  <subjectdef keys="audiences">
    <subjectdef keys="admin"/>
    <subjectdef keys="developer"/>
    <subjectdef keys="author"/>
  </subjectdef>
  <enumerationdef>
    <attributedef name="audience"/>
    <subjectdef keyref="audiences"/>
  </enumerationdef>
</subjectScheme>
```

In AEM Guides: Subject schemes enable dropdown selection for conditional attributes in the Web Editor, preventing typos and ensuring consistency.

<subjectdef> defines an individual subject or controlled value in a subject scheme. Can nest for hierarchical taxonomies.

Example:
```xml
<subjectdef keys="platforms">

  <subjectdef keys="macos"/>
  <subjectdef keys="linux">

</subjectdef>
```

is a metadata element in for conditional processing. It identifies the intended reader. Example: ```xml ``` As a conditional attribute on content: ```xml

Access the Admin Console to manage users.

Open the Web Editor to start writing.

``` Filtered via DITAVAL to produce audience-specific output.

@conrefend pairs with @conref to reference a RANGE of consecutive sibling elements. The conref points to the start element, conrefend points to the end element. All elements between start and end (inclusive) are pulled in.

KEY RULES:
1. Start and end elements MUST be siblings (same parent element)
2. Start element MUST come before end element in document order
3. The referencing element must match the type of the START element
4. Both conref and conrefend must point to the SAME file and topic
5. The referencing element needs valid placeholder content (e.g. <cmd/> in <step>)

SYNTAX:
conref='file.dita#topicId/startId' conrefend='file.dita#topicId/endId'

EXAMPLE — Reuse steps 2 through 5:
```xml
<!-- Source file: shared-steps.dita -->
<task id="common-steps">
  <taskbody>
    <steps>
      <step id="step1"><cmd>Open the application.</cmd></step>
      <step id="step2"><cmd>Navigate to Settings.</cmd></step>
      <step id="step3"><cmd>Click Advanced.</cmd></step>
      <step id="step4"><cmd>Enable the feature.</cmd></step>
      <step id="step5"><cmd>Click Save.</cmd></step>
    </steps>
  </taskbody>
</task>

<!-- Reusing file: my-task.dita -->
<task id="my-task">
  <taskbody>
    <steps>
      <step><cmd>Log in to the system.</cmd></step>
      <!-- Pull in steps 2-5 as a range -->
      <step conref="shared-steps.dita#common-steps/step2"
            conrefend="shared-steps.dita#common-steps/step5">
        <cmd/><!-- placeholder required -->
      </step>
      <step><cmd>Verify the changes.</cmd></step>
    </steps>
  </taskbody>
</task>
```

RESULT: my-task.dita gets steps: Login, Navigate to Settings, Click Advanced, Enable the feature, Click Save, Verify.

AEM GUIDES: Conref ranges resolve in the Web Editor when a root map is selected. The range is displayed as a single conref block with a blue border.
attributes: {"conref": "required \u2014 path to FIRST element in range. Format: file.dita#topicId/startElementId", "conrefend": "required \u2014 path to LAST element in range. Format: file.dita#topicId/endElementId"}
common_mistakes: Start and end elements are NOT siblings — they must share the same parent
conrefend points to a different file than conref — both must be in the same file#topic
End element comes BEFORE start element in document order — start must precede end
Missing placeholder content in the referencing element — e.g. <step> needs <cmd/> even with conref
Using conrefend without conref — conrefend is meaningless alone, must pair with conref
Trying to range across different parent elements — e.g. steps in different <steps> blocks
Using conrefend to pull non-contiguous elements — only contiguous siblings are supported
