text_content:
Comparison: <topicref> vs <topichead> vs <topicgroup> — the three main elements for organizing topics in a DITA map.

| Element | Points to a file? | Visible in TOC? | Has a title? | Purpose |
|---------|-------------------|-----------------|-------------|----------|
| <topicref> | Yes (@href) | Yes | Yes (from topic or @navtitle) | Include a topic in the build |
| <topichead> | No | Yes | Yes (@navtitle) | Visible section heading only |
| <topicgroup> | No | No | No | Silent/invisible grouping |

When to use each:
- <topicref> — Every topic you want published needs one. It's the workhorse.
- <topichead> — Use for chapter/section headings when there's no actual topic file for the heading.
- <topicgroup> — Use to silently group topics for shared metadata or conditional processing without affecting navigation.

Example showing all three:
```xml
<map>
  <title>AEM Guides Admin Guide</title>

  <!-- topichead: Visible heading, no content page -->
  <topichead navtitle="Installation">
    <!-- topicref: Actual topic files -->
    <topicref href="prerequisites.dita"/>
    <topicref href="install-steps.dita"/>
  </topichead>

  <!-- topicgroup: Invisible grouping -->
  <topicgroup>
    <topicref href="config-general.dita"/>
    <topicref href="config-advanced.dita"/>
  </topicgroup>
</map>
```

Output TOC:
  Installation              ← from topichead (heading only)
    ├── Prerequisites       ← from topicref (content page)
    └── Install Steps       ← from topicref (content page)
  General Configuration     ← from topicref in topicgroup (no heading above it)
  Advanced Configuration    ← from topicref in topicgroup (no heading above it)

Comparison: conref vs keyref vs conkeyref — DITA's three main content reuse mechanisms. | Mechanism | Type | Syntax | Resolves via | Best for | |-----------|------|--------|-------------|----------| | conref | Direct content reuse | conref="file.dita#topic/elem" | File path + element ID | Simple, single-source reuse | | keyref | Indirect reference | keyref="keyname" | Map keydef | Text variables, link targets | | conkeyref | Indirect content reuse | conkeyref="keyname/elemId" | Map keydef + element ID | Multi-product reuse, swappable sources | When to use each: - conref: You know the exact source file and it won't change across products/versions. - keyref: You need a text variable (product name, version) or indirect link target. - conkeyref: You want conref's content reuse power but with keyref's indirection (different maps can point the key to different source files). Example: ```xml

Welcome to .

``` Progression: Start with conref for simplicity. Move to conkeyref when you need to swap sources across different maps or product variants.
usage_contexts: Choosing the right reuse mechanism for the situation
common_mistakes: Using conref when conkeyref is available — always prefer key-based references
Using keyref expecting it to copy content — keyref creates links, conkeyref copies content
Writing <conref> or <conkeyref> or <keyref> as elements — ALL THREE are ATTRIBUTES, not elements
correct_examples:
<!-- conref: direct content copy (by path) -->
<note conref="shared/warnings.dita#shared-warnings/backup-warning"/>

<!-- conkeyref: indirect content copy (by key — preferred) -->
<note conkeyref="shared-warnings/backup-warning">placeholder</note>

<!-- keyref: link or text variable (NOT content copy) -->
<keyword keyref="product-name"/>
<xref keyref="install-topic"/>

text_content:
Comparison: <map> vs <bookmap> — two ways to organize DITA content.

| Feature | <map> | <bookmap> |
|---------|-------|-----------|
| Structure | Flexible, flat or nested topicrefs | Book structure: chapters, parts, appendices |
| Metadata | Basic <topicmeta> | Rich <bookmeta> (ISBN, publisher, etc.) |
| Frontmatter/backmatter | No | Yes (preface, glossary, index) |
| Chapter numbering | Manual | Automatic |
| Best for | Web help, AEM Sites, flexible output | PDF books, printed manuals, formal documents |

Use <map> when:
- Publishing to AEM Sites or HTML5
- You need a flexible, lightweight structure
- Your content doesn't follow a traditional book format

Use <bookmap> when:
- Generating formal PDF documents
- You need chapters, appendices, and front/back matter
- You want automatic chapter numbering and book-level metadata

In AEM Guides: Both are fully supported. The Web Editor offers templates for both. PDF output from bookmaps includes title pages and chapter separators automatically.

Comparison: <concept> vs <task> vs <reference> — DITA's three core topic types.

The 'information typing' principle: classify every topic by its purpose.

| Type | Purpose | Body element | Answers... | Example topics |
|------|---------|-------------|------------|----------------|
| <concept> | Explain what/why | <conbody> | 'What is X?' | What is content reuse?, Understanding keyscopes |
| <task> | Step-by-step how-to | <taskbody> | 'How do I X?' | Creating a conref, Publishing to PDF |
| <reference> | Lookup data | <refbody> | 'What are the details of X?' | API parameters, Configuration options |

Best practices:
- Each topic should be ONE type — don't mix procedures into concept topics
- Use concept to provide background BEFORE a task
- Use reference for information users look up (not read linearly)
- Link related concept-task-reference topics via reltables

In AEM Guides: The Web Editor offers templates for all three types. The topic type determines which elements are available in the editor.

Comparison: <mapref> vs <navref> — two ways to include submaps.

| Feature | <mapref> | <navref> |
|---------|---------|--------|
| Imports navigation? | Yes | Yes |
| Imports key definitions? | Yes | No |
| Imports relationship tables? | Yes | No |
| Use when... | Submaps share key scope | Submaps should be key-isolated |

Example:
```xml
<map>
  <!-- mapref: imports everything from submap -->
  <mapref href="shared/common-keys.ditamap"/>

  <!-- navref: imports only navigation, not keys -->
  <navref mapref="partner-guide.ditamap"/>
</map>
```

Use navref when combining independently-authored guides to prevent key collisions.

Comparison: <table> vs <simpletable> — when to use each table type.

| Feature | <simpletable> | <table> |
|---------|-------------|--------|
| Markup complexity | Simple (sthead/strow/stentry) | Complex (tgroup/colspec/thead/tbody) |
| Column width control | No | Yes (colspec @colwidth) |
| Cell spanning | No | Yes (@namest/@nameend, @morerows) |
| Title | No | Yes |
| Best for | Key-value pairs, simple grids | Complex data with spanning, alignment |

Rule of thumb: Use <simpletable> by default. Switch to <table> only when you need column widths, cell spanning, or formal table titles.

Topic hierarchy best practice:
- Aim for ~7 child topics per parent (Miller's Law for cognitive load)
- Maximum 4 nesting levels deep
- Use a single root topic from which all others branch
- Prefer stub topics with titles over empty headings
- Use collection-type=sequence for procedural content (tutorials) to get prev/next links
- Use collection-type=family for closely related siblings that should all link to each other

Example of good hierarchy:
```
Admin Guide (root)
├── Installation (topichead, 3 children)
│   ├── Prerequisites
│   ├── Install Steps
│   └── Verify Installation
├── Configuration (topicref, 4 children)
│   ├── General Settings
│   ├── Advanced Settings
│   ├── Security
│   └── Performance
└── Troubleshooting (topichead, 3 children)
    ├── Common Errors
    ├── Log Analysis
    └── Support Contact
```

Reltable best practice:
- Prefer map-level reltables over topic-level <related-links> (keeps topics independent)
- Use descriptive @title for author reference (not visible in output)
- Common pattern: 3-column table for concept → task → reference linking
- Topics in the same CELL are NOT linked; topics in different CELLS of the same ROW are linked
- Use linking='targetonly' on reference columns to prevent reference topics from linking back to concepts/tasks
- Place reltables at the END of the map, after all topicrefs

Example:
```xml
<reltable title="Content Reuse Relationships">
  <relheader>
    <relcolspec type="concept"/>
    <relcolspec type="task"/>
    <relcolspec type="reference" linking="targetonly"/>
  </relheader>
  <relrow>
    <relcell><topicref href="what-is-conref.dita"/></relcell>
    <relcell><topicref href="create-conref.dita"/></relcell>
    <relcell><topicref href="conref-syntax-reference.dita"/></relcell>
  </relrow>
</reltable>
```

Map structure best practice:
- One top-level map from which all content branches
- Use topichead for section headings without content
- Use topicgroup for silent grouping (shared metadata, conditional processing)
- Use keydefs at the top of the map for variables and indirect references
- Use maprefs for modular documentation (separate submaps per chapter)
- Place reltables at the end of the map

Recommended map skeleton:
```xml
<map id="product-guide">
  <title>Product Guide</title>

  <!-- 1. Key definitions first -->
  <keydef keys="product-name"><topicmeta><linktext>AEM Guides</linktext></topicmeta></keydef>
  <keydef keys="product-version"><topicmeta><linktext>4.6</linktext></topicmeta></keydef>

  <!-- 2. Content structure -->
  <topichead navtitle="Getting Started">
    <topicref href="overview.dita"/>
    <topicref href="quick-start.dita"/>
  </topichead>

  <mapref href="submaps/installation.ditamap"/>
  <mapref href="submaps/configuration.ditamap"/>

  <!-- 3. Reltables last -->
  <reltable title="Cross-references">
    <relrow>
      <relcell><topicref href="overview.dita"/></relcell>
      <relcell><topicref href="quick-start.dita"/></relcell>
    </relrow>
  </reltable>
</map>
```

Content reuse strategy best practice: 1. Start simple — use conref for direct reuse within a single product 2. Scale up — move to conkeyref when you need the same content across multiple products/versions 3. Use keyref for text variables (product names, version numbers, URLs) 4. Create a shared 'warehouse' topic for reusable fragments (warnings, legal text, common steps) Reuse decision tree: - Need to reuse a paragraph/note/step? → conref or conkeyref - Need a text variable (product name)? → keyref + keydef - Need to swap link targets across maps? → keyref - Need to reuse content but change source per product? → conkeyref - Need to reuse an entire topic? → topicref (reference it from multiple maps) Common reuse patterns: ```xml

Back up before proceeding.

Contact support at helpx.adobe.com.

AEM Guides is Adobe's enterprise DITA CMS.

 ```

Self-reference: a topic can reference itself via keyref or topicref. keydef in map points to the same topic. Used for testing outgoing links panel, forward reference display. AEM Guides right panel shows self-reference as outgoing link.

Same-file xref/conref: use href="#topicId/elementId" for xref to section, li, fig, table within same topic. Use conref="#topicId/elementId" for same-file content reuse. conrefend pairs with conref for element ranges. Source and target must be different elements; avoid self-loop.

<choicetable> is a 2-column table used INSIDE a <step> element in DITA task topics to present a set of options/choices with descriptions.

What is <choicetable>?
When a task step offers multiple choices (e.g., 'Select one of the following options...'), a <choicetable> presents them as a structured two-column table with Option and Description columns. The DITA processor renders it with automatic column headers.

IMPORTANT: <choicetable> does NOT use CALS table syntax. It does NOT use <tgroup>, <tbody>, <row>, or <entry>. It has its own element set:

Structure:
choicetable
  chhead (optional — custom column headers)
    choptionhd — header for option column (default: 'Option')
    chdeschd — header for description column (default: 'Description')
  chrow (one per option, required)
    choption — the option/choice label
    chdesc — description of what this option does

Example:
```xml
<task id="configure-output">
  <title>Configure Output Preset</title>
  <taskbody>
    <steps>
      <step>
        <cmd>Select an output format for your DITA map.</cmd>
        <choicetable>
          <chhead>
            <choptionhd>Format</choptionhd>
            <chdeschd>Details</chdeschd>
          </chhead>
          <chrow>
            <choption>AEM Sites</choption>
            <chdesc>Publishes to AEM as web pages with search and navigation.</chdesc>
          </chrow>
          <chrow>
            <choption>PDF (Native)</choption>
            <chdesc>Generates print-ready PDF. Best for downloadable guides.</chdesc>
          </chrow>
          <chrow>
            <choption>Custom HTML5</choption>
            <chdesc>Standalone responsive HTML5 for self-hosted portals.</chdesc>
          </chrow>
        </choicetable>
      </step>
    </steps>
  </taskbody>
</task>
```

Comparison: choicetable vs choices vs simpletable
| Element | Format | Where? | Descriptions? | Use when... |
|---------|--------|--------|---------------|-------------|
| <choicetable> | 2-column table | Inside <step> only | Yes (option + description) | Options need explanations |
| <choices> | Bullet list | Inside <step> only | No (labels only) | Simple list of options |
| <simpletable> | Flexible table | Anywhere in body | Yes (flexible) | Need more than 2 columns |

Common mistakes:
- Using <tgroup>/<row>/<entry> inside <choicetable> — WRONG. Use <chrow>/<choption>/<chdesc>.
- Placing <choicetable> outside a <step> — WRONG. It's step-only.
- Using <choices> when options need descriptions — use <choicetable> instead.

In AEM Guides:
- Web Editor: choicetable appears in the Elements panel when cursor is inside a <step>. Insert via Elements > choicetable.
- PDF Output: Renders as a styled 2-column table within the step, with header row if <chhead> is present. Custom PDF templates can style .choicetable class.
- Sites Output: Renders as an HTML table with class='choicetable' — inherits AEM component styling.
- Review mode: Reviewers can comment on individual <chrow> entries for granular feedback.
common_mistakes: Using CALS syntax (tgroup, row, entry) - choicetable uses chrow/choption/chdesc
Placing choicetable outside <step>
Adding cols attribute - choicetable is always 2 columns
Using <choices> when options need descriptions
correct_examples:
<step>
  <cmd>Select format:</cmd>
  <choicetable>
    <chhead>
      <choptionhd>Format</choptionhd>
      <chdeschd>Description</chdeschd>
    </chhead>
    <chrow>
      <choption>PDF</choption>
      <chdesc>PDF document</chdesc>
    </chrow>
    <chrow>
      <choption>HTML5</choption>
      <chdesc>Responsive HTML</chdesc>
    </chrow>
  </choicetable>
</step>
usage_contexts: Choices with descriptions inside a task step
Always 2 columns: option + description
Only valid inside <step>

text_content:
<choices> is a simple bullet list of options used INSIDE a <step> element in DITA task topics.

What is <choices>?
When a step presents options that DON'T need descriptions, use <choices> instead of <choicetable>. It renders as a simple unordered list.

Structure:
choices
  choice (one per option)

Example:
```xml
<step>
  <cmd>Select a publishing format:</cmd>
  <choices>
    <choice>PDF</choice>
    <choice>HTML5</choice>
    <choice>AEM Sites</choice>
    <choice>JSON</choice>
  </choices>
</step>
```

When to use <choices> vs <choicetable>:
| Scenario | Use |
|----------|-----|
| Options are self-explanatory (PDF, HTML5, etc.) | <choices> |
| Options need descriptions/explanations | <choicetable> |
| Need more than 2 columns | <simpletable> inside <info> |

In AEM Guides: Rendered as a bulleted list within the step. Available in Elements panel when cursor is inside <step>.

<substeps> is a nested set of steps INSIDE a <step> element. Use when a step needs sub-procedures.

Structure:
substeps
  substep (one per sub-step)
    cmd (required — the instruction)
    info (optional)
    stepresult (optional)

Example:
```xml
<step>
  <cmd>Configure the output preset:</cmd>
  <substeps>
    <substep><cmd>Click <uicontrol>Settings</uicontrol>.</cmd></substep>
    <substep><cmd>Select the PDF template.</cmd></substep>
    <substep><cmd>Set paper size to A4.</cmd></substep>
  </substeps>
</step>
```

Key rules:
- Only ONE level of substeps allowed (no nested substeps).
- Each <substep> requires exactly one <cmd>.
- Rendered as lettered sub-steps (a, b, c) in most processors.

In AEM Guides: Substeps render as lettered items (a, b, c) indented under the parent step in both PDF and Sites output.

<glossterm> contains the glossary term text inside a <glossentry>. It's the word or phrase being defined.

Example:
```xml
<glossentry id="gl-conref">
  <glossterm>conref</glossterm>
  <glossdef id="def-conref">A DITA mechanism for reusing content by referencing the ID of a source element.</glossdef>
</glossentry>
```

Key rules:
- Required as first child of <glossentry>
- Used by keyref resolution: when you write <term keyref="gl-conref"/>, the processor pulls the <glossterm> text as the link label
- Keep terms concise — the full explanation goes in <glossdef>

In AEM Guides: glossterm text appears as the term label in glossary output and is used for automatic term linking when keyref-based glossary linking is enabled.

contains the definition of a glossary term inside a . Give it an @id to enable conkeyref-based content reuse. Example: ```xml DITA Darwin Information Typing Architecture — an XML-based standard for structured authoring and content reuse.

placeholder

``` Key rules: - Optional but strongly recommended in every glossentry - Give it @id='def-TERMNAME' for conkeyref reuse - Can contain block content:

,

    , , etc. In AEM Guides: glossdef content appears in glossary output. When publishing, AEM Guides resolves conkeyref references to glossdef elements during output generation.

is a topic specialization for diagnosing and resolving problems. It uses a structured symptom → cause → remedy pattern. Structure: troubleshooting title shortdesc troublebody condition — the symptom or error the user observes troubleSolution (one or more) cause — why the problem occurs remedy — how to fix it (contains or ) Example: ```xml PDF Output Generation Fails Troubleshoot PDF generation failures in AEM Guides.

When you click Generate for a PDF preset, the output fails with 'XSLT transformation error'.

The PDF template contains an invalid XSL-FO construct.

 Open the PDF template in the Template Editor. Check the XSL files for syntax errors. Fix the error and regenerate.

A topic referenced in the map contains invalid DITA markup.

 Run validation on the DITA map. Fix any validation errors flagged in the Output log. ``` When to use: - Error messages the user might encounter - Known issues with documented workarounds - FAQ-style 'Why does X happen?' questions In AEM Guides: - Web Editor: Troubleshooting template available when creating new topics. - PDF Output: Rendered with symptom/cause/remedy structure clearly labeled. - Sites Output: Each troubleSolution appears as an expandable section.

Comparison: <choicetable> vs <choices> vs <substeps> — three elements that go inside <step>

| Element | Format | Purpose | Has descriptions? |
|---------|--------|---------|-------------------|
| <choicetable> | 2-column table | Present options with explanations | Yes (choption + chdesc) |
| <choices> | Bullet list | Present simple options | No (just labels) |
| <substeps> | Numbered sub-list (a,b,c) | Break a step into sub-steps | N/A (sub-procedures) |

All three go INSIDE <step> only. They are siblings to <cmd>, <info>, <stepresult>.

Use <choicetable> when: the user must choose AND each option needs explanation.
Use <choices> when: the user must choose AND options are self-explanatory.
Use <substeps> when: the step has multiple sub-actions (not choices).

Common mistake: Using <simpletable> inside <info> instead of <choicetable> — loses the semantic 'choice' meaning, making it harder for processors to generate accessible choice tables.

is an inline element used to mark a word or phrase as a glossary term. What is ? The element identifies a word that has a specific definition in your glossary. When combined with a @keyref attribute, it links the term to a topic, allowing the DITA processor to resolve the display text automatically from the glossary definition. Structure: can contain text content or be self-closing when used with @keyref (the processor fills in the text). Example: ```xml Darwin Information Typing Architecture An XML-based architecture for authoring, producing, and delivering topic-oriented information. Darwin Information Typing Architecture (DITA) DITA

The DITA standard provides a structured authoring framework.

``` How processor resolves term text: 1. If has text content AND @keyref — the text is used as fallback; processor may replace it with glossterm text. 2. If has @keyref only (empty element) — processor pulls text from the in the referenced glossentry. 3. If has no @keyref — it simply marks the word semantically as a term (italic in many outputs). Comparison with related elements: | Element | Purpose | Links to glossary? | Semantic meaning | |---------|---------|--------------------|-----------------| | | Glossary term | Yes (with @keyref) | 'This word is defined in the glossary' | | | Key word/token | Yes (with @keyref) | 'This is a significant word or product name' | | | Generic phrase | Yes (with @keyref) | No specific semantics | Common mistakes: - Using instead of for glossary terms — is for product names and tokens, not glossary-linked terms. - Forgetting to define the key in the map — the @keyref will not resolve and fallback text (or nothing) will appear. In AEM Guides: - Web Editor: with @keyref shows as a hyperlink in Author mode. You can Ctrl+click to navigate to the glossentry. - PDF Output: Terms can be rendered as italic or hyperlinked to a glossary section depending on the PDF template configuration. - Sites Output: Terms render as links to the glossary page if the glossentry is included in the published map.

is an inline element that references a glossary entry and displays a shorter version of the term. What is ? It works with glossentry topics to automatically display the full form of a term on first occurrence and the abbreviated form on subsequent occurrences within the same topic or output. This is the DITA mechanism for handling acronyms correctly — write once, let the processor manage first-use expansion. Structure: is ALWAYS an empty element with a @keyref attribute. It never contains text. Example: ```xml Darwin Information Typing Architecture An XML-based architecture for topic-oriented content. Darwin Information Typing Architecture (DITA) DITA

The standard is widely used.

Many organizations adopt for structured content.

``` How the processor resolves text: 1. First occurrence in a deliverable: uses (e.g., 'Darwin Information Typing Architecture (DITA)'). 2. Subsequent occurrences: uses (e.g., 'DITA'). 3. If no : falls back to . 4. If no : uses for all occurrences. Common mistakes: - Putting text inside — it must be empty; the processor fills in the text. - Missing — without it, the first-use expansion won't include the abbreviation in parentheses. - Using instead — does NOT do first-use/subsequent-use toggling. In AEM Guides: - Web Editor: Shows as a placeholder in Author mode with the resolved term text. - PDF Output (Native): Handles first-use/subsequent-use correctly when the glossentry has glossSurfaceForm and glossAbbreviation. - Sites Output: Rendered as text with potential tooltip showing the full glossterm.

<glossBody> is the extended body of a <glossentry> topic, containing detailed glossary information beyond the basic term and definition.

What is <glossBody>?
While <glossterm> and <glossdef> provide the basic name and definition, <glossBody> holds the rich metadata that powers features like <abbreviated-form> and <term> resolution. It's where you define surface forms, abbreviations, alternate terms, usage notes, and scope.

Structure (all children are optional):
- <glossSurfaceForm> — the full expansion shown on first use (e.g., 'Darwin Information Typing Architecture (DITA)')
- <glossAbbreviation> — the short form (e.g., 'DITA') — used by <abbreviated-form> after first occurrence
- <glossAlt> — container for alternate terms, abbreviations, acronyms, and synonyms
- <glossUsage> — notes on how to use the term correctly
- <glossScopeNote> — describes the scope or context where this definition applies
- <glossSymbol> — an image representing the term
- <glossProperty> — additional properties of the term

Full example:
```xml
<glossentry id="gl-xml">
  <glossterm>Extensible Markup Language</glossterm>
  <glossdef>A markup language that defines rules for encoding documents in a format that is both human-readable and machine-readable.</glossdef>
  <glossBody>
    <glossSurfaceForm>Extensible Markup Language (XML)</glossSurfaceForm>
    <glossUsage>Use 'XML' after first mention. Always capitalize all three letters.</glossUsage>
    <glossScopeNote>In DITA context, XML refers specifically to XML 1.0 Fifth Edition.</glossScopeNote>
    <glossAlt>
      <glossAbbreviation>XML</glossAbbreviation>
    </glossAlt>
    <glossAlt>
      <glossSynonym>xml</glossSynonym>
    </glossAlt>
  </glossBody>
</glossentry>
```

Key relationships:
- <glossSurfaceForm> is used by <abbreviated-form> for first-occurrence text.
- <glossAbbreviation> (inside <glossAlt>) is used by <abbreviated-form> for subsequent occurrences.
- <glossSynonym> (inside <glossAlt>) provides alternative lookup terms.

Common mistakes:
- Placing <glossAbbreviation> directly inside <glossBody> — it must be inside <glossAlt>.
- Confusing <glossSurfaceForm> with <glossterm> — the surface form typically includes the abbreviation in parentheses.

In AEM Guides:
- Web Editor: The glossentry template includes glossBody structure. Fill in glossSurfaceForm and glossAbbreviation for proper acronym handling.
- PDF Output: glossUsage and glossScopeNote can be included in glossary appendix sections.
- Sites Output: Glossary terms are rendered as definition lists with full metadata.

<sthead> is the header row element for a <simpletable>.

What is <sthead>?
It defines the single header row of a simpletable. It contains <stentry> elements that label each column. A simpletable can have at most ONE <sthead>.

Structure:
```xml
<simpletable>
  <sthead>              <!-- 0 or 1: optional header row -->
    <stentry>Col 1</stentry>
    <stentry>Col 2</stentry>
  </sthead>
  <strow>               <!-- 1 or more: data rows -->
    <stentry>Data</stentry>
    <stentry>Data</stentry>
  </strow>
</simpletable>
```

Full example:
```xml
<simpletable>
  <sthead>
    <stentry>Setting</stentry>
    <stentry>Default Value</stentry>
    <stentry>Description</stentry>
  </sthead>
  <strow>
    <stentry>timeout</stentry>
    <stentry>30s</stentry>
    <stentry>Maximum wait time for connection</stentry>
  </strow>
  <strow>
    <stentry>retries</stentry>
    <stentry>3</stentry>
    <stentry>Number of retry attempts</stentry>
  </strow>
</simpletable>
```

IMPORTANT — simpletable vs CALS table children:
| simpletable | CALS table |
|-------------|------------|
| <sthead> | <thead> |
| <strow> | <row> |
| <stentry> | <entry> |

These are NOT interchangeable. Using <row> inside <simpletable> is invalid. Using <strow> inside <table> is invalid.

Common mistakes:
- Using <thead>/<row>/<entry> in a simpletable — those belong to CALS <table> only.
- Adding multiple <sthead> elements — only one is allowed.

In AEM Guides:
- Web Editor: Simpletable header row is visually distinguished with bold/shaded styling in Author mode.
- PDF Output: Header row is styled according to the PDF template's table header settings.
- Sites Output: Renders as <thead> in HTML output.

<strow> is a data row in a <simpletable>.

What is <strow>?
It represents one row of data in a simpletable. Each <strow> contains one or more <stentry> elements. A simpletable must have at least one <strow>.

Example:
```xml
<simpletable>
  <sthead>
    <stentry>Parameter</stentry>
    <stentry>Type</stentry>
  </sthead>
  <strow>
    <stentry>name</stentry>
    <stentry>String</stentry>
  </strow>
  <strow>
    <stentry>count</stentry>
    <stentry>Integer</stentry>
  </strow>
</simpletable>
```

IMPORTANT: <strow> is for <simpletable> ONLY. For CALS <table>, use <row>. For <choicetable>, use <chrow>. These are NOT interchangeable.

In AEM Guides:
- Web Editor: Each strow appears as a table row. Use Tab to navigate between entries. Right-click to insert/delete rows.
- PDF/Sites Output: Rendered as standard table rows.

is a single cell in a row (either or ). What is ? It holds the content of one table cell in a simpletable. It can contain inline elements, paragraphs, lists, and other block content. Example: ```xml output.format

Specifies the output format.

  • html5 — HTML5 output
 ``` IMPORTANT: is for ONLY. For CALS , use . These are different elements and NOT interchangeable. In AEM Guides: - Web Editor: Click into an stentry to edit. Supports rich content including images and code blocks. - PDF/Sites Output: Rendered as
(or in sthead) in the output.

<chhead> is the header row of a <choicetable>, containing <choptionhd> and <chdeschd>.

What is <chhead>?
It defines the column headers for a choicetable. A choicetable has exactly two columns: one for the option and one for the description. The <chhead> labels these columns.

Structure:
```xml
<choicetable>
  <chhead>
    <choptionhd>Option</choptionhd>
    <chdeschd>Description</chdeschd>
  </chhead>
  <chrow>
    <choption>PDF</choption>
    <chdesc>Generate PDF output</chdesc>
  </chrow>
</choicetable>
```

See the <choicetable> entry for complete usage details and examples.

In AEM Guides: The chhead is optional — if omitted, default headers 'Option' and 'Description' may be generated by the processor.

<chrow> is a data row in a <choicetable>, containing one <choption> and one <chdesc>.

What is <chrow>?
Each <chrow> represents one choice that the user can make within a task step. It always has exactly two cells: the option name and its description.

Example:
```xml
<choicetable>
  <chrow>
    <choption>AEM Sites</choption>
    <chdesc>Publishes content as AEM Sites pages for web delivery.</chdesc>
  </chrow>
  <chrow>
    <choption>Native PDF</choption>
    <chdesc>Generates PDF using the built-in AEM Guides PDF engine.</chdesc>
  </chrow>
</choicetable>
```

See the <choicetable> entry for full context.

In AEM Guides: Each chrow renders as a table row in the choice table. The choicetable goes inside a <step> element in task topics.

<choption> is the option/choice cell in a <chrow> within a <choicetable>. It names the choice the user can select.

Example:
```xml
<chrow>
  <choption>Dark mode</choption>
  <chdesc>Switches the editor to a dark color scheme.</chdesc>
</chrow>
```

See the <choicetable> entry for full context and examples.

In AEM Guides: Rendered as the first column cell in the choice table across all output formats.

<chdesc> is the description cell in a <chrow> within a <choicetable>. It explains what the choice does or means.

Example:
```xml
<chrow>
  <choption>Auto-save</choption>
  <chdesc>Automatically saves your work every 60 seconds. Disable if you experience performance issues with large topics.</chdesc>
</chrow>
```

See the <choicetable> entry for full context and examples.

In AEM Guides: Rendered as the second column cell in the choice table. Supports rich content including paragraphs, lists, and inline elements.

<choptionhd> is the header label for the option column in a <choicetable>. It goes inside <chhead>.

Example:
```xml
<chhead>
  <choptionhd>Output Type</choptionhd>
  <chdeschd>Details</chdeschd>
</chhead>
```

If <chhead> is omitted, processors typically generate default headers. See the <choicetable> entry for full context.

In AEM Guides: Rendered as the first column header of the choice table.

<chdeschd> is the header label for the description column in a <choicetable>. It goes inside <chhead>.

Example:
```xml
<chhead>
  <choptionhd>Setting</choptionhd>
  <chdeschd>What it does</chdeschd>
</chhead>
```

See the <choicetable> entry for full context.

In AEM Guides: Rendered as the second column header of the choice table.

text_content:
<thead> is the header section of a CALS <table>, inside <tgroup>. Contains <row> elements that form the table header.

Example:
```xml
<thead>
  <row>
    <entry>Column A</entry>
    <entry>Column B</entry>
  </row>
</thead>
```

IMPORTANT: <thead> is for CALS <table> only. For <simpletable>, use <sthead>. These are NOT interchangeable.

In AEM Guides: Header rows are rendered with distinct styling (bold, shaded) across all output formats.

<tbody> is the body section of a CALS <table>, inside <tgroup>. Contains the data <row> elements.

Example:
```xml
<tbody>
  <row>
    <entry>Value 1</entry>
    <entry>Value 2</entry>
  </row>
</tbody>
```

IMPORTANT: <tbody> is required in a CALS <table> tgroup. It is NOT used in <simpletable> (which has no explicit body wrapper).

In AEM Guides: The tbody rows are rendered as standard data rows in all output formats.

<row> is a single row in a CALS <table>, used inside <thead> or <tbody>. Contains <entry> elements.

Example:
```xml
<row>
  <entry>Cell 1</entry>
  <entry>Cell 2</entry>
  <entry>Cell 3</entry>
</row>
```

IMPORTANT: <row> is for CALS <table> ONLY. For <simpletable>, use <strow>. For <choicetable>, use <chrow>.

In AEM Guides: Rows support spanning via @namest and @nameend attributes on <entry>.

Detailed description with code formatting.

  • Option A
  • Option B
 Spanning two columns ``` Column and row spanning: - @namest and @nameend — span columns (reference colspec names) - @morerows — span rows downward (e.g., morerows="2" spans 3 rows total) IMPORTANT: is for CALS
ONLY. For , use . In AEM Guides: - Web Editor: Supports merge/split cells via right-click context menu. - PDF Output: Column/row spanning rendered correctly. - Sites Output: Renders as
or with colspan/rowspan attributes.

Wrong spanning: use namest/nameend for columns, morerows for rows
Placing entry outside <row>
correct_examples:
<row>
  <entry>Cell 1</entry>
  <entry namest="c2" nameend="c3">Spans 2 cols</entry>
</row>

NOT for simpletable - use <stentry>

text_content:
<colspec> defines the properties of a column in a CALS <table>, inside <tgroup>.

What is <colspec>?
It specifies the column name (used for spanning), width, and alignment. Column specs must appear before <thead> and <tbody> in the <tgroup>.

Example:
```xml
<tgroup cols="3">
  <colspec colname="c1" colnum="1" colwidth="1*"/>
  <colspec colname="c2" colnum="2" colwidth="3*"/>
  <colspec colname="c3" colnum="3" colwidth="1*"/>
  <thead>...</thead>
  <tbody>...</tbody>
</tgroup>
```

Column width formats:
- Proportional: colwidth="1*", colwidth="3*" — column 2 is 3x wider than columns 1 and 3.
- Fixed: colwidth="5cm", colwidth="200px"
- Mixed: colwidth="2in" alongside colwidth="1*"

In AEM Guides:
- Web Editor: Column widths can be adjusted by dragging column borders.
- PDF Output: colwidth values are respected in generated PDF tables.
- Sites Output: Proportional widths converted to percentage-based HTML column widths.
common_mistakes: Missing colname - needed for cell spanning
Wrong colwidth syntax - use proportional (1*, 2*) or fixed (2in)
colspec count not matching tgroup cols value
Placing colspec outside <tgroup>
correct_examples:
<tgroup cols="3">
  <colspec colname="c1" colwidth="1*"/>
  <colspec colname="c2" colwidth="2*"/>
  <colspec colname="c3" colwidth="1*"/>
  <tbody>...</tbody>
</tgroup>
usage_contexts: Define column names and widths inside <tgroup>
colname enables cell spanning via namest/nameend

text_content:
<prophead> is the header row of a <properties> table in a <reference> topic.

What is <prophead>?
It labels the three columns of a properties table: type, value, and description. It contains <proptypehd>, <propvaluehd>, and <propdeschd>.

Structure:
```xml
<properties>
  <prophead>
    <proptypehd>Type</proptypehd>
    <propvaluehd>Value</propvaluehd>
    <propdeschd>Description</propdeschd>
  </prophead>
  <property>
    <proptype>String</proptype>
    <propvalue>output.dir</propvalue>
    <propdesc>Directory path for generated output files.</propdesc>
  </property>
</properties>
```

The <properties> table is unique to <reference> topics (<refbody>). It provides a semantic three-column table specifically for documenting properties, parameters, or configuration settings.

In AEM Guides: Properties tables are rendered as styled three-column tables in all output formats.
common_mistakes: Using <thead> instead of <prophead> — <thead> is for CALS tables, <prophead> is for <properties>
Wrong child elements — prophead uses <proptypehd>/<propvaluehd>/<propdeschd>, NOT <entry> or <stentry>
Omitting prophead entirely — without it, readers don't know what the 3 columns represent
Using custom column names in <property> instead of <prophead> — the header row is prophead's job
correct_examples:
Standard headers:
<prophead>
  <proptypehd>Type</proptypehd>
  <propvaluehd>Property</propvaluehd>
  <propdeschd>Description</propdeschd>
</prophead>

Custom headers for API docs:
<prophead>
  <proptypehd>Required</proptypehd>
  <propvaluehd>Parameter</propvaluehd>
  <propdeschd>Notes</propdeschd>
</prophead>
usage_contexts: Labeling the 3 columns of a properties table
Customizing column labels (Type/Property/Description vs Required/Parameter/Notes)

text_content:
<property> is a data row in a <properties> table in a <reference> topic.

What is <property>?
Each <property> describes one property/parameter with three cells: type, value, and description. All three child elements are optional — you can omit any column.

Example:
```xml
<properties>
  <property>
    <proptype>Boolean</proptype>
    <propvalue>enable.toc</propvalue>
    <propdesc>When set to true, a table of contents is generated in the PDF output.</propdesc>
  </property>
  <property>
    <proptype>Integer</proptype>
    <propvalue>max.depth</propvalue>
    <propdesc>Maximum heading depth to include in TOC. Default is 4.</propdesc>
  </property>
</properties>
```

In AEM Guides: Each property row is rendered in the three-column properties table format across all outputs.
common_mistakes: Using <row> instead of <property> — <row> is for CALS tables, <property> is for properties tables
Putting text directly in <property> — content must go inside <proptype>, <propvalue>, or <propdesc> children
Thinking all 3 children are required — all are OPTIONAL, use only the columns you need
Using <entry> inside <property> — properties table cells are <proptype>/<propvalue>/<propdesc>, not <entry>
correct_examples:
Full 3-column row:
<property>
  <proptype>String</proptype>
  <propvalue>output.dir</propvalue>
  <propdesc>Directory for generated output files.</propdesc>
</property>

Minimal row (only value + description):
<property>
  <propvalue>--verbose</propvalue>
  <propdesc>Enable detailed logging output.</propdesc>
</property>
usage_contexts: Each <property> row documents one parameter, setting, or configuration option
Children are optional — omit columns that don't apply

text_content:
<proptype> is the FIRST column cell in a <property> row inside a <properties> table. It describes the TYPE or CATEGORY of the property.

WHAT TO PUT IN proptype:
- Data type: String, Boolean, Integer, Float, Array, Object
- Requirement: Required, Optional, Deprecated
- Category grouping: Input, Output, Advanced, Security
- Parameter kind: Path parameter, Query parameter, Header

proptype can contain inline markup like <codeph>, <keyword>, <ph>.

DIFFERENCE FROM propvalue AND propdesc:
- proptype = WHAT TYPE is it (String, Required, etc.)
- propvalue = WHAT IS IT CALLED or what are the allowed values
- propdesc = WHAT DOES IT DO (explanation, constraints)
common_mistakes: Confusing proptype with propvalue — proptype is the TYPE (String, Boolean), propvalue is the NAME or ALLOWED VALUES
Putting the property name in proptype — the name goes in propvalue
Putting the description in proptype — the description goes in propdesc
Using very generic types like 'parameter' — be specific: String, Integer, Boolean, Required, Optional
correct_examples:
Data type:
<property>
  <proptype>Boolean</proptype>
  <propvalue>enable.toc</propvalue>
  <propdesc>Enable table of contents generation.</propdesc>
</property>

Requirement indicator:
<property>
  <proptype>Required</proptype>
  <propvalue>api_key</propvalue>
  <propdesc>Your API authentication key. Obtain from the admin console.</propdesc>
</property>
usage_contexts: Data type column in property tables (String, Boolean, Integer)
Requirement indicator (Required, Optional, Deprecated)
Category grouping (Input, Output, Advanced)

text_content:
<propvalue> is the SECOND column cell in a <property> row inside a <properties> table. It holds the property NAME, ALLOWED VALUES, or DEFAULT VALUE.

WHAT TO PUT IN propvalue:
- Property name/identifier: output.format, max-retries, --verbose
- Allowed values: true | false, pdf | html5 | sites
- Default value: 30 (seconds), /var/log/output
- Configuration key: aem.guides.pdf.toc.depth

propvalue can contain inline markup like <codeph>, <varname>, <filepath>.

DIFFERENCE FROM proptype AND propdesc:
- proptype = WHAT TYPE is it (String, Required)
- propvalue = WHAT IS IT CALLED / what values are allowed
- propdesc = WHAT DOES IT DO (explanation)
common_mistakes: Putting the description in propvalue — descriptions go in propdesc, not propvalue
Putting the data type in propvalue — types go in proptype (String, Boolean, etc.)
Writing full sentences in propvalue — keep it to names, values, or short value lists
Not using <codeph> for code-like values — wrap property names and values in <codeph>
correct_examples:
Property name:
<property>
  <proptype>String</proptype>
  <propvalue><codeph>output.format</codeph></propvalue>
  <propdesc>Output format for publishing.</propdesc>
</property>

Allowed values:
<property>
  <proptype>Enum</proptype>
  <propvalue><codeph>pdf</codeph> | <codeph>html5</codeph> | <codeph>sites</codeph></propvalue>
  <propdesc>Target output format. Default: <codeph>pdf</codeph>.</propdesc>
</property>
usage_contexts: Property name or identifier
Allowed/enumerated values
Default configuration values
CLI flags and options

is the THIRD column cell in a row inside a table. It explains WHAT THE PROPERTY DOES — its purpose, behavior, constraints, and side effects. WHAT TO PUT IN propdesc: - Explanation of what the property controls - Valid value ranges or constraints - Default behavior when not set - Side effects or dependencies on other properties - Links to related settings via propdesc can contain block elements:

Duplicating propvalue content — propdesc should explain, not repeat the name

Not using block elements when needed — propdesc supports

,

Putting the property name in propdesc — the name goes in propvalue

Simple description:
<propdesc>Maximum number of concurrent publishing jobs. Default: 4. Increase for large documentation sets.</propdesc>

Rich description with block elements:

Controls the maximum TOC depth in PDF output.

Values above 6 may cause rendering issues in narrow page layouts.

Documenting constraints, defaults, and side effects

defines post-requisites or follow-up actions after completing a task. What is ? It appears at the end of , after and . Use it to tell the user what to do next — verification steps, related procedures, or cleanup actions. Structure in taskbody: ```xml ... ... ... ... ... ``` Example: ```xml

After creating the output preset:

Set the baseline in the Baseline tab. ``` When to use: - Use when order matters (step 1 before step 2). - Use when all steps are independent and can be done in any sequence. - Use when you need free-form procedural text without structured steps. creates a footnote. What is ? It marks content that should appear as a footnote. In PDF output, the content is moved to the bottom of the page. In HTML output, it typically becomes an endnote or inline popup. Example: ```xml

DITA was originally developed by IBMIBM released DITA as an open standard to OASIS in 2005. and is now maintained by the OASIS DITA Technical Committee.

``` Footnote with @callout: ```xml

AEM Guides supports DITA 1.3Some DITA 1.3 features require specific configuration. features.

``` Common mistakes: - Using footnotes excessively — they disrupt reading flow. Use for important asides instead. - Placing inside a table cell and expecting page-bottom footnotes — behavior varies by processor. In AEM Guides: - PDF Output (Native): Footnotes appear at the bottom of the page with auto-numbered callouts. - Sites Output: Footnotes are rendered as endnotes or tooltip popups depending on the template. - Web Editor: Footnotes appear as inline annotations in Author mode.

creates an index entry for back-of-book or generated indexes. What is ? It marks content for inclusion in a generated index. Index terms can be nested to create primary, secondary, and tertiary index entries. They can be placed in // (topic-level) or inline within body content (point-level). Examples: ```xml output presets PDFgenerating publishing PDF output native PDF AEM Sites output

Configure the timeout settingstimeout for optimal performance.

DITA-OTDITA Open Toolkit PDF outputNative PDF ``` Common mistakes: - Placing indexterms only inline — topic-level placement (in prolog) is preferred for maintainability. - Forgetting to nest — a flat list of indexterms produces a flat index without subcategories. In AEM Guides: - Web Editor: Index terms added via the metadata dialog or inline. They appear as markers in Author mode. - PDF Output (Native): Index is auto-generated from indexterms when the PDF template includes an index page layout. - Bookmap: Use in / to place the generated index.

is an author-only comment that is NOT included in final published output by default. What is ? It allows authors and reviewers to leave notes, questions, and comments directly in the DITA source. These comments are visible during authoring but are filtered out during publishing (unless draft mode is explicitly enabled). Example: ```xml

AEM Guides supports multiple output formats.

TODO: Add a comparison table of output formats here. Check with the PM for the latest list.

Each format can be configured through output presets.

This section needs technical review. The API endpoint may have changed in the latest release. ``` Attributes: - @author — who wrote the comment - @time — when the comment was written - @disposition — status: 'open', 'completed', 'rejected', etc. Common mistakes: - Relying on draft-comment for published notes — use instead for reader-visible callouts. - Forgetting to resolve draft-comments before final publishing. In AEM Guides: - Web Editor: Draft comments appear as highlighted annotations in Author mode. They are hidden in Preview mode. - PDF Output: Excluded by default. Enable draft mode in output preset settings to include them (useful for review copies). - Sites Output: Excluded by default. Can be included via DITAVAL or output preset configuration.

<required-cleanup> flags content that needs revision or cleanup before publication.

What is <required-cleanup>?
It wraps content that is known to be incomplete, incorrect, or in need of restructuring. Unlike <draft-comment> (which is a note), <required-cleanup> wraps actual content that should be fixed. It serves as a mandatory action item — the content inside must be addressed before final publication.

Example:
```xml
<section>
  <title>Configuration Parameters</title>
  <required-cleanup remap="ul">
    This section was migrated from legacy docs. The parameter list needs to be converted to a properties table and verified against the current API.
    - timeout: connection timeout
    - retries: number of retries
    - format: output format
  </required-cleanup>
</section>

<!-- With remap showing original element type -->
<required-cleanup remap="table">
  Original table data lost during migration. Reconstruct from source.
</required-cleanup>
```

Attributes:
- @remap — indicates the original element type of the content (useful when migrating from other formats)

Common mistakes:
- Leaving <required-cleanup> in production content — processors will warn or error on this element.
- Using it as a general comment mechanism — use <draft-comment> for comments, <required-cleanup> for content that must be fixed.

In AEM Guides:
- Web Editor: Displayed with a warning highlight in Author mode.
- PDF/Sites Output: Excluded by default with a build warning. Content must be cleaned up before final publishing.

<lq> (long quote) is a block-level quotation element, optionally with a citation source.

What is <lq>?
It's used for extended quotations — passages from other documents, specifications, or authorities. Unlike inline quotes, <lq> is a block element that stands apart from surrounding paragraphs. It can include an @href attribute for the citation source and @reftitle for the source title.

Example:
```xml
<lq href="https://www.oasis-open.org/committees/dita/" reftitle="OASIS DITA Specification">
  DITA defines a set of document types for authoring and organizing topic-oriented information, as well as a set of mechanisms for combining, extending, and constraining document types.
</lq>

<!-- Simple long quote without citation -->
<lq>
  Content reuse is the single most impactful practice for reducing documentation costs and improving consistency across deliverables.
</lq>
```

Common mistakes:
- Using <lq> for short inline quotes — use <q> for brief inline quotations.
- Forgetting @reftitle — without it, the citation link has no display text in some processors.

In AEM Guides:
- PDF Output: Rendered as an indented block quote with optional citation.
- Sites Output: Rendered as <blockquote> in HTML.
- Web Editor: Displayed as an indented block in Author mode.

What is

It's the base preformatted element. Use it when you need to preserve exact spacing and line formatting but the content doesn't fit a more specific element like  or .

Example:
```xml

--------    --------    --------
Value 1     Value 2     Value 3

```

Comparison of preformatted text elements:
| Element | Purpose | When to use |
|---------|---------|-------------|
|

|  | Source code listing | Programming code, XML, JSON, configuration files |
|  | Terminal/console output | Command output, log messages, terminal sessions |

- Using  for non-code preformatted text —

- PDF Output:
