<lines> preserves line breaks but uses a proportional (non-monospace) font.

What is <lines>?
Unlike <pre> and <codeblock>, <lines> maintains line breaks without switching to monospace. It's ideal for poetry, addresses, song lyrics, and other text where line structure matters but monospace is inappropriate.

Example:
```xml
<lines>
Adobe Systems Incorporated
345 Park Avenue
San Jose, CA 95110
United States
</lines>

<lines>
Structured content flows like water,
Through maps and topics, authored with care,
Single-sourced, translated, delivered everywhere.
</lines>
```

In AEM Guides:
- PDF Output: Rendered in the body font (not monospace) with preserved line breaks.
- Sites Output: Rendered as <div class='lines'> or <pre> with proportional font styling.

<screen> represents text displayed on a computer screen, terminal, or console.

What is <screen>?
It's a preformatted block specifically for terminal output, console sessions, command prompts, and log output. It signals to processors that this is computer-displayed text, enabling distinct styling from <codeblock> (source code) or <pre> (generic).

Example:
```xml
<screen>
$ dita --input=userguide.ditamap --format=pdf
Processing userguide.ditamap
Rendering topics: 45/45
Build successful. Output written to: /out/userguide.pdf
</screen>
```

In AEM Guides:
- PDF Output: Rendered in monospace with a background color distinct from <codeblock>.
- Sites Output: Rendered as <pre class='screen'> in HTML.

<msgblock> contains a multi-line system message, error message, or log output.

What is <msgblock>?
It's a preformatted block for system messages — error messages, warning messages, stack traces, and system notifications. It signals that the content is a system-generated message rather than user-entered code or commands.

Example:
```xml
<msgblock>
ERROR [2024-03-15 10:23:45] com.adobe.fmdita.publish.PDFGenerator
  Failed to generate PDF output for map: userguide.ditamap
  Cause: Missing template file: custom-template.pdf
  Resolution: Verify the template path in output preset settings.
</msgblock>
```

In AEM Guides:
- PDF Output: Rendered with message-specific styling (often red or warning-colored background).
- Sites Output: Rendered as <pre class='msgblock'> in HTML.

<frontmatter> contains the front-of-book material in a <bookmap>.

What is <frontmatter>?
It's the container for all preliminary pages that appear before the first chapter: title page, preface, table of contents, dedication, notices, and other introductory content.

Structure:
```xml
<bookmap>
  <booktitle>
    <mainbooktitle>AEM Guides User Guide</mainbooktitle>
  </booktitle>
  <frontmatter>
    <booklists>
      <toc/>              <!-- Table of contents placement -->
      <figurelist/>       <!-- List of figures -->
      <tablelist/>        <!-- List of tables -->
    </booklists>
    <preface href="preface.dita"/>
    <notices href="legal-notices.dita"/>
    <dedication href="dedication.dita"/>
  </frontmatter>
  <chapter href="getting-started.dita"/>
  <chapter href="configuration.dita"/>
  <backmatter>...</backmatter>
</bookmap>
```

Common children:
- <booklists> — generated lists (TOC, figure list, table list, index)
- <preface> — introductory topic
- <notices> — legal notices, copyright
- <dedication> — dedication page
- <topicref> — any additional front matter topics

Common mistakes:
- Placing <toc/> directly in <frontmatter> — it must be inside <booklists>.
- Forgetting frontmatter entirely — chapters still work but you won't get a proper title page and TOC in PDF output.

In AEM Guides:
- PDF Output (Native): Frontmatter elements are rendered using specific page layouts (title page, TOC page, preface page) defined in the PDF template.
- Sites Output: Frontmatter content is typically included as introductory pages.
- Web Editor: Bookmap structure is visible in the Map View panel.

<backmatter> contains the back-of-book material in a <bookmap>.

What is <backmatter>?
It's the container for all content that appears after the last chapter: appendixes, glossary, index, and other end-of-book sections.

Structure:
```xml
<bookmap>
  <booktitle>...</booktitle>
  <frontmatter>...</frontmatter>
  <chapter href="chapter1.dita"/>
  <chapter href="chapter2.dita"/>
  <backmatter>
    <appendix href="appendix-a.dita"/>
    <appendix href="appendix-b.dita"/>
    <booklists>
      <glossarylist href="glossary.ditamap"/>
      <indexlist/>        <!-- Generated index -->
    </booklists>
  </backmatter>
</bookmap>
```

Common children:
- <appendix> — appendix topics/maps
- <booklists> — generated lists (glossary, index)
- <topicref> — any additional back matter topics
- <colophon> — publication details

Common mistakes:
- Placing <appendix> outside <backmatter> — it should be inside backmatter for proper book structure.
- Forgetting <indexlist/> — without it, indexterms in your topics won't generate an index.

In AEM Guides:
- PDF Output (Native): Backmatter elements use specific page layouts (appendix, glossary, index pages).
- Sites Output: Backmatter content appears as end-of-publication pages.
- Web Editor: Visible in Map View with proper nesting under backmatter.

<chapter> defines a chapter in a <bookmap>.

What is <chapter>?
It's a topicref specialization that represents a book chapter. It uses @href to point to a topic or nested map that contains the chapter content. Chapters can contain nested <topicref> elements for sections within the chapter.

Example:
```xml
<bookmap>
  <booktitle><mainbooktitle>AEM Guides Admin Guide</mainbooktitle></booktitle>
  <frontmatter>...</frontmatter>

  <chapter href="ch01-installation.dita">
    <topicref href="system-requirements.dita"/>
    <topicref href="installation-steps.dita"/>
    <topicref href="post-install-config.dita"/>
  </chapter>

  <chapter href="ch02-configuration.dita">
    <topicref href="global-settings.dita"/>
    <topicref href="output-presets-config.dita"/>
  </chapter>

  <!-- Chapter referencing a sub-map -->
  <chapter href="ch03-publishing.ditamap" format="ditamap"/>

  <backmatter>...</backmatter>
</bookmap>
```

Common mistakes:
- Using <chapter> in a regular <map> — chapters are only valid in <bookmap>.
- Using <topicref> at the top level of a bookmap instead of <chapter> — it works but loses the chapter semantics for PDF generation.

In AEM Guides:
- PDF Output (Native): Each chapter starts on a new page with chapter numbering (Chapter 1, Chapter 2, etc.) based on PDF template settings.
- Sites Output: Chapters are rendered as major sections with navigation.
- Web Editor: Chapters appear as top-level nodes in the Map View.

<part> groups chapters into larger divisions in a <bookmap>. Like a super-chapter.

What is <part>?
In large books, chapters are grouped into parts (e.g., 'Part I: Getting Started', 'Part II: Advanced Topics'). A <part> contains one or more <chapter> elements and generates a part divider page in PDF output.

Example:
```xml
<bookmap>
  <booktitle><mainbooktitle>AEM Guides Complete Reference</mainbooktitle></booktitle>
  <frontmatter>...</frontmatter>

  <part href="part1-overview.dita">
    <chapter href="ch01-introduction.dita"/>
    <chapter href="ch02-architecture.dita"/>
  </part>

  <part href="part2-authoring.dita">
    <chapter href="ch03-web-editor.dita"/>
    <chapter href="ch04-content-reuse.dita"/>
    <chapter href="ch05-maps.dita"/>
  </part>

  <part href="part3-publishing.dita">
    <chapter href="ch06-output-presets.dita"/>
    <chapter href="ch07-pdf-publishing.dita"/>
  </part>

  <backmatter>...</backmatter>
</bookmap>
```

In AEM Guides:
- PDF Output (Native): Part divider pages can be styled with specific page layouts in the PDF template.
- Sites Output: Parts create a higher-level grouping in the navigation structure.

<appendix> defines an appendix in a <bookmap>. It goes inside <backmatter>.

What is <appendix>?
It's a topicref specialization for supplementary content at the end of a book. Appendixes use letter-based numbering (Appendix A, Appendix B) in most output formats.

Example:
```xml
<backmatter>
  <appendix href="appendix-a-shortcuts.dita">
    <topicref href="editor-shortcuts.dita"/>
    <topicref href="review-shortcuts.dita"/>
  </appendix>
  <appendix href="appendix-b-migration.dita"/>
  <booklists>
    <glossarylist href="glossary.ditamap"/>
    <indexlist/>
  </booklists>
</backmatter>
```

In AEM Guides:
- PDF Output (Native): Appendixes are numbered with letters (A, B, C) and start on new pages per the PDF template.
- Sites Output: Appendixes appear in the navigation after the main chapters.

<bookmeta> contains metadata about the book in a <bookmap>.

What is <bookmeta>?
It holds publication-level metadata: author, publisher, copyright, publication dates, ISBN, and other bibliographic information. This metadata can be pulled into the PDF output's title page, copyright page, and headers/footers.

Example:
```xml
<bookmap>
  <booktitle>
    <mainbooktitle>AEM Guides User Guide</mainbooktitle>
    <booktitlealt>Version 4.3</booktitlealt>
  </booktitle>
  <bookmeta>
    <authorinformation>
      <personinfo><namedetails><personname>
        <firstname>Adobe</firstname><lastname>Documentation Team</lastname>
      </personname></namedetails></personinfo>
    </authorinformation>
    <publisherinformation>
      <published><publishtype value="general"/><completed><year>2024</year></completed></published>
    </publisherinformation>
    <bookrights>
      <copyrfirst><year>2020</year></copyrfirst>
      <copyrlast><year>2024</year></copyrlast>
      <bookowner><organization>Adobe Systems Incorporated</organization></bookowner>
    </bookrights>
  </bookmeta>
  <frontmatter>...</frontmatter>
</bookmap>
```

In AEM Guides:
- PDF Output (Native): Bookmeta values can be inserted into title pages, headers, footers, and copyright pages using the PDF template editor.
- Web Editor: Bookmeta is editable through the bookmap properties panel.

<booktitle> provides the structured title of a bookmap.

What is <booktitle>?
Unlike a simple <title>, <booktitle> allows structured title components: main title, subtitle, and alternate title. It replaces <title> as the first child of <bookmap>.

Example:
```xml
<bookmap>
  <booktitle>
    <booklibrary>Adobe Experience Manager</booklibrary>
    <mainbooktitle>AEM Guides User Guide</mainbooktitle>
    <booktitlealt>For Content Authors and Administrators</booktitlealt>
  </booktitle>
  <bookmeta>...</bookmeta>
</bookmap>
```

Children:
- <booklibrary> — the product or library name (e.g., 'Adobe Experience Manager')
- <mainbooktitle> — the primary book title (required)
- <booktitlealt> — subtitle or alternative title

Common mistakes:
- Using <title> instead of <booktitle> in a bookmap — <title> works but you lose the structured title components.

In AEM Guides:
- PDF Output: The structured title components are placed on the title page layout. <booklibrary> appears above the main title; <booktitlealt> below.
- Web Editor: The bookmap title is shown in the Map View and Repository panels.

<booklists> is a container for generated lists in a <bookmap>: table of contents, index, list of figures, list of tables, and glossary.

What is <booklists>?
It holds placeholder elements that tell the processor where to generate automatic lists. It can appear in both <frontmatter> (for TOC, figure lists) and <backmatter> (for index, glossary).

Example:
```xml
<!-- In frontmatter: TOC and list of figures -->
<frontmatter>
  <booklists>
    <toc/>              <!-- Generated table of contents -->
    <figurelist/>       <!-- Generated list of figures -->
    <tablelist/>        <!-- Generated list of tables -->
  </booklists>
  <preface href="preface.dita"/>
</frontmatter>

<!-- In backmatter: glossary and index -->
<backmatter>
  <appendix href="appendix-a.dita"/>
  <booklists>
    <glossarylist href="glossary.ditamap"/>
    <indexlist/>        <!-- Generated index from indexterms -->
  </booklists>
</backmatter>
```

Available list elements:
| Element | What it generates | Typical placement |
|---------|-------------------|-------------------|
| <toc/> | Table of Contents | frontmatter |
| <figurelist/> | List of Figures | frontmatter |
| <tablelist/> | List of Tables | frontmatter |
| <indexlist/> | Back-of-book Index | backmatter |
| <glossarylist> | Glossary | backmatter |

In AEM Guides:
- PDF Output (Native): Each list type uses a dedicated page layout in the PDF template. The TOC, index, and other lists are auto-generated.
- Sites Output: Lists are generated as navigational elements.

AEM Guides Output Presets — configuring and managing publishing outputs.

What are Output Presets?
Output presets define how your DITA content is transformed into deliverables. Each preset specifies an output format, template, conditions, and other settings. You can create multiple presets for the same map to generate different outputs (e.g., internal PDF, customer-facing HTML, translation-ready XLIFF).

Available output types in AEM Guides:
| Output Type | Description | Engine |
|-------------|-------------|--------|
| AEM Sites | Publishes as AEM web pages | Built-in |
| PDF (Native) | Generates PDF using AEM Guides' built-in engine | Native |
| PDF (DITA-OT) | Generates PDF using DITA Open Toolkit | DITA-OT |
| Custom HTML5 | Generates responsive HTML5 output | DITA-OT |
| JSON | Generates JSON for headless delivery | Built-in |
| Custom | Custom transformation using DITA-OT plugins | DITA-OT |

How to create an output preset:
1. Open the DITA map in the Map Dashboard.
2. Go to the Output Presets tab.
3. Click Add (+ icon) and select the output type.
4. Configure settings: template, conditions (DITAVAL), baseline, output path.
5. Click Save. Click Generate to produce the output.

Key preset settings:
- Transformation: The output type/format.
- DITAVAL: Condition filter file to include/exclude content.
- Baseline: Lock content to a specific version snapshot.
- Template: PDF template or HTML5 template to use.
- Output Path: Where the generated output is stored.
- Post-generation: Workflow to trigger after output is ready.

Common mistakes:
- Generating output without a baseline — you may get draft content mixed with published content.
- Forgetting to set DITAVAL — conditional content (e.g., internal-only notes) may leak into customer-facing output.
- Not testing with Preview before full generation.

In AEM Guides:
- Map Dashboard: The primary interface for managing output presets. Each map has its own set of presets.
- Web Editor: Quick Generate option available from the map menu for immediate output generation.
- Bulk Publishing: Dashboard allows generating multiple presets simultaneously.

AEM Guides Native PDF Publishing — generating PDF output without DITA-OT.

What is Native PDF?
Native PDF is AEM Guides' built-in PDF generation engine that converts DITA content directly to PDF without going through DITA Open Toolkit. It provides a visual template editor, page layout designer, and CSS-based stylesheet system for complete control over PDF appearance.

Key components:
1. PDF Template: The master configuration containing page layouts, stylesheets, and resources.
2. Page Layouts: Define the structure of different page types (cover, TOC, chapter, index, blank).
3. Stylesheets: CSS-based styling for content elements (headings, tables, code blocks, notes).
4. Header/Footer: Configurable running headers and footers with variables (page number, chapter title, book title).
5. TOC Configuration: Table of contents depth, styling, and leader dots.
6. Index Generation: Automatic index from <indexterm> elements.

Template editor features:
- Visual page layout designer with drag-and-drop regions.
- CSS stylesheet editor with live preview.
- Variable system: {{chapter.title}}, {{page.number}}, {{book.title}}, etc.
- Resource management: fonts, images, logos for the template.
- Condition styling: Different visual treatments for different audience/platform conditions.

Comparison: Native PDF vs DITA-OT PDF:
| Feature | Native PDF | DITA-OT PDF |
|---------|-----------|-------------|
| Template system | Visual editor + CSS | XSL-FO customization |
| Ease of use | Low learning curve | Requires XSL-FO expertise |
| Customization | CSS-based | XSL-FO overrides |
| Page layouts | Visual designer | Plugin customization |
| Performance | Fast (direct rendering) | Slower (multi-step pipeline) |
| Header/Footer | Visual editor | XSL-FO attributes |
| Index support | Built-in | Plugin-dependent |
| Cross-references | Auto-resolved | Auto-resolved |

Common mistakes:
- Trying to use DITA-OT PDF customization techniques with Native PDF — they are completely different systems.
- Not setting up page layouts for all page types — missing layouts cause fallback rendering.
- Forgetting to configure fonts — custom fonts must be uploaded to the template resources.

In AEM Guides:
- Template Editor: Accessible from Tools > PDF Templates in the Web Editor sidebar.
- Output Preset: Select 'PDF' type and choose 'Native' as the engine.
- Preview: Use Quick Generate for rapid PDF preview during authoring.

AEM Guides Web Editor — the primary authoring environment for DITA content.

What is the Web Editor?
The Web Editor is AEM Guides' browser-based DITA authoring tool. It provides a rich editing experience with multiple panels, views, and tools for creating, editing, reviewing, and publishing DITA content without needing a desktop XML editor.

Key panels:
1. Repository Panel (left): Browse and manage files in the DAM. Drag-and-drop topics into maps.
2. Map View Panel (left): View and edit map structure, reorder topics, set attributes.
3. Editor Area (center): The main editing canvas with three modes.
4. Properties Panel (right): Edit attributes of the selected element.
5. Content Reuse Panel (right): Search and insert reusable content (conrefs, keyrefs).
6. Conditions Panel (right): Apply and manage conditional attributes.
7. Review Panel: Comment threading, accept/reject changes.

Editor modes:
| Mode | Purpose | Best for |
|------|---------|----------|
| Author | WYSIWYG-like visual editing | Day-to-day content authoring |
| Source | Raw XML editing | Advanced element manipulation, debugging |
| Preview | Read-only rendered view | Reviewing content appearance |

Key features:
- Smart Insert: Context-aware element insertion — only valid child elements are offered.
- Content Reuse: Insert conref/conkeyref via dialog with preview.
- Inline Validation: Real-time DITA validation with error markers.
- Version History: Compare versions, revert, create labels.
- Find & Replace: Across topics with regex support.
- Snippets: Reusable content fragments for common patterns.
- Tag View: Toggle element tag visibility in Author mode.

Key shortcuts:
- Ctrl+Space: Insert element (smart insert)
- Ctrl+S: Save topic
- Ctrl+Shift+S: Save as new version
- Alt+Enter: Element properties
- Ctrl+B/I/U: Bold/Italic/Underline (mapped to <b>/<i>/<u>)
- Ctrl+1/2/3: Insert note types

In AEM Guides:
- The Web Editor is the recommended authoring environment for all AEM Guides users.
- Accessible from: AEM Navigation > Guides > Editor, or by opening any .dita/.ditamap file.
- Supports concurrent editing with automatic save and version management.

AEM Guides Review and Approval Workflow — collaborative content review within AEM.

What is the Review Workflow?
AEM Guides provides a built-in review system that allows authors to send content for review, reviewers to add comments and suggest changes, and authors to accept or reject feedback — all within the Web Editor.

Workflow steps:
1. Create Review Task: Author selects topics/map and creates a review task.
2. Assign Reviewers: Add users or groups as reviewers with a due date.
3. Notify Reviewers: System sends email notifications with a review link.
4. Review Content: Reviewers open topics in the Review panel, add comments, suggest changes.
5. Comment Threading: Authors and reviewers discuss comments in threaded conversations.
6. Accept/Reject: Author reviews each comment and accepts or rejects the suggested change.
7. Complete Review: Author marks the review as complete.

Creating a review task:
- From Map Dashboard: Select topics > Create Review Task.
- From Web Editor: Right-click topic > Send for Review.
- Settings: Title, description, due date, reviewers, version (baseline or latest).

Reviewer capabilities:
- Add inline comments on specific text selections.
- Add topic-level comments for general feedback.
- Suggest text replacements (tracked changes).
- Highlight text with different colors/categories.
- View other reviewers' comments.
- Reply to existing comments.

Review Dashboard:
- Shows all active review tasks.
- Status tracking: pending, in-progress, completed, overdue.
- Comment summary: total comments, resolved, unresolved.
- Export review report.

Common mistakes:
- Not specifying a baseline — reviewers may see different versions than intended.
- Not resolving all comments before publishing — unresolved comments indicate incomplete review.
- Sending too many topics in one review task — smaller, focused reviews get better feedback.

In AEM Guides:
- Web Editor: Review panel integrated into the editor for seamless authoring-review cycles.
- Email Integration: Automatic notifications to reviewers and authors.
- Audit Trail: All review actions are logged for compliance and traceability.

AEM Guides Baselines — snapshot versioned content for consistent publishing.

What is a Baseline?
A baseline is a snapshot of a DITA map and all its referenced topics at a specific point in time. It captures the exact version of each topic, allowing you to publish a consistent set of content even as authors continue editing. Baselines are essential for release management and versioned documentation.

How baselines work:
1. A baseline records the version number of every topic referenced by the map.
2. When you generate output using a baseline, the processor uses those specific versions — not the latest.
3. Multiple baselines can exist for the same map (e.g., v1.0, v2.0, v2.1-hotfix).

Creating a baseline:
- From Map Dashboard > Baseline tab > Create Baseline.
- Options:
  - Name: Descriptive name (e.g., 'Release 4.3', 'Sprint-22-Snapshot').
  - Based on: Latest version, specific date, or version labels.
  - Label-based: Select topics with a specific version label (e.g., 'approved').

Using baselines with output presets:
```
Output Preset Settings:
  Type: PDF (Native)
  Baseline: Release 4.3      <-- locks all topic versions
  DITAVAL: customer-facing    <-- condition filter
  Template: corporate-pdf     <-- PDF template
```

Baseline management:
- View: See all topics and their pinned versions.
- Edit: Update a baseline to include newer versions of specific topics.
- Duplicate: Copy a baseline as the starting point for a new release.
- Delete: Remove obsolete baselines.

Common mistakes:
- Publishing without a baseline — latest (possibly draft) content gets published.
- Not creating baselines before major content changes — you lose the ability to reproduce previous output.
- Confusing baselines with version labels — labels mark individual topics; baselines capture the entire map's state.

In AEM Guides:
- Map Dashboard: Baseline tab shows all baselines for the map.
- Output Presets: Each preset can be linked to a specific baseline.
- Versioning Integration: Baselines work with AEM's version management system.

DITAVAL Conditional Processing — filtering and flagging content based on metadata attributes. What is Conditional Processing? Conditional processing allows you to include, exclude, or flag content based on profiling attributes like @audience, @platform, @product, and @otherprops. You write content once with conditions applied, then use a DITAVAL file to control what appears in each output. Profiling attributes: | Attribute | Purpose | Example values | |-----------|---------|----------------| | @audience | Target reader | administrator, author, developer | | @platform | Operating system/platform | windows, mac, linux | | @product | Product variant | basic, professional, enterprise | | @otherprops | Custom conditions | internal, customer, partner | | @rev | Revision/version | 1.0, 2.0, 3.0 | | @props | Generic profiling | (any custom value) | Applying conditions to content: ```xml

This setting requires administrator access to the AEM instance.

Contact your administrator to change this setting.

Open C:\Program Files\AEM in Explorer. Open /Applications/AEM in Finder. ``` DITAVAL file example: ```xml Internal only ``` DITAVAL actions: | Action | Effect | |--------|--------| | include | Content is included in output | | exclude | Content is removed from output | | flag | Content is included with visual marker (icon, color) | | passthrough | Content passes through without filtering | Common mistakes: - Forgetting the default behavior — if no DITAVAL rule matches, content is INCLUDED by default. - Applying conditions at too fine a level (individual words) — apply at paragraph or section level for maintainability. - Not testing all condition combinations — some combinations may produce empty sections. In AEM Guides: - Web Editor: Conditions panel lets you apply profiling attributes visually. Color-coded highlighting shows which conditions are active. - Output Presets: Select a DITAVAL file in the preset to apply conditions during publishing. - Condition Presets: AEM Guides supports named condition presets that can be reused across output presets.

DITA 1.3 Branch Filtering — applying different DITAVAL filters to different branches of a map.

What is Branch Filtering?
Branch filtering allows you to apply different condition filters to different sections of the same DITA map. Instead of one DITAVAL for the entire map, you can attach specific DITAVAL files to specific <topicref> branches using <ditavalref>.

Why is this useful?
- Generate platform-specific chapters within a single map.
- Include a topic multiple times with different conditions (e.g., once for Windows, once for Mac).
- Avoid maintaining separate maps for each product variant.

Example:
```xml
<map>
  <title>Multi-Platform Installation Guide</title>

  <!-- Common introduction - no branch filter -->
  <topicref href="introduction.dita"/>

  <!-- Windows-specific branch -->
  <topicref href="installation.dita">
    <ditavalref href="ditaval/windows.ditaval">
      <ditavalmeta>
        <dvrResourceSuffix>-win</dvrResourceSuffix>
      </ditavalmeta>
    </ditavalref>
  </topicref>

  <!-- Mac-specific branch -->
  <topicref href="installation.dita">
    <ditavalref href="ditaval/mac.ditaval">
      <ditavalmeta>
        <dvrResourceSuffix>-mac</dvrResourceSuffix>
      </ditavalmeta>
    </ditavalref>
  </topicref>

  <!-- Common troubleshooting - no branch filter -->
  <topicref href="troubleshooting.dita"/>
</map>
```

In this example, installation.dita is included twice — once filtered for Windows and once for Mac. The <dvrResourceSuffix> ensures unique output filenames.

Key elements:
- <ditavalref>: References a DITAVAL file and applies it to the parent branch.
- <ditavalmeta>: Contains metadata for the branch filter.
- <dvrResourceSuffix>/<dvrResourcePrefix>: Modify output filenames to avoid conflicts.
- <dvrKeyscopeSuffix>/<dvrKeyscopePrefix>: Modify key scopes for the filtered branch.

Common mistakes:
- Forgetting <dvrResourceSuffix> when the same topic appears in multiple branches — causes filename conflicts.
- Applying ditavalref at the map root — this is equivalent to a global DITAVAL and defeats the purpose of branch filtering.
- Not understanding that branch filtering is a DITA 1.3 feature — older processors may not support it.

In AEM Guides:
- Web Editor: ditavalref elements can be added to map branches in Source mode.
- Processing: AEM Guides supports DITA 1.3 branch filtering in all output types.
- Alternative: For simpler cases, use output preset-level DITAVAL instead of branch filtering.

DITA Specialization — creating new element types by extending existing DITA types. What is Specialization? Specialization is DITA's mechanism for creating new, more specific element types based on existing ones. Instead of modifying the DITA standard, you extend it — your specialized elements inherit all the processing behavior of their base types while adding domain-specific semantics. How it works: 1. Choose a base type to specialize from (e.g., ,

,

). 2. Define new element names with more specific semantics. 3. Declare the inheritance chain in the @class attribute. 4. Processors that don't understand your specialization fall back to the base type processing. The @class attribute: Every DITA element has a @class attribute that records its inheritance chain: ```xml ``` Simple specialization example: ```xml ``` Types of specialization: | Type | What you specialize | Example | |------|--------------------|---------| | Topic specialization | New topic types | troubleshooting, faq, release-note | | Domain specialization | New inline/block elements | ui-d (uicontrol, menucascade), sw-d (cmdname, filepath) | | Map specialization | New map types | bookmap (specializes map), learningMap | Built-in DITA specializations: - , , — specialize - — specializes - , — UI domain - , — programming domain - , — software domain Common mistakes: - Modifying DITA DTDs directly instead of specializing — breaks interoperability. - Not including the full @class inheritance chain — processors can't fall back correctly. - Over-specializing — only specialize when you need distinct processing or validation. In AEM Guides: - The Web Editor supports custom specializations when properly registered. - Custom DTDs/schemas must be uploaded to the AEM repository. - PDF and Sites output processors use the @class attribute for fallback rendering.

DITA 1.3 Key Scoping — using the same key name with different values in different parts of a map. What is Key Scoping? Key scoping (introduced in DITA 1.3) allows the same key name to resolve to different values depending on where it's used in the map hierarchy. This is done using the @keyscope attribute on , , or other map elements. Why is this useful? - Reuse the same topic in multiple contexts with different variable values. - Product-specific content within a shared map structure. - Different translations or branding per section. Example: ```xml Multi-Product Guide AEM Guides Basic AEM Guides Enterprise ``` In topics, the key reference resolves based on scope: ```xml

Welcome to !

``` Accessing keys across scopes: - Within scope: — resolves to the local scope's value. - Cross-scope: — uses scope-qualified key name. Common mistakes: - Forgetting to set @keyscope — without it, keys are global and the last definition wins. - Not understanding scope inheritance — child scopes inherit parent scope keys. - Using key scoping when simple conditional processing would suffice. In AEM Guides: - Web Editor: Key scopes are visible in the Map View. Key resolution follows scope hierarchy. - Processing: AEM Guides supports DITA 1.3 key scoping in all output types. - Preview: Author mode shows resolved key values based on the current scope context.

DITA Cascading Metadata — how attributes and metadata flow from maps to topics.

What is Cascading Metadata?
In DITA, certain attributes set on map elements (<map>, <topicref>) automatically cascade (flow down) to child topicrefs and the topics they reference. This allows you to set metadata once at the map level and have it apply to all topics below.

Which attributes cascade?
| Attribute | Cascades? | Notes |
|-----------|-----------|-------|
| @audience | Yes | Profiling attribute |
| @platform | Yes | Profiling attribute |
| @product | Yes | Profiling attribute |
| @otherprops | Yes | Profiling attribute |
| @rev | Yes | Revision attribute |
| @props | Yes | Generic profiling |
| @linking | Yes | Controls cross-reference linking |
| @toc | Yes | Controls TOC inclusion |
| @print | Yes | Controls print inclusion |
| @search | Yes | Controls search indexing |
| @format | No | Must be set per topicref |
| @scope | No | Must be set per topicref |
| @type | No | Must be set per topicref |
| @href | No | Unique per topicref |

Example:
```xml
<map>
  <!-- All topics inherit audience='administrator' -->
  <topicref href="admin-guide.dita" audience="administrator">
    <topicref href="installation.dita"/>      <!-- inherits audience='administrator' -->
    <topicref href="configuration.dita"/>     <!-- inherits audience='administrator' -->
    <topicref href="basic-usage.dita" audience="author"/>  <!-- overrides to 'author' -->
  </topicref>
</map>
```

The @cascade attribute (DITA 1.3):
Controls how metadata cascading behaves:
```xml
<!-- merge: cascaded values merge with topic-level values (default) -->
<topicref href="topic.dita" audience="admin" cascade="merge"/>

<!-- nomerge: cascaded values are used only if the topic has no value -->
<topicref href="topic.dita" audience="admin" cascade="nomerge"/>
```

Common mistakes:
- Assuming @format and @scope cascade — they don't; set them on each topicref that needs them.
- Not understanding merge vs nomerge — with 'merge', a topic with audience='author' that inherits audience='admin' ends up with BOTH values.
- Setting conflicting cascading values at different levels without understanding resolution order.

In AEM Guides:
- Web Editor: Cascaded attributes are shown in the Properties panel with an indicator that they are inherited.
- Conditional Processing: Cascaded profiling attributes work with DITAVAL filtering.
- Map Dashboard: Metadata report shows effective (cascaded) attributes for all topics in the map.

AEM Guides Version Management — tracking, comparing, and managing content versions.

What is Version Management?
AEM Guides leverages AEM's built-in versioning system to track changes to DITA topics and maps over time. Every save can create a new version, allowing authors to compare changes, revert to previous versions, and label versions for release management.

Key capabilities:
1. Auto-versioning: Automatically creates a new version on every save (configurable).
2. Manual versioning: Authors explicitly create a version with Save As New Version (Ctrl+Shift+S).
3. Version labels: Tag versions with labels like 'approved', 'release-4.3', 'reviewed'.
4. Version comparison: Side-by-side diff showing additions, deletions, and modifications.
5. Version revert: Restore a topic to any previous version.
6. Version history: Complete audit trail of all changes with timestamps and authors.

Version operations in Web Editor:
- Save (Ctrl+S): Saves the file. May or may not create a version depending on auto-versioning settings.
- Save As New Version (Ctrl+Shift+S): Always creates a new version with an optional comment.
- Version History: View all versions with date, author, and comments.
- Compare Versions: Select two versions to see a visual diff.
- Revert: Restore content from a previous version (creates a new version with the old content).
- Labels: Add labels to versions for easy identification.

Version labels and baselines:
```
Topic: installation.dita
  Version 1.0 — label: 'draft'
  Version 1.1 — label: 'reviewed'
  Version 1.2 — label: 'approved', 'release-4.3'
  Version 1.3 — (current, no label)

Baseline 'Release 4.3' captures:
  installation.dita → Version 1.2 (labeled 'release-4.3')
  configuration.dita → Version 2.1 (labeled 'release-4.3')
  ...
```

Configuration:
- Auto-version on save: Enable/disable in AEM Guides configuration.
- Version limit: Maximum number of versions to retain per file.
- Version purge: Automated cleanup of old versions.

Common mistakes:
- Not labeling versions — makes it hard to identify which version was published.
- Confusing versions with baselines — versions are per-topic; baselines are per-map snapshots.
- Reverting without checking — reverting replaces current content; make sure you saved your current work.

In AEM Guides:
- Web Editor: Version history accessible from the right panel or file menu.
- Map Dashboard: Version information shown in the Topics tab.
- Bulk Operations: Apply labels to multiple topic versions simultaneously.

AEM Guides Translation Integration — managing multilingual content and translation workflows.

What is the Translation Workflow?
AEM Guides integrates with AEM's translation framework to support translating DITA content into multiple languages. It handles XLIFF export, translation memory integration, language copies, and translation status tracking.

Translation workflow steps:
1. Prepare source content: Ensure all topics are finalized and version-labeled.
2. Create language copies: Set up target language folders in the DAM (e.g., /content/dam/guides/fr, /content/dam/guides/de).
3. Create translation project: From Map Dashboard > Translation tab, create a new translation project.
4. Send for translation: Export content as XLIFF packages or send to a connected Translation Management System (TMS).
5. Receive translations: Import translated XLIFF files or receive from TMS.
6. Review translations: Review translated content in the Web Editor.
7. Publish translated output: Generate output from the translated map.

Translation features:
- XLIFF Export/Import: Industry-standard translation exchange format.
- Translation Memory (TM): Leverages previously translated segments to reduce cost.
- Out-of-Sync Detection: Identifies translated topics that are out of date with the source.
- Incremental Translation: Only send changed content for re-translation.
- Language Copies: AEM's language copy mechanism for managing translated content trees.
- Translation Providers: Integration with SDL, Lionbridge, and other TMS providers.

Translation status tracking:
| Status | Meaning |
|--------|--------|
| Not translated | No translation exists |
| In progress | Sent to TMS, awaiting return |
| Translated | Translation received but not reviewed |
| Approved | Translation reviewed and approved |
| Out of sync | Source has changed since last translation |

Best practices:
- Use keys for product names and UI strings — keys can resolve to different values per language.
- Use conrefs for reusable content — translate once, reuse everywhere.
- Apply version labels to source content before translation — ensures translators work from stable content.
- Use baselines for translation projects — locks the source versions being translated.

Common mistakes:
- Translating inline code and element names — use <translate="no"> or DITAVAL to exclude technical content.
- Not setting up language copies correctly — translated files must mirror the source folder structure.
- Sending draft content for translation — costly revisions when source changes.

In AEM Guides:
- Map Dashboard: Translation tab shows translation status for all topics.
- Web Editor: Translated topics can be opened and reviewed side-by-side with source.
- Bulk Translation: Send entire maps for translation with a single action.

AEM Guides Map Dashboard — the central hub for managing DITA map operations.

What is the Map Dashboard?
The Map Dashboard is the primary management interface for a DITA map in AEM Guides. It provides tabs for output generation, baseline management, reports, translation, and review — all in one place.

Dashboard tabs:
1. Output Presets: Create, configure, and generate output presets (PDF, Sites, HTML5, JSON).
2. Output: View generation status, download generated output, view logs.
3. Baseline: Create and manage baselines (version snapshots).
4. Translation: Manage translation projects, view translation status.
5. Reports: Content reports — broken links, missing topics, condition coverage.
6. Topics: List all topics in the map with version info and status.

Accessing the Map Dashboard:
- From Repository Panel: Right-click a .ditamap > Open Map Dashboard.
- From Web Editor: Map menu > Map Dashboard.
- Direct URL: /editor.html/content/dam/path/to/map.ditamap (Map Dashboard view).

Key operations:
- Generate Output: Select a preset and click Generate. Monitor progress in the Output tab.
- Quick Generate: Generate output directly without opening the full dashboard.
- Bulk Publish: Generate multiple presets simultaneously.
- Download Output: Download generated PDF, HTML, or other output files.
- View Logs: Troubleshoot generation errors by viewing build logs.
- Create Baseline: Snapshot current topic versions for stable publishing.
- Run Reports: Check for broken conrefs, missing images, orphan topics.

Output generation workflow:
1. Open Map Dashboard for your DITA map.
2. Go to Output Presets tab.
3. Select (or create) a preset.
4. Optionally set a baseline and DITAVAL.
5. Click Generate.
6. Monitor progress in the Output tab.
7. Download or preview the generated output.

Common mistakes:
- Generating output without checking the Reports tab — broken links and missing topics will cause errors.
- Not monitoring the Output tab for generation status — failures may go unnoticed.
- Forgetting to select a baseline for production output.

In AEM Guides:
- The Map Dashboard is the recommended interface for all publishing operations.
- It consolidates all map-level operations that were previously spread across multiple AEM interfaces.
- Accessible to users with Editor or Administrator roles.

Comparison: <frontmatter> vs <backmatter> — what goes at the front vs back of a bookmap.

| Aspect | frontmatter | backmatter |
|--------|-------------|------------|
| Position in bookmap | Before chapters | After chapters |
| Purpose | Introductory/preliminary material | Supplementary/reference material |
| Common children | <booklists> (toc, figurelist, tablelist), <preface>, <notices>, <dedication> | <appendix>, <booklists> (indexlist, glossarylist), <colophon> |

Typical bookmap structure:
```xml
<bookmap>
  <booktitle><mainbooktitle>User Guide</mainbooktitle></booktitle>
  <bookmeta>...</bookmeta>

  <frontmatter>
    <booklists>
      <toc/>                    <!-- Table of Contents -->
      <figurelist/>             <!-- List of Figures -->
      <tablelist/>              <!-- List of Tables -->
    </booklists>
    <notices href="copyright.dita"/>  <!-- Legal notices -->
    <preface href="preface.dita"/>    <!-- Author's preface -->
  </frontmatter>

  <chapter href="ch01.dita"/>        <!-- Main content -->
  <chapter href="ch02.dita"/>

  <backmatter>
    <appendix href="appendix-a.dita"/>  <!-- Supplementary content -->
    <appendix href="appendix-b.dita"/>
    <booklists>
      <glossarylist href="glossary.ditamap"/>  <!-- Glossary -->
      <indexlist/>                              <!-- Generated Index -->
    </booklists>
    <colophon href="colophon.dita"/>            <!-- Publication info -->
  </backmatter>
</bookmap>
```

What goes where:
| Content | Placement | Element |
|---------|-----------|--------|
| Table of Contents | frontmatter | <toc/> in <booklists> |
| List of Figures | frontmatter | <figurelist/> in <booklists> |
| List of Tables | frontmatter | <tablelist/> in <booklists> |
| Preface | frontmatter | <preface> |
| Legal Notices | frontmatter | <notices> |
| Dedication | frontmatter | <dedication> |
| Appendixes | backmatter | <appendix> |
| Glossary | backmatter | <glossarylist> in <booklists> |
| Index | backmatter | <indexlist/> in <booklists> |
| Colophon | backmatter | <colophon> |

In AEM Guides:
- PDF Output (Native): Frontmatter and backmatter each use dedicated page layouts in the PDF template.
- The template editor lets you design title pages, TOC pages, index pages, and appendix pages separately.

text_content:
<colspec> defines properties for a single column in a CALS <table>. Place one <colspec> per column inside <tgroup>, before <thead> and <tbody>.

Key attributes:
- @colname — column identifier used for spanning (namest/nameend on <entry>)
- @colnum — column number (1-based), auto-assigned if omitted
- @colwidth — column width: proportional ("1*", "2*"), fixed ("3cm", "100px"), or mixed
- @align — text alignment for this column: left, right, center, justify
- @colsep — column separator line (0=off, 1=on)
- @rowsep — row separator for this column (0=off, 1=on)

Example — proportional widths:
```xml
<tgroup cols="3">
  <colspec colname="name" colnum="1" colwidth="2*"/>    <!-- 2/6 of table width -->
  <colspec colname="type" colnum="2" colwidth="1*"/>    <!-- 1/6 of table width -->
  <colspec colname="desc" colnum="3" colwidth="3*"/>    <!-- 3/6 of table width -->
  <!-- ... -->
</tgroup>
```

Example — fixed widths:
```xml
<colspec colname="icon" colwidth="50px" align="center"/>
<colspec colname="label" colwidth="200px" align="left"/>
```

Spanning columns: Reference @colname in <entry> via @namest (start column) and @nameend (end column):
```xml
<entry namest="name" nameend="desc">This spans all 3 columns</entry>
```

In AEM Guides: Column widths are editable in the visual table editor. Dragging column borders updates @colwidth values. PDF output respects proportional and fixed widths.
usage_contexts: Controlling column widths in formal CALS tables
Naming columns for cell spanning with namest/nameend
Setting per-column alignment (e.g., right-align numeric columns)
Controlling column separator visibility
common_mistakes: Not providing @colname when using spanning — <entry namest/nameend> requires named columns
Using percentage widths ('50%') instead of proportional ('1*') — CALS uses star notation
Placing <colspec> after <thead> — colspec MUST appear before thead and tbody
Mismatching number of <colspec> elements with @cols on <tgroup>
correct_examples: <colspec colname="c1" colnum="1" colwidth="1*"/>
<colspec colname="desc" colwidth="3*" align="left" rowsep="1"/>

<row> is a single table row inside <thead> or <tbody>. Each row contains one or more <entry> elements.

Example:
```xml
<tbody>
  <row>
    <entry>Cell 1</entry>
    <entry>Cell 2</entry>
    <entry>Cell 3</entry>
  </row>
  <row rowsep="0">
    <entry>No bottom border</entry>
    <entry>On this row</entry>
    <entry>Because rowsep=0</entry>
  </row>
</tbody>
```

Key attributes:
- @rowsep — row separator line after this row (0=off, 1=on), overrides tgroup default
- @valign — vertical alignment of all entries in this row: top, middle, bottom

Row spanning: Use @morerows on an <entry> to span multiple rows:
```xml
<tbody>
  <row>
    <entry morerows="1">Spans 2 rows</entry>
    <entry>Row 1, Col 2</entry>
  </row>
  <row>
    <!-- first entry is spanned from above -->
    <entry>Row 2, Col 2</entry>
  </row>
</tbody>
```

Header rows inside <thead> for column headings

Having fewer <entry> elements than @cols on <tgroup> without spanning to account for it

<row valign="top" rowsep="0"><entry>Aligned top</entry><entry>No bottom border</entry></row>

Forgetting to skip entries in subsequent rows when using @morerows
correct_examples: <entry namest="c1" nameend="c3" align="center">Spans 3 columns</entry>
<entry morerows="1" valign="middle">Spans 2 rows</entry>
<entry dir="rtl" xml:lang="he">עברית</entry>

text_content:
@dir is a DITA attribute that controls bidirectional text rendering. It instructs processors how to handle text direction for languages like Arabic, Hebrew, Farsi, and Urdu. Values: - ltr — left-to-right (default for Latin, CJK languages). Overrides direction of neutral characters (spaces, punctuation) only. - rtl — right-to-left (for Arabic, Hebrew, Farsi, Urdu). Overrides neutral characters only. - lro — left-to-right override. Forces ALL characters left-to-right, overriding the Unicode Bidirectional Algorithm. - rlo — right-to-left override. Forces ALL characters right-to-left, overriding the Unicode Bidirectional Algorithm. IMPORTANT: Use lro/rlo overrides sparingly. Only use them when ltr/rtl cannot achieve the desired effect. The standard ltr/rtl values work with the Unicode Bidirectional Algorithm and only affect neutral characters. Where to set @dir: - Set on the highest-level element possible (root topic, map, or table) - The value inherits to all child elements - Override on specific elements when mixing directions Example — RTL topic: ```xml دليل المستخدم

مرحبا بكم في دليل AEM Guides.

Code samples remain left-to-right: dita --format=pdf

 ``` Example — mixed-direction table: ```xml Englishعربي Installation Guide دليل التثبيت
``` Critical for tables: @dir on determines column rendering order. RTL tables render the first column on the right side. Definition lists also reverse label/value order. Coordination with @xml:lang: Always set BOTH @dir and @xml:lang together. @xml:lang tells processors the language; @dir tells them the rendering direction. They are complementary. In AEM Guides: PDF output respects @dir for page layout mirroring, table column order, and text alignment. Sites output maps @dir to HTML dir attribute.
usage_contexts: Arabic, Hebrew, Farsi, Urdu content — set dir='rtl' on root element
Mixed-direction documents — set on root, override on specific elements
Tables with RTL content — controls column rendering order
Inline direction changes — override for code samples or quoted text in opposite direction
common_mistakes: Setting @xml:lang without @dir — language alone does not guarantee correct rendering direction
Using lro/rlo when ltr/rtl would suffice — overrides bypass the Unicode Bidirectional Algorithm
Not setting @dir on the root element — processors may assume LTR even for RTL languages
Forgetting @dir on tables — columns render in wrong order for RTL content
correct_examples:
عنوان

محتوى
English text with עברית inline.
عمود أولعمود ثاني

text_content:
@xml:lang specifies the language (and optional locale) of element content. It affects processing, translation workflows, spell-checking, hyphenation, and sort order. Format: lowercase language code, optional hyphen + locale. Per RFC 5646 / BCP 47. Common values: - en — English - en-US — English (United States) - en-GB — English (United Kingdom) - fr — French - fr-CA — French (Canada) - de — German - ja — Japanese - zh-CN — Chinese (Simplified) - zh-TW — Chinese (Traditional) - ar — Arabic - he — Hebrew - ko — Korean - pt-BR — Portuguese (Brazil) Inheritance rules: - Values cascade from parent to child elements - Child elements can override with their own @xml:lang - Does NOT cascade from maps to topics (each topic should set its own) - Does NOT cascade across separate map files Where to set: - Root element of every topic: - Root element of every map: - Override on specific elements for mixed-language content Example — single-language topic: ```xml Installation Guide

Follow these steps to install AEM Guides.

 ``` Example — multilingual topic: ```xml Multilingual Glossary

English definition: A map is a collection of topic references.

Définition française : Un map est un recueil de références de sujets.

Deutsche Definition: Eine Map ist eine Sammlung von Themenreferenzen.

 ``` Example — map with mixed languages: ```xml Product Documentation ``` Processor behavior: - DITA processors MUST use @xml:lang to determine document language - Affects: spell-checking, hyphenation, sort order, date/number formatting - If absent, processors assume a default (which may be inappropriate) Coordination with @dir: For RTL languages (Arabic, Hebrew, Farsi, Urdu), always set BOTH @xml:lang and @dir. Example: xml:lang="ar" dir="rtl". In AEM Guides: @xml:lang drives translation workflows, language-specific output formatting, and the Web Editor's language-aware features. Set it on topic root elements for proper translation management.
usage_contexts: Set on root element of every topic and map for proper language identification
Override on specific elements for mixed-language content (e.g., foreign terms)
Coordinate with @dir for RTL languages
Translation workflow — processors use @xml:lang to identify translation targets
common_mistakes: Not setting @xml:lang on topic root — processors may assume wrong language
Expecting @xml:lang to cascade from map to topics — it does NOT; set on each topic independently
Setting @xml:lang without @dir for RTL languages — both are needed for correct rendering
Using uppercase language codes ('EN-US') — use lowercase language, uppercase locale ('en-US')
correct_examples:
Guide

Content
<map xml:lang="en-US"><title>Guide</title><topicref href="ch1.dita"/></map>
Ceci est un paragraphe en français.

عنوان

محتوى عربي

text_content:
Comparison: (CALS) vs — choosing the right table type in DITA. | Feature |
(CALS) | | |---------|---------------|---------------| | Markup complexity | Complex (tgroup, colspec, row, entry) | Simple (sthead, strow, stentry) | | Column spanning | Yes (namest/nameend) | No | | Row spanning | Yes (@morerows) | No | | Column widths | Yes (@colwidth on colspec: "1*", "2*") | Yes (@relcolwidth: "1* 2*") | | Multiple header rows | Yes (multiple rows in thead) | No (single sthead only) | | Title | Yes ( inside table) | No | | @frame control | Yes (all, top, bottom, sides, none) | No | | Column/row separators | Yes (@colsep, @rowsep) | No | | @dir support | Yes | Yes | | @xml:lang support | Yes | Yes | | Per-column alignment | Yes (align on colspec) | No | | Multiple tgroups | Yes (different column sections) | No | Use <table> when: - You need cell spanning (horizontal or vertical) - You need precise column width control with colspec - You need column-level alignment or separator control - You need a table title - You have complex multi-section table layouts Use <simpletable> when: - Simple key-value pairs or grids - No spanning required - Fewer columns (2-4 typically) - You want minimal markup for maintainability - Quick reference tables Example — same data, both formats: <table> version: ```xml <table id="params"> <title>Configuration Parameters ParameterDescriptiontimeoutConnection timeout in secondsretriesNumber of retry attempts
``` version: ```xml ParameterDescription timeoutConnection timeout in seconds retriesNumber of retry attempts ``` Common mistakes: - Using / in — it uses / - Using / in — it uses / - Adding to — simpletable has no tgroup - Using
when would suffice — adds unnecessary complexity In AEM Guides: Both types are available from Insert > Table in the Web Editor. The visual editor supports both, but simpletable has a simpler editing experience.

specifies an alternative sort phrase for an element when the default sort phrase (typically derived from ) is inadequate. Essential for glossary entries in non-Latin scripts, numeric titles, and special characters. <sort-as> specializes from <data>, so it can appear wherever <data> is permitted. Where to place <sort-as>: - Inside <title>, <searchtitle>, or <navtitle> — overrides title-based sorting - Inside <glossterm> — controls glossary entry order - Inside topic <prolog> — fallback when not in title - Inside <dt> — controls definition list item order - Inside <indexterm> — equivalent to <index-sort-as> Key attributes: - @value — specifies sort text; takes precedence over element content - @name — metadata identifier; defaults to "sort-as" Processing rules: 1. Processors MUST construct the effective sort phrase by PREPENDING the <sort-as> content to the base sort phrase 2. <sort-as> within titles overrides prolog-level instances 3. Only direct-child <sort-as> elements are processed 4. First direct-child instance (document order) takes precedence if multiple exist Example — numeric title sorting: ```xml <topic id="support-24h"> <title>24 Hour Support Hotline<sort-as value="twenty-four hour"/>

Contact support anytime.

``` Effective sort phrase: "twenty-four hour24 Hour Support Hotline" — sorts under T, not under 2. Example — CJK glossary entry with transliteration: ```xml 大大

Mandarin informal term meaning 'big'.

 ``` Sorts by Mandarin transliteration "dada" instead of Unicode code point. Example — using @value attribute: ```xml
MacOS
``` Example — keyref-based sort phrase: ```xml <sort-as><keyword keyref="sort-phrase-key"/></sort-as>Original Title ``` Related element: — specifically for index term sorting. In AEM Guides: Sort order affects glossary output, index generation, and TOC ordering. Set in glossterm for non-Latin glossaries to ensure correct alphabetical output in PDF and Sites.
usage_contexts: Glossary entries in non-Latin scripts (CJK, Arabic, Hebrew) — provide transliteration for sorting
Numeric titles ('24 Hour Support') — provide spelled-out form for alphabetical order
Special character titles — control sort position
Index terms with non-obvious sort order
common_mistakes: Placing <sort-as> outside of <title>/<glossterm>/<prolog>/<dt> — it won't be processed
Expecting <sort-as> to REPLACE the sort phrase — it PREPENDS to the base phrase
Using multiple <sort-as> in the same parent — only the first is used
Forgetting <sort-as> on CJK glossary entries — they sort by Unicode code point without it
correct_examples: <title>24 Hour Support<sort-as value="twenty-four hour"/></title>
<glossterm><sort-as value="pinyin-reading"/>中文术语</glossterm>
<dt><sort-as value="mac"/>MacOS</dt>

text_content:
<imagemap> creates clickable regions (hotspots) over an image, enabling navigation to different topics or resources. It is a utilities-domain specialization of <fig>.

Structure: <imagemap> → <image> (required first child) → <area> (one or more clickable regions)
Each <area> contains: <shape> → <coords> → <xref>

Shape types (same as HTML 4.1 image maps):
- rect — rectangle: coords="left,top,right,bottom"
- circle — circle: coords="centerX,centerY,radius"
- poly — polygon: coords="x1,y1,x2,y2,...xN,yN"
- default — entire image (no coords needed)

Example:
```xml
<imagemap>
  <image href="images/workflow-diagram.png" placement="break">
    <alt>Documentation workflow diagram</alt>
  </image>
  <!-- Rectangle hotspot linking to authoring topic -->
  <area>
    <shape>rect</shape>
    <coords>10,20,150,80</coords>
    <xref href="authoring.dita">Authoring phase</xref>
  </area>
  <!-- Circle hotspot linking to review topic -->
  <area>
    <shape>circle</shape>
    <coords>250,50,40</coords>
    <xref href="review.dita">Review phase</xref>
  </area>
  <!-- Polygon hotspot -->
  <area>
    <shape>poly</shape>
    <coords>300,10,350,50,300,90,250,50</coords>
    <xref href="publish.dita">Publishing phase</xref>
  </area>
  <!-- Default: clicking anywhere else -->
  <area>
    <shape>default</shape>
    <coords></coords>
    <xref href="overview.dita">Workflow overview</xref>
  </area>
</imagemap>
```

Key attributes on <imagemap>:
- @id — for conref/reuse
- @dir — text direction for area labels
- @xml:lang — language of area labels

Output rendering:
- HTML/Sites: Renders as standard HTML image map (<map> + <area> tags)
- PDF: Can render as clickable regions or as a table-based navigation listing
- Accessible: <xref> content provides alternative text for each region

Coordinate system: Pixel-based from top-left corner of the image. Coordinates follow HTML 4.1 specification.

In AEM Guides: Imagemaps are supported in Web Editor output. For PDF, the clickable regions are rendered as hyperlinked areas. Ensure image dimensions match coordinate values for accurate hotspot placement.
usage_contexts: Interactive workflow diagrams with clickable phases
Clickable product images linking to feature documentation
Architecture diagrams with hotspots linking to component topics
Geographic maps linking regions to locale-specific content
common_mistakes: Forgetting <image> as the first child — <imagemap> requires <image> before any <area>
Using HTML-style <map>/<area> tags — DITA uses <imagemap> with <shape>/<coords>/<xref>
Mismatching coordinates with actual image dimensions — hotspots will be misplaced
Omitting <alt> on the <image> — required for accessibility
correct_examples: <imagemap><image href="diagram.png"><alt>System diagram</alt></image><area><shape>rect</shape><coords>0,0,100,50</coords><xref href="component.dita">Component A</xref></area></imagemap>
<imagemap><image href="map.png"><alt>Region map</alt></image><area><shape>circle</shape><coords>150,150,30</coords><xref href="region-north.dita">North</xref></area></imagemap>

text_content:
DITA module compatibility determines whether content can be safely copied or referenced between documents that use different vocabulary and constraint modules.

The @domains attribute on the root element declares which modules a document requires. Processors use this to assess compatibility during content reuse operations (conref, copy-paste, content reference).

Compatibility rule:
A copy/conref operation is SAFE when the source document requires a SUBSET of the modules required by the target document. If the source needs modules not present in the target, the operation is unsafe.

Example:
```
Source document @domains: (topic) (hi-d) (ut-d)
Target document @domains: (topic) (hi-d) (ut-d) (pr-d) (sw-d)
→ SAFE: source is a subset of target

Source document @domains: (topic) (hi-d) (ut-d) (custom-d)
Target document @domains: (topic) (hi-d) (ut-d)
→ UNSAFE: source has (custom-d) which target lacks
```

Processor behavior:
1. Examine @domains on both source and target documents
2. If source requires modules not in target → warn or block the operation
3. If constraints are incompatible → attempt generalization as fallback
4. Processors SHOULD issue a warning when allowing unsafe copies

Constraint types:
- Weak constraints: May be relaxed during compatibility checking
- Strong constraints: Must be satisfied — cannot be relaxed

Generalization fallback:
When an unsafe copy is detected, processors may generalize specialized elements back to their base types to achieve compatibility. For example, a <step-xref> (task domain) could be generalized to <xref> (base) if the target document lacks the task domain.

Best practices:
- Always include @domains on root elements of topics and maps
- When creating specialized content, document which modules are required
- Test conref operations across documents with different module sets
- Use the same module set across a documentation suite for seamless reuse

Common mistakes:
- Omitting @domains — processors cannot check compatibility
- Using specialized elements from domains not declared in @domains
- Conrefing content from a heavily-specialized source into a base-only target

In AEM Guides: The Web Editor handles module compatibility automatically during conref insertion. If you encounter conref errors, check that both source and target documents declare compatible @domains.
usage_contexts: Content reuse via conref across documents with different specializations
Copy-paste between topics using different domain modules
Ensuring consistent module declarations across a documentation suite
Troubleshooting conref failures due to module mismatches
common_mistakes: Omitting @domains attribute on root elements — processors cannot verify compatibility
Conrefing specialized elements into documents that lack the required domain
Assuming all DITA documents are compatible — module sets can differ
Ignoring processor warnings about unsafe copy operations

text_content:
Display attributes control how elements are visually rendered in output. They apply primarily to block-level elements like , , , 
, , and .

Three display attributes:

1. @expanse — controls horizontal placement/width:
   - page — element stretches to page margins
   - column — aligns with current column margin
   - textline — aligns with text line margin (respects indentation)
   - spread — renders across multi-page spreads
   - -dita-use-conref-target — inherits from conref source
   NOTE: For
, use @pgwide instead (1=page width, 0=column width). 2. @frame — specifies border/lines around the element: - all — full box border - top — line before element - bottom — line after element - topbot — lines before and after - sides — lines on left and right - none — no border - -dita-use-conref-target — inherits from conref source 3. @scale — resizes font as percentage of normal text size: Values: 50, 60, 70, 80, 90, 100, 110, 120, 140, 160, 180, 200 NOTE: Images inside scaled elements use their OWN scale property, not the parent's. Example — scaled table with frame: ```xml
API Endpoints (compact view) MethodPathDescriptionGET/api/topicsList all topicsPOST/api/topicsCreate a topic
``` Example — figure with frame and expanse: ```xml Architecture Diagram ``` Example — codeblock with frame: ```xml <topic id="example"> <title>Example</title> </topic> ``` Elements that accept display attributes: , , , 
, , , , .

In AEM Guides:
- PDF output: @frame renders as visible borders, @scale adjusts font size, @pgwide=1 makes tables span the full page.
- Sites output: @frame maps to CSS border classes, @scale maps to font-size.
- Web Editor: Frame and scale are editable in element properties panel.
usage_contexts: Tables: @frame='all' for bordered tables, @pgwide=1 for full-page-width tables
Code blocks: @frame='all' with @scale='90' for compact code listings
Figures: @frame='topbot' for horizontal rules above/below diagrams
Scaled content: @scale='80' to fit large tables or code into available space
common_mistakes: Using @expanse on <table> — use @pgwide instead (0 or 1)
Expecting @scale to resize images — images use their own @scale or @width/@height
Using arbitrary scale values (e.g., 75) — only the predefined set (50,60,70,...200) is valid
Applying display attributes to inline elements — they only work on block-level elements
correct_examples: AB
<fig frame="topbot" expanse="page"><title>Diagram</title><image href="img.png"/></fig>
<codeblock frame="all" scale="90">code here</codeblock>

text_content:
@expiry and @golive are DITA date metadata attributes used in metadata to track content lifecycle dates. Two date attributes: 1. @golive — the publication or general availability date. When the content is intended to go live / be published. Format: YYYY-MM-DD (e.g., 2024-03-15) 2. @expiry — the expiration or retirement date. When the content should be reviewed, refreshed, or retired. Format: YYYY-MM-DD (e.g., 2025-03-15) These attributes appear on metadata elements inside , specifically on and elements within . Example — setting lifecycle dates: ```xml Release Notes — Version 4.3

This document describes features in version 4.3.

 ``` The (critical dates) element contains: - — original creation date - @date (required) — creation date - @golive (optional) — intended publication date - @expiry (optional) — expiration date - — revision dates (can have multiple) - @modified (required) — last modification date - @golive (optional) — publication date for this revision - @expiry (optional) — expiration for this revision Example — multiple revisions: ```xml ``` Processing uses: - Content management systems can filter or flag expired content based on @expiry - Publishing workflows can schedule content release based on @golive - Reports can identify stale content approaching @expiry dates - Translation management can prioritize content by @golive urgency Date format: Always YYYY-MM-DD (ISO 8601). Year is 4 digits, month 01-12, day 01-31. In AEM Guides: The Web Editor's Properties panel shows critdates fields. @expiry dates can drive content review workflows. Reports can filter by expiration to identify content needing refresh.
usage_contexts: Content lifecycle management — track when content goes live and expires
Release notes and versioned docs — set @expiry to flag for review when version is EOL
Translation workflows — @golive helps prioritize urgent content
Compliance documentation — @expiry triggers mandatory review cycles
common_mistakes: Using non-ISO date formats (MM/DD/YYYY or DD-MM-YYYY) — must be YYYY-MM-DD
Putting @golive/@expiry on <topic> or <map> directly — they belong on <created>/<revised> inside <critdates>
Forgetting @date on <created> or @modified on <revised> — these are required attributes
Not using <critdates> wrapper — <created> and <revised> must be inside <critdates> inside <prolog>
correct_examples: <prolog><critdates><created date="2024-01-15" golive="2024-02-01" expiry="2025-02-01"/></critdates></prolog>
<critdates><created date="2024-01-01"/><revised modified="2024-06-15" golive="2024-07-01" expiry="2025-07-01"/></critdates>

text_content:
Link relationship attributes control how DITA elements reference other resources. They appear on linking elements: <xref>, <link>, <topicref>, <navref>, <image>, <object>, and other elements that point to resources.

Four link relationship attributes:

1. @href — the reference to a target resource.
   Formats:
   - DITA topic: href="install.dita" or href="install.dita#topicid"
   - DITA topic element: href="install.dita#topicid/elementid"
   - DITA map: href="guide.ditamap"
   - External URL: href="https://example.com/docs"
   - Non-DITA resource: href="readme.pdf"

2. @format — identifies the format of the referenced resource.
   Common values:
   - dita — DITA topic (default for .dita files)
   - ditamap — DITA map
   - html — HTML page
   - pdf — PDF document
   - txt — plain text
   Cascading: @format inherits from ancestor elements in maps and related-links.

3. @scope — indicates the relationship closeness between current document and target.
   Values:
   - local — target is part of the same deliverable/documentation set (default for DITA refs)
   - peer — target is DITA content but in a different deliverable (linked at build time)
   - external — target is outside the documentation set (URL opens in new window)
   - -dita-use-conref-target — inherits from conref source

4. @type — describes the target type for cross-references and navigation.
   Common values: topic, concept, task, reference, fig, table, section, fn
   Processors use this for link text generation and validation.

Example — xref to another topic:
```xml
<xref href="install.dita" type="task">Installation steps</xref>
```

Example — topicref in a map:
```xml
<topicref href="chapter1.dita" format="dita" scope="local" type="concept"/>
```

Example — external link:
```xml
<xref href="https://docs.adobe.com/aem-guides" format="html" scope="external">
  AEM Guides Documentation
</xref>
```

Example — PDF reference:
```xml
<xref href="whitepaper.pdf" format="pdf" scope="local">Download whitepaper</xref>
```

Example — peer reference (different deliverable):
```xml
<topicref href="../admin-guide/security.dita" scope="peer" format="dita"/>
```

Example — image with href:
```xml
<image href="images/screenshot.png" placement="break">
  <alt>Web Editor screenshot</alt>
</image>
```

Critical rules:
- External URLs MUST have scope="external" — otherwise processors treat them as local files
- Non-DITA resources MUST have @format set — processors assume "dita" by default
- @format cascades in maps: set it on a parent <topicref> and children inherit it
- @href on <topicref> uses the file path (no #fragment for topic-level refs)

In AEM Guides:
- Web Editor: Insert Cross Reference dialog sets @href, @format, @scope automatically
- Map Editor: Drag-and-drop sets @href and @format based on file type
- Publishing: @scope='external' links open in new tabs in Sites output; peer refs are resolved across deliverables
- Broken link detection: The Web Editor validates @href targets and flags missing references
usage_contexts: Cross-references (<xref>) to other DITA topics or external resources
Topic references (<topicref>) in maps pointing to topics
External links — always set scope='external' and format='html'
Non-DITA resources (PDF, images) — always set @format explicitly
Peer references to content in other deliverables
common_mistakes: Omitting scope='external' on URLs — processor treats them as local file paths
Not setting @format on non-DITA resources — processor assumes 'dita' and fails
Using @href with fragment (#) on <topicref> for topic-level refs — fragments are for element-level refs only
Forgetting format='ditamap' when referencing a .ditamap file from another map
correct_examples: <xref href="install.dita" type="task">Installation</xref>
<xref href="https://example.com" format="html" scope="external">External docs</xref>
<topicref href="chapter1.dita" format="dita" scope="local"/>
<xref href="guide.pdf" format="pdf" scope="local">PDF Guide</xref>

text_content:
DITA common attributes are shared across most elements. They fall into several groups. Universal attributes (available on nearly every DITA element): - @id — unique identifier within the file. Required on topic root elements, optional on others. - @conref — direct content reference: conref="file.dita#topicid/elementid" - @conkeyref — indirect content reference via key: conkeyref="keyname/elementid" - @conrefend — end of a conref range - @conaction — conref push action: pushafter, pushbefore, pushreplace, mark - @class — specialization hierarchy (set by DTD/schema, never by authors) - @outputclass — semantic styling hook for output processing (author-defined) - @translate — yes|no — marks content for translation - @xml:lang — language and locale (e.g., en-US, fr, ar) - @dir — text direction: ltr, rtl, lro, rlo Conditional processing (filtering/flagging) attributes: - @props — general-purpose conditional attribute - @base — general-purpose metadata attribute - @platform — target platform (e.g., windows, linux, macos) - @product — product name (e.g., aem-guides, aem-sites) - @audience — target audience (e.g., administrator, author, developer) - @otherprops — additional conditional values - @rev — revision identifier for change tracking - @importance — optional, required, deprecated, default - @status — new, changed, deleted, unchanged Other common attributes: - @keyref — redirectable reference based on a key defined in a map - @compact — controls vertical spacing in lists: yes (tight) or no (normal) - @xml:space — preserve whitespace (fixed value "preserve" on preformatted elements) Example — element with multiple common attributes: ```xml

Placeholder for conref content

``` Example — conditional processing: ```xml

Install using the MSI installer.

Install using apt-get or yum.

Configure SSL before going live.

``` Example — outputclass for styling: ```xml ...

Always use keyrefs for maintainability.

 ``` In AEM Guides: - Conditional attributes drive DITAVAL filtering in output presets - @outputclass maps to CSS classes in Sites and PDF output - @translate controls which content is sent to translation workflows - @id is required for conref targets and is auto-generated by the Web Editor
usage_contexts: Conditional processing — @platform, @product, @audience for DITAVAL filtering
Content reuse — @conref, @conkeyref for pulling content from other topics
Localization — @xml:lang, @dir, @translate for multilingual content
Styling — @outputclass for custom CSS classes in output
common_mistakes: Using @id without ensuring uniqueness within the file — IDs must be unique per file
Forgetting @translate='no' on code samples or product names — they get sent to translation
Using @class directly — it's set by DTD/schema, never authored manually
Putting conditional attributes on block elements but not matching DITAVAL filter rules
correct_examples: Important safety warning.
<section platform="windows" audience="administrator">Windows-specific admin steps.</section>
<codeblock outputclass="language-java" translate="no">System.out.println("Hello");</codeblock>

text_content:
Simpletable-specific attributes control column widths and row headings for <simpletable> and its specializations (<choicetable>, <properties>).

Three simpletable attributes:

1. @relcolwidth — proportional column widths.
   Format: space-separated list of numbers followed by asterisks.
   The proportions are relative — the actual width depends on available space.
   Examples:
   - relcolwidth="1* 2* 3*" → columns at 16.7%, 33.3%, 50%
   - relcolwidth="90* 150*" → columns at 37.5%, 62.5%
   - relcolwidth="1* 1* 1*" → equal-width columns

2. @keycol — identifies which column serves as the row heading.
   Value: column number (1-based). When set, that column's cells are rendered as headers (bold, <th> in HTML).
   Default: no key column.
   Examples:
   - keycol="1" → first column is the row header
   - keycol="0" or omitted → no row headers

3. @refcols — reserved for future use (do not use).

Example — simpletable with proportional widths and key column:
```xml
<simpletable relcolwidth="1* 3*" keycol="1">
  <sthead>
    <stentry>Parameter</stentry>
    <stentry>Description</stentry>
  </sthead>
  <strow>
    <stentry>timeout</stentry>  <!-- rendered as row header because keycol=1 -->
    <stentry>Connection timeout in seconds. Default: 30.</stentry>
  </strow>
  <strow>
    <stentry>retries</stentry>  <!-- rendered as row header -->
    <stentry>Number of retry attempts before failure. Default: 3.</stentry>
  </strow>
</simpletable>
```

Example — equal columns, no key column:
```xml
<simpletable relcolwidth="1* 1* 1*">
  <sthead><stentry>A</stentry><stentry>B</stentry><stentry>C</stentry></sthead>
  <strow><stentry>1</stentry><stentry>2</stentry><stentry>3</stentry></strow>
</simpletable>
```

IMPORTANT: These attributes are for <simpletable> ONLY. CALS <table> uses <colspec @colwidth> for column widths and <thead> for headers.

In AEM Guides: The Web Editor renders @keycol cells as bold headers. @relcolwidth is respected in both PDF and Sites output. Drag column borders in the visual editor to adjust proportions.
usage_contexts: Key-value parameter tables — keycol=1 makes the parameter name a row header
Proportional column sizing — relcolwidth='1* 3*' for narrow label + wide description
Properties tables — @keycol highlights the property name column
common_mistakes: Using @colwidth on <simpletable> — that's for CALS <table> <colspec>; use @relcolwidth instead
Using percentage values ('33%') in @relcolwidth — use star notation ('1*')
Setting @keycol on CALS <table> — it only works on <simpletable>
Mismatching @relcolwidth count with number of columns — must match <stentry> count
correct_examples: <simpletable relcolwidth="1* 2*" keycol="1"><sthead><stentry>Key</stentry><stentry>Value</stentry></sthead><strow><stentry>Name</stentry><stentry>AEM Guides</stentry></strow></simpletable>

<simpletable relcolwidth="1* 1* 1*"><sthead><stentry>A</stentry><stentry>B</stentry><stentry>C</stentry></sthead><strow><stentry>1</stentry><stentry>2</stentry><stentry>3</stentry></strow></simpletable>

text_content:
Topicref element attributes control how topics behave within maps — navigation, collection behavior, and output inclusion.

Key attributes on <topicref>:

1. @collection-type — how children are related:
   - unordered — children are unrelated peers
   - sequence — children form an ordered sequence (prev/next links)
   - choice — children are alternatives (pick one)
   - family — children are all related to each other (generates sibling links)

2. @processing-role — whether the reference contributes to navigation:
   - normal — topic appears in TOC and output (default)
   - resource-only — topic is available for conref/keyref but does NOT appear in TOC or output

3. @locktitle — whether navtitle can be overridden by the topic's own title:
   - yes — use the navtitle from the map, ignore the topic's title
   - no — use the topic's actual title (default)

4. @linking — controls link generation:
   - targetonly — can be linked TO but doesn't generate outgoing links
   - sourceonly — generates outgoing links but can't be linked to
   - normal — both directions (default)
   - none — no link generation

5. @toc — include in table of contents:
   - yes — include in TOC (default)
   - no — exclude from TOC

6. @print — include in print/PDF output:
   - yes — include
   - no — exclude
   - printonly — include ONLY in print, exclude from other formats

7. @search — include in search index:
   - yes — include in search (default)
   - no — exclude from search

8. @chunk — control topic chunking for output:
   - to-content — combine referenced topics into one output file
