   - by-topic — split combined document into per-topic output files
   - by-document — each source document produces one output file
   - select-topic — include only the referenced topic, not nested children
   - select-branch — include the referenced topic and all nested children
   - select-document — include the entire source document

9. @copy-to — alternative output filename for this topicref instance

10. @navtitle — navigation title (deprecated in favor of <navtitle> element in <topicmeta>)

Example — sequenced chapters with resource-only conref warehouse:
```xml
<map>
  <title>Admin Guide</title>
  <topicref href="shared/conref-warehouse.dita" processing-role="resource-only"/>
  <topicref href="ch1-install.dita" collection-type="sequence">
    <topicref href="ch1a-prereqs.dita"/>
    <topicref href="ch1b-steps.dita"/>
    <topicref href="ch1c-verify.dita"/>
  </topicref>
  <topicref href="appendix.dita" toc="no" print="no"/>
</map>
```

Example — controlling linking and TOC:
```xml
<topicref href="glossary.dita" linking="targetonly" toc="yes"/>
<topicref href="legal-notice.dita" toc="no" print="printonly"/>
```

In AEM Guides:
- @processing-role='resource-only' is essential for conref warehouse topics
- @collection-type='sequence' generates Previous/Next navigation in Sites output
- @toc='no' hides topics from the left-nav TOC in Sites output
- @chunk controls how topics combine in PDF output
usage_contexts: Sequenced chapters — collection-type='sequence' for prev/next navigation
Conref warehouses — processing-role='resource-only' to exclude from output
Glossary/appendix — toc='no' or linking='targetonly' for reference-only topics
Print-only content — print='printonly' for content excluded from web output
chunk='to-content' on PARENT topicref — merges all child topics into one output page
chunk='by-topic' on PARENT topicref — splits children into separate output pages
common_mistakes: Forgetting processing-role='resource-only' on conref warehouse topics — they appear in output
Using @navtitle attribute instead of <navtitle> element — attribute is deprecated since DITA 1.2
Setting collection-type='sequence' on leaf topicrefs — it only affects children
Using @toc='no' but expecting the topic to still appear in PDF TOC — it's excluded from all TOCs
Using chunk='to-content' on a LEAF topicref with no children — has no merging effect since there are no children to merge
correct_examples: <topicref href="warehouse.dita" processing-role="resource-only"/>
<topicref href="ch1.dita" collection-type="sequence"><topicref href="ch1a.dita"/><topicref href="ch1b.dita"/></topicref>
<topicref href="glossary.dita" linking="targetonly" toc="yes"/>
<topicref href="admin-guide.dita" chunk="to-content"><topicref href="install.dita"/><topicref href="configure.dita"/><topicref href="upgrade.dita"/></topicref>

text_content:
@type describes the target of a cross-reference or the kind of note. It appears on linking elements (, , ) and on . On linking elements (, , ): Describes the type of the referenced target. Processors may generate link text based on this. Topic-level values: topic, concept, task, reference Below-topic values (for only): fig, table, li, fn, section Default: "topic" if not specified. Cascading: @type cascades from ancestor elements (e.g., set on parent topicref, children inherit). Validation: For DITA targets, @type must match the target's @class hierarchy. For non-DITA targets, validation is skipped. IMPORTANT: Only can link BELOW topic level (to fig, table, section, etc.). Other linking elements (, ) can only target whole topics. Example — xref with type: ```xml See installation steps Prerequisites table Key concepts ``` Example — topicref with type: ```xml ``` On element: Controls the note label, icon, and styling in output. Values: note (default), tip, important, caution, warning, danger, attention, remember, restriction, trouble, fastpath, notice, other Special: danger and warning reference ANSI Z535 and ISO 3864 safety standards. Example — note types: ```xml

Use keyrefs for easier maintenance.

Data loss may occur if you proceed without backup.

Electrical hazard — disconnect power before servicing.

 ``` In AEM Guides: - @type on determines the auto-generated link text (e.g., "See task: Installation") - @type on is used for topic type icons in the Map Editor - @type on controls the icon and color in both Web Editor preview and output
usage_contexts: Cross-references — set type to enable auto-generated link text
Map topicrefs — type helps processors validate target topic types
Note elements — type controls icon, label, and styling in output
Below-topic xrefs — type='fig' or type='table' for linking to specific elements
common_mistakes: Using type='task' on <xref> pointing to a <concept> — type must match target's actual type
Using type='fig' on <link> — only <xref> can target below-topic elements
Omitting type on <note> — defaults to 'note' which may not convey the intended severity
Using type='error' on <note> — not a valid value; use 'danger', 'warning', or 'caution'
correct_examples: <xref href="install.dita" type="task">Installation steps</xref>
<xref href="guide.dita#guide/fig-arch" type="fig">Architecture diagram</xref>
Back up data before proceeding.

text_content:
@conkeyref enables indirect content references by combining key resolution with conref. Instead of hard-coding a file path, you reference a key defined in a map, making content references portable across maps and deliverables. Syntax: conkeyref="keyname/elementid" - keyname — a key defined in the current map's key space - elementid — the @id of the target element within the topic resolved by the key - The slash (/) separates the key from the element ID - For topic-level conref (entire topic), use just the key name without /elementid Resolution process: 1. Processor looks up the key in the map's key space 2. Key resolves to a specific topic file (e.g., shared-content.dita) 3. Processor finds the element with the specified @id in that topic 4. Content from that element replaces the referencing element Fallback behavior: - If the key cannot be resolved AND a @conref attribute exists on the same element, the processor falls back to @conref - If the key cannot be resolved and there is NO @conref fallback, processors SHOULD issue a warning - Processors MAY warn even when falling back to @conref Example — basic conkeyref: ```xml

Placeholder for safety warning

``` Resolves to: the

element in shared/warnings.dita Example — with conref fallback: ```xml Placeholder ``` If key "shared-notes" is not defined, falls back to the direct conref path. Example — conkeyref for reusable steps: ```xml Placeholder Placeholder ``` Interaction with @conrefend: @conrefend cannot use a key. When @conkeyref resolves, the resolved file path replaces the file portion of @conrefend. conkeyref vs conref vs keyref: | Attribute | What it does | Syntax | |-----------|-------------|--------| | @conref | Direct content pull by file path | conref="file.dita#topicid/elemid" | | @keyref | Key-based link or variable | keyref="keyname" | | @conkeyref | Key-based content pull | conkeyref="keyname/elemid" | In AEM Guides: - Define keys in the root map or submap for @conkeyref to resolve - The Web Editor's Insert Conref dialog supports both @conref and @conkeyref - @conkeyref makes content references portable — changing the key definition in the map redirects all references without editing individual topics
usage_contexts: Portable conref — reference content by key so the same topic works across different maps
Shared content libraries — define keys for warning notes, legal text, common steps
Multi-product documentation — different maps define different keys resolving to product-specific content
Conref with fallback — pair with @conref for graceful degradation when key is undefined
common_mistakes: Using conkeyref="keyname" without /elementid for element-level refs — you need the slash and element ID
Forgetting to define the key in the map — conkeyref silently fails without a keydef
Writing conkeyref="keyname#topicid/elementid" — conkeyref uses slash, NOT hash (#); hash syntax is for @conref
Using conkeyref on an element whose type doesn't match the target — the referencing element type must match
correct_examples: Placeholder
<step conkeyref="common-steps/step-verify"><cmd>Placeholder</cmd></step>
<note conkeyref="shared-notes/backup-note" conref="fallback.dita#notes/backup-note" type="warning">Placeholder</note>

text_content:
Comparison: Baseline vs Version in AEM Guides — two related but distinct concepts.

| Aspect | Version | Baseline |
|--------|---------|----------|
| Scope | Single topic/file | Entire map + all referenced topics |
| What it captures | One file at one point in time | All files in a map at one point in time |
| Created by | Saving a topic (auto or manual) | Explicitly from Map Dashboard |
| Identified by | Version number (1.0, 1.1, 1.2) | Baseline name ('Release 4.3') |
| Labels | Optional labels on individual versions | Baseline can select topics BY label |
| Used for | Tracking changes to one file | Publishing a consistent set of content |
| Revert | Restore one topic to a previous state | Regenerate output from a previous snapshot |

How they work together:
```
Map: user-guide.ditamap

Topic Versions:
  install.dita:  v1.0, v1.1, v1.2 (labeled 'release-4.3'), v1.3
  config.dita:   v1.0, v1.1 (labeled 'release-4.3'), v1.2
  publish.dita:  v1.0 (labeled 'release-4.3'), v1.1

Baseline 'Release 4.3':
  install.dita  → v1.2  (selected by label 'release-4.3')
  config.dita   → v1.1  (selected by label 'release-4.3')
  publish.dita  → v1.0  (selected by label 'release-4.3')

When you generate PDF using Baseline 'Release 4.3':
  → Uses install.dita v1.2, config.dita v1.1, publish.dita v1.0
  → Even though newer versions exist, the baseline locks the versions
```

Analogy:
- Version = a photo of one person at one moment.
- Baseline = a group photo of the entire team at one moment.

Workflow:
1. Authors create versions as they edit topics (auto or manual save).
2. When content is ready for release, add version labels (e.g., 'release-4.3').
3. Create a baseline that selects all topics with that label.
4. Configure output preset to use the baseline.
5. Generate output — always reproducible from the baseline.

Common mistakes:
- Thinking a baseline 'freezes' content — it's a snapshot of version references, not a content lock. Authors can still edit topics.
- Publishing without a baseline — the latest (possibly in-progress) versions are used.
- Not labeling versions before creating baselines — label-based baselines are the most reliable method.

In AEM Guides:
- Versions: Managed per-topic in the Web Editor's Version History panel.
- Baselines: Managed per-map in the Map Dashboard's Baseline tab.
- Output Presets: Link a preset to a baseline for reproducible publishing.

<data> is a DITA element for storing metadata in the flow of content within the topic body. Despite the name, it is for metadata, not visible content. It is most useful as the basis for specializing other elements.

Within base DITA, you would most likely use the data element to store information about the source of code fragments, citations, or quotations. However, it is not a commonly used element.

The data element can be nested inside many DITA elements. Other data elements can be nested inside a data element. A data-about element can also be nested inside a data element.

Key attributes:
- name: the unique name for the piece of data
- value: the value of the piece of data
- href: a link to the resource used for the piece of data

The data-about element is used to specify the subject of the metadata stored in a data element. It allows you to associate metadata with a specific element in the topic rather than the topic as a whole.

How the metadata stored in the data element structure is used is up to the authoring and publishing tools used. It is primarily a specialization base.
parent_element: body, conbody, taskbody, refbody, section, p, li, and most body content elements
attributes: {"name": "unique name for the data", "value": "value of the data", "href": "link to resource", "datatype": "type of data"}
usage_contexts: Storing metadata about citations, quotations, or code sources inline
Base element for specialization into domain-specific metadata
Associating structured metadata with content elements
NOT for visible content - data is metadata only
common_mistakes: Using <data> for visible content - it is metadata only, not rendered in output
Confusing <data> with <ph> or <keyword> - data is not inline text
Not providing name attribute - without name, the data has no semantic meaning
Using <data> when prolog/metadata would be more appropriate for topic-level metadata
correct_examples:
<lq reftitle="Lynch and Horton (2009)">
  <data name="ref_source">
    <data name="date_retrieved" value="13 September 2009"/>
    <data name="chapter" value="9"/>
    <data name="pub_type" value="web"/>
  </data>
  The inverted pyramid style used in journalism works well on web pages...
</lq>

<codeblock>
  <data name="source" value="GitHub" href="https://github.com/example/repo"/>
  function hello() { return 'world'; }
</codeblock>

text_content:
DITA includes semantic elements with almost identical names: keyword vs kwd, var vs varname. These are NOT duplicates - they belong to different domains and serve different purposes.

DITA base elements are organized into domains:
- Programming domain: kwd (keyword in code), var (variable in code syntax)
- Software domain: varname (variable name in software context)
- User Interface domain: uicontrol, menucascade, wintitle
- Highlighting (typographic) domain: b, i, u, tt

Why similar names exist:
- keyword (base DITA): General-purpose keyword for indexing, metadata, or text. Can be used anywhere in body content. Supports keyref for variable text substitution.
- kwd (programming domain): A keyword in programming language syntax. Used inside <syntaxdiagram>, <synph>, or code-related elements. Indicates a reserved word in code.

- varname (software domain): A variable name in a software interface or system. General-purpose, used in body content to mark variable names.
- var (programming domain): A variable in programming syntax. Used inside <syntaxdiagram> or <synph>. Indicates a user-supplied value in code syntax.

When to use which:
- Use <keyword> for general keywords, product names (via keyref), and index terms
- Use <kwd> only inside programming syntax diagrams or code syntax descriptions
- Use <varname> for variable names mentioned in regular documentation text
- Use <var> only inside programming syntax diagrams for user-supplied values

The programming domain elements (kwd, var) should only be used when documenting programming language syntax. For general technical documentation, use the base elements (keyword, varname).
usage_contexts: Choosing between keyword vs kwd when marking up code-related terms
Choosing between varname vs var for variable references
Understanding DITA domain organization (programming, software, UI, highlighting)
Avoiding incorrect element choice that breaks domain specialization
common_mistakes: Using <kwd> instead of <keyword> in regular body text - kwd is for programming syntax only
Using <var> instead of <varname> outside syntax diagrams - var is for programming syntax only
Not understanding domain separation - programming domain elements are specialized, not general-purpose
Using <b>/<i> instead of semantic elements like <uicontrol>, <filepath>, <codeph>
correct_examples:
General text:

Set the MAX_RETRIES variable to 5.

Syntax diagram: SELECT column_name FROM table_name

Product name via keyref: <keyword keyref="product-name"/> is better than hardcoding
Code keyword: Use <kwd>public</kwd> only in Java syntax descriptions

text_content:
topichead is a DITAMAP-only element that creates a heading in the TOC without linking to any topic file. It requires a navtitle attribute (or child <navtitle> element) to supply the heading text. outputclass on topichead or topicref lets you attach a custom CSS class that AEM Guides Native PDF rendering maps to .toc-level-N.outputclass in layout.css.

KEY RULES:
1. topichead MUST have navtitle - without it the TOC entry is blank.
2. locktitle='yes' is the DEFAULT on topichead (unlike topicref where default is 'no'). You rarely need to set it explicitly.
3. outputclass value becomes a CSS class on the TOC entry. Use it for visual styling (color, font-weight, indent) NOT for semantic meaning.
4. Multiple outputclass values are space-separated: outputclass='red bold'.
5. topichead can nest other topichead or topicref elements.

DITAMAP EXAMPLE (4 use-cases):
  <topichead navtitle="Administration Guide" outputclass="red">
    <topicref href="admin-overview.dita"/>
  </topichead>
  <topichead navtitle="Developer Reference" outputclass="blue-italic">
    <topicref href="api-ref.dita"/>
  </topichead>
  <topichead navtitle="Deprecated Section" outputclass="hidden">
    <topicref href="legacy.dita"/>
  </topichead>
  <topichead navtitle="Parent Group">
    <topichead navtitle="Child Group" outputclass="nested-bold">
      <topicref href="child-topic.dita"/>
    </topichead>
  </topichead>

LAYOUT.CSS for AEM Guides Native PDF:
  .toc-level-1.red { color: #cc0000; font-weight: bold; }
  .toc-level-1.blue-italic { color: #0055aa; font-style: italic; }
  .toc-level-1.hidden { display: none; }
  .toc-level-2.nested-bold { font-weight: 700; text-transform: uppercase; }

The CSS class is .toc-level-N where N is the nesting depth (1-based). The outputclass value is appended as an additional class.
usage_contexts: Creating TOC-only headings that group topics without a landing page
Applying custom CSS styling to specific TOC sections in AEM Guides Native PDF
Hiding deprecated sections from TOC while keeping content accessible
Creating visually distinct TOC groups (color-coded chapters, bold sections)
common_mistakes: Forgetting navtitle on topichead - produces a blank/missing TOC entry
Adding href to topichead - topichead is for headings only, use topicref if you need a link
Setting locktitle='yes' explicitly on topichead - it is already the default, unlike topicref
Using outputclass='toc-level-1' as the value - toc-level-N is the CONTAINER class added by the renderer, your outputclass is an ADDITIONAL class
Expecting outputclass to work without corresponding CSS in layout.css - outputclass only adds the class, you must define the styles
Nesting topichead inside a topic file - topichead is DITAMAP-only, not allowed in .dita files

Placing topichead as a direct child of <bookmap> - WRONG: <bookmap><topichead navtitle='Ch1'>. In bookmap, topichead MUST be inside <chapter>, <part>, <appendix>, or another structural container. Valid direct children of <bookmap> are: booktitle, bookmeta, frontmatter, chapter, part, appendix, backmatter, reltable. Use <map> if you want topichead at the root level.
Confusing <map> vs <bookmap> child rules - in <map>, topichead CAN be a direct child; in <bookmap>, it CANNOT. Always wrap topichead inside <chapter> or <part> when using bookmap.
Placing topichead directly inside <bookmap> - topichead must be inside <chapter>, <part>, or <appendix> in a bookmap
correct_examples:
In a <map> (topichead at root is OK):
<map>
  <title>Product Guide</title>
  <topichead navtitle="Getting Started" outputclass="highlight">
    <topicref href="install.dita"/>
    <topicref href="configure.dita"/>
  </topichead>
</map>

layout.css:
.toc-level-1.highlight { color: #2563eb; font-weight: 600; }

In a <bookmap> (topichead MUST be inside chapter):
<bookmap>
  <booktitle><mainbooktitle>Product Guide</mainbooktitle></booktitle>
  <chapter>
    <topichead navtitle="Advanced Topics">
      <topichead navtitle="Performance" outputclass="perf">
        <topicref href="caching.dita"/>
      </topichead>
    </topichead>
  </chapter>
</bookmap>

❌ WRONG: <bookmap><topichead navtitle="Chapter 1">...</topichead></bookmap>
✅ CORRECT: <bookmap><chapter><topichead navtitle="Section 1">...</topichead></chapter></bookmap>

usage_contexts: Styling TOC in AEM Guides Native PDF output
Creating color-coded or visually grouped TOC sections
Customizing font sizes and indentation per TOC level
Hiding specific TOC entries via display:none with outputclass
Adding custom borders, backgrounds, or icons to TOC entries
common_mistakes: Trying to style TOC from within .dita topic files - TOC styling is CSS-only in layout.css
Using inline styles in DITAMAP - DITAMAP does not support inline CSS, use outputclass + layout.css
Confusing toc-level-N with outputclass - toc-level-N is auto-generated from depth, outputclass is user-defined
Forgetting to assign the PDF template to the output preset - styles won't apply without template assignment
Using !important excessively in layout.css - AEM Guides CSS specificity is straightforward, prefer proper selectors
Not testing with actual PDF generation - browser preview does not match Native PDF rendering exactly

text_content:
<subjectScheme> is a specialized DITA map used for taxonomy, classification, and controlled attribute value enumeration. It does NOT contain topic content - it defines relationships between subjects (concepts/categories).

PURPOSE:
1. Define taxonomies (KIND-OF, INSTANCE-OF, PART-OF hierarchies)
2. Control attribute values - restrict what values authors can pick for @audience, @platform, @product, @otherprops, etc.
3. Define relationships between subjects (hasRelated)

KEY CHILDREN:
- <hasKind> - KIND-OF hierarchy (City is a kind of Settlement)
- <hasInstance> - INSTANCE-OF hierarchy (NYC is an instance of City)
- <hasNarrower> - narrower/broader relationships
- <hasPart> - part/whole relationships
- <hasRelated> - associative relationships
- <subjectdef> - defines individual subjects with keys
- <enumerationdef> - BINDS subjects to attributes (this is what controls dropdowns)

EXAMPLE - Controlled @audience values:
```xml
<subjectScheme>
  <subjectdef keys="audienceValues">
    <subjectdef keys="admin" navtitle="Administrator"/>
    <subjectdef keys="developer" navtitle="Developer"/>
    <subjectdef keys="enduser" navtitle="End User"/>
  </subjectdef>
  <enumerationdef>
    <attributedef name="audience"/>
    <subjectdef keyref="audienceValues"/>
  </enumerationdef>
</subjectScheme>
```

AEM GUIDES AUTHOR VIEW:
- When a subjectScheme map is added to the root map, the Web Editor restricts attribute dropdowns to only the defined values.
- Authors see a filtered dropdown instead of free-text entry.
- The subjectScheme map must be referenced from the root ditamap/bookmap via <mapref> or <topicref> with processing-role='resource-only'.
- Subject scheme maps are NOT published - they only control authoring.
usage_contexts: Controlling attribute value dropdowns in AEM Guides Web Editor
Defining taxonomy hierarchies for classification
Restricting @audience, @platform, @product values to a controlled list
Creating subject-based filtering for conditional processing
common_mistakes: Forgetting <enumerationdef> - without it, the subject definitions exist but don't control any attributes
Putting subjectScheme content in a regular ditamap - must be a separate <subjectScheme> map
Not referencing the subjectScheme map from the root map - it won't take effect
Expecting subjectScheme to appear in published output - it is authoring-only
Using href instead of keys on subjectdef - keys is the primary referencing mechanism
correct_examples:
<subjectScheme>
  <subjectdef keys="platformValues">
    <subjectdef keys="windows" navtitle="Windows"/>
    <subjectdef keys="linux" navtitle="Linux"/>
    <subjectdef keys="macos" navtitle="macOS"/>
  </subjectdef>
  <enumerationdef>
    <attributedef name="platform"/>
    <subjectdef keyref="platformValues"/>
  </enumerationdef>
</subjectScheme>

Reference from root map:
<map>
  <title>Product Guide</title>
  <topicref href="subjectScheme.ditamap" format="ditamap" processing-role="resource-only"/>
  <topicref href="chapter1.dita"/>
</map>

text_content:
<hasInstance> is a subject scheme element that establishes INSTANCE-OF relationships. It is a specialization of <topicref> used inside <subjectScheme> maps.

WHAT IT MEANS:
Child subjects are specific INSTANCES of the parent subject. Example: 'New York City' is an instance of 'City'. The parent is a type/class, the children are concrete entities of that type.

HREF REQUIREMENT:
- href is OPTIONAL on hasInstance. It can point to a topic that describes the relationship, but this is rarely used.
- The primary mechanism is keys/keyref on the child <subjectdef> elements.
- In practice, href is almost never needed on hasInstance itself.

EXAMPLE:
```xml
<subjectScheme>
  <hasInstance>
    <subjectdef keys="city" navtitle="City">
      <subjectdef keys="nyc" navtitle="New York City"/>
      <subjectdef keys="reykjavik" navtitle="Reykjavik"/>
      <subjectdef keys="tokyo" navtitle="Tokyo"/>
    </subjectdef>
  </hasInstance>
</subjectScheme>
```

AEM GUIDES AUTHOR VIEW:
- Subject scheme maps control attribute value dropdowns in the Web Editor.
- hasInstance hierarchies appear as nested/grouped options in dropdowns.
- The resolution happens at the map level, NOT at topic level.
- Authors do not see <hasInstance> directly - they see the filtered attribute values it defines.
- If href is provided on a subjectdef child, clicking it in the DITAMAP Editor navigates to that topic, but this does NOT affect Author view attribute filtering.

DIFFERENCE FROM hasKind:
- hasKind = TYPE hierarchy (City IS A KIND OF Settlement)
- hasInstance = INSTANCE hierarchy (NYC IS AN INSTANCE OF City)
- Both use <subjectdef> children, both are optional-href
usage_contexts: Defining instance-of relationships in subject scheme taxonomy
Controlling attribute enumeration where instances are concrete entities
Classification hierarchies where children are specific examples of the parent type
common_mistakes: Thinking href is required on hasInstance - it is optional, keys on child subjectdef is the primary mechanism
Using hasInstance outside a <subjectScheme> map - it is only valid inside subjectScheme
Confusing hasInstance with hasKind - hasInstance is for concrete instances, hasKind is for type/subtype hierarchies
Expecting hasInstance to render in topic output - it only affects attribute value enumeration in the editor
Looking for hasInstance in AEM Guides Author view - authors see filtered dropdowns, not the subject scheme elements directly
correct_examples:
<subjectScheme>
  <hasInstance>
    <subjectdef keys="database" navtitle="Database">
      <subjectdef keys="mysql" navtitle="MySQL"/>
      <subjectdef keys="postgres" navtitle="PostgreSQL"/>
      <subjectdef keys="oracle" navtitle="Oracle DB"/>
    </subjectdef>
  </hasInstance>
</subjectScheme>

Combined with enumerationdef:
<subjectScheme>
  <hasInstance>
    <subjectdef keys="products">
      <subjectdef keys="aem-guides" navtitle="AEM Guides"/>
      <subjectdef keys="aem-sites" navtitle="AEM Sites"/>
    </subjectdef>
  </hasInstance>
  <enumerationdef>
    <attributedef name="product"/>
    <subjectdef keyref="products"/>
  </enumerationdef>
</subjectScheme>

text_content:
<hasKind> is a subject scheme element that establishes KIND-OF (IS-A) relationships. It is a specialization of <topicref> used inside <subjectScheme> maps.

WHAT IT MEANS:
Child subjects are SUBTYPES or KINDS of the parent subject. Example: 'City' is a kind of 'Human Settlement'. This is a generic/subsumption hierarchy - each child is a more specific category.

HREF REQUIREMENT:
- href is OPTIONAL on hasKind. It can point to a topic describing the relationship, but is rarely used.
- The primary mechanism is keys on child <subjectdef> elements.

EXAMPLE:
```xml
<subjectScheme>
  <hasKind>
    <subjectdef keys="settlement" navtitle="Human Settlement">
      <subjectdef keys="city" navtitle="City">
        <subjectdef keys="bigcity" navtitle="Big City"/>
        <subjectdef keys="smallcity" navtitle="Small City"/>
      </subjectdef>
      <subjectdef keys="town" navtitle="Town"/>
      <subjectdef keys="village" navtitle="Village"/>
    </subjectdef>
  </hasKind>
</subjectScheme>
```

AEM GUIDES AUTHOR VIEW:
- Like hasInstance, hasKind hierarchies control attribute value enumeration in the Web Editor.
- The KIND-OF nesting determines grouping in attribute dropdowns.
- Authors see the navtitle values, not the element structure.

DIFFERENCE FROM hasInstance:
- hasKind = TYPE hierarchy (City IS A KIND OF Settlement)
- hasInstance = INSTANCE hierarchy (NYC IS AN INSTANCE OF City)
- Use hasKind when children are subcategories/subtypes
- Use hasInstance when children are concrete examples/entities
usage_contexts: Defining type/subtype taxonomies in subject scheme maps
Creating hierarchical category structures for attribute enumeration
IS-A relationship modeling (e.g., types of documents, types of users)
common_mistakes: Confusing hasKind with hasInstance - hasKind is for type hierarchies, hasInstance is for concrete instances
Using hasKind outside <subjectScheme> - only valid inside subjectScheme maps
Forgetting enumerationdef to bind the hasKind hierarchy to an attribute
Thinking href is required - it is optional, keys on subjectdef is primary
correct_examples:
<subjectScheme>
  <hasKind>
    <subjectdef keys="doctype" navtitle="Document Type">
      <subjectdef keys="concept" navtitle="Concept"/>
      <subjectdef keys="task" navtitle="Task"/>
      <subjectdef keys="reference" navtitle="Reference"/>
      <subjectdef keys="troubleshooting" navtitle="Troubleshooting"/>
    </subjectdef>
  </hasKind>
</subjectScheme>

<subjectScheme>
  <hasKind>
    <subjectdef keys="audience" navtitle="Audience">
      <subjectdef keys="internal" navtitle="Internal">
        <subjectdef keys="dev" navtitle="Developer"/>
        <subjectdef keys="qa" navtitle="QA Engineer"/>
      </subjectdef>
      <subjectdef keys="external" navtitle="External">
        <subjectdef keys="enduser" navtitle="End User"/>
        <subjectdef keys="partner" navtitle="Partner"/>
      </subjectdef>
    </subjectdef>
  </hasKind>
  <enumerationdef>
    <attributedef name="audience"/>
    <subjectdef keyref="audience"/>
  </enumerationdef>
</subjectScheme>

text_content:
<subjectdef> defines a single subject (concept, category, or value) within a <subjectScheme> map. It is a specialization of <topicref>.

PURPOSE:
- Defines a subject with a key identifier and optional navigation title
- Can nest other <subjectdef> elements for hierarchical taxonomies
- Used as children of <hasKind>, <hasInstance>, <hasNarrower>, <hasPart>, <hasRelated>, or directly under <subjectScheme>

KEY ATTRIBUTES:
- keys (primary) - space-separated key identifiers for referencing this subject
- navtitle - human-readable label shown in dropdowns/UI
- href (optional) - points to a topic with full description of the subject
- keyref (optional) - indirect reference to another subject

EXAMPLE:
```xml
<subjectScheme>
  <subjectdef keys="os" navtitle="Operating System">
    <subjectdef keys="windows" navtitle="Windows"/>
    <subjectdef keys="linux" navtitle="Linux">
      <subjectdef keys="ubuntu" navtitle="Ubuntu"/>
      <subjectdef keys="rhel" navtitle="Red Hat Enterprise Linux"/>
    </subjectdef>
  </subjectdef>
  <enumerationdef>
    <attributedef name="platform"/>
    <subjectdef keyref="os"/>
  </enumerationdef>
</subjectScheme>
```

AEM GUIDES:
- The navtitle values appear in attribute dropdowns in the Web Editor
- The keys values are what get written to the @platform/@audience etc. attribute
- Nested subjectdef creates grouped/hierarchical dropdown options
usage_contexts: Defining subjects in subject scheme taxonomies
Creating controlled vocabulary for attribute values
Nesting for hierarchical category structures
common_mistakes: Omitting keys attribute - without keys, the subject cannot be referenced by enumerationdef
Omitting navtitle - without it, the dropdown shows the key value which may not be user-friendly
Using subjectdef outside a subjectScheme map - only valid inside subjectScheme
Confusing subjectdef with topicref - subjectdef defines a subject, topicref references a topic
correct_examples:
<subjectdef keys="priority" navtitle="Priority Level">
  <subjectdef keys="high" navtitle="High Priority"/>
  <subjectdef keys="medium" navtitle="Medium Priority"/>
  <subjectdef keys="low" navtitle="Low Priority"/>
</subjectdef>

With href to full definition:
<subjectdef keys="conref" navtitle="Content Reference" href="glossary/gl-conref.dita"/>

text_content:
is a convenience map element for referencing glossentry topics. It is equivalent to but with clearer intent. PURPOSE: - Reference glossentry .dita files from a ditamap or bookmap - Assign keys for keyref-based glossary linking in topics - Build a glossary section in your map KEY ATTRIBUTES: - href (REQUIRED) - points to the glossentry .dita file - keys (RECOMMENDED) - assigns a key for keyref-based term linking - print='no' - optional, excludes from print output EXAMPLE - Glossary section in bookmap: ```xml ``` KEYREF-BASED GLOSSARY LINKING: Once a glossref assigns a key, topics can link to the term: ```xml

Use to reuse content.

``` The keyword resolves to the glossterm text at build time. AEM GUIDES WEB EDITOR: - glossref + keys enables tooltip popups when hovering over in topics - The Web Editor shows the glossterm + glossdef in the tooltip - Without keys on glossref, the tooltip linking does not work - glossref entries appear in the Glossary panel of the Web Editor
usage_contexts: Building glossary sections in ditamaps and bookmaps
Enabling keyref-based glossary term linking across topics
AEM Guides Web Editor glossary tooltip popups
Glossary appendix in bookmap output
common_mistakes: Omitting href on glossref - href is REQUIRED, unlike hasInstance/hasKind where it is optional
Omitting keys on glossref - without keys, keyref-based term linking and tooltips won't work
Using glossref outside a map - it is a MAP element, not valid in topics
Placing glossref directly in <bookmap> - must be inside <chapter>, <appendix>, or <topichead>
Confusing glossref with glossentry - glossref goes in the MAP to reference a glossentry TOPIC
Using <topicref href='glossary.dita'/> instead of <glossref> - works but loses semantic clarity
correct_examples:
<map>
  <title>User Guide</title>
  <topicref href="chapter1.dita"/>
  <topichead navtitle="Glossary">
    <glossref href="glossary/gl-api.dita" keys="gl-api"/>
    <glossref href="glossary/gl-sdk.dita" keys="gl-sdk"/>
  </topichead>
</map>

In a bookmap: In a topic:

Use for content reuse.

text_content:
<hasNarrower> is a subject scheme element that establishes a NARROWER/BROADER relationship without specifying the exact nature (kind-of, instance-of, part-of). It is a specialization of <topicref> used inside <subjectScheme> maps.

WHAT IT MEANS:
The parent subject is more GENERAL, child subjects are more SPECIFIC. This is the default/generic hierarchy - use it when you want to say 'X is narrower than Y' without committing to whether X is a kind-of, instance-of, or part-of Y.

WHEN TO USE vs hasKind/hasInstance:
- Use <hasKind> when children are SUBTYPES (City is a kind of Settlement)
- Use <hasInstance> when children are CONCRETE INSTANCES (NYC is an instance of City)
- Use <hasNarrower> when the relationship is simply narrower/broader without a precise semantic (e.g., 'Planting Roses' is narrower than 'Horticulture')

HREF REQUIREMENT:
- href is OPTIONAL. keys on child <subjectdef> is the primary mechanism.

EXAMPLE:
```xml
<subjectScheme>
  <hasNarrower>
    <subjectdef keys="horticulture" navtitle="Horticulture">
      <subjectdef keys="planting-roses" navtitle="Planting Roses"/>
      <subjectdef keys="pruning" navtitle="Pruning Techniques"/>
      <subjectdef keys="soil-prep" navtitle="Soil Preparation"/>
    </subjectdef>
  </hasNarrower>
</subjectScheme>
```

AEM GUIDES:
- Same behavior as hasKind/hasInstance for attribute enumeration.
- The hierarchy appears in attribute dropdowns when bound via <enumerationdef>.
- Authors see the navtitle values in the Web Editor dropdown.
usage_contexts: Generic narrower/broader subject hierarchies in subject scheme maps
When the exact relationship type (kind-of, instance-of) is not important
Topic-based classification where subjects have varying levels of specificity
common_mistakes: Using hasNarrower when hasKind or hasInstance is more precise - prefer specific relationship types when possible
Using hasNarrower outside <subjectScheme> - only valid inside subjectScheme maps
Thinking href is required - it is optional, keys on subjectdef is primary
Forgetting enumerationdef to bind the hierarchy to an attribute
correct_examples:
<subjectScheme>
  <hasNarrower>
    <subjectdef keys="security" navtitle="Security">
      <subjectdef keys="auth" navtitle="Authentication"/>
      <subjectdef keys="encryption" navtitle="Encryption"/>
      <subjectdef keys="access-control" navtitle="Access Control"/>
    </subjectdef>
  </hasNarrower>
  <enumerationdef>
    <attributedef name="otherprops"/>
    <subjectdef keyref="security"/>
  </enumerationdef>
</subjectScheme>

text_content:
<b> applies bold highlighting to its content. It is a PRESENTATION element, not a semantic one. In DITA, you should almost always prefer semantic elements over <b>.

WHEN TO USE <b>:
- Only when no semantic element fits the purpose
- For emphasis that has no specific meaning (rare in technical docs)

PREFER THESE SEMANTIC ALTERNATIVES:
- UI element name → <uicontrol>Save</uicontrol>
- File/path → <filepath>/etc/config</filepath>
- Code/API → <codeph>main()</codeph>
- Command → <cmdname>git</cmdname>
- Menu sequence → <menucascade><uicontrol>File</uicontrol><uicontrol>Save</uicontrol></menucascade>
- Term being defined → <term>conref</term>
- Variable name → <varname>MAX_RETRIES</varname>
usage_contexts: Bold emphasis when no semantic element applies
Highlighting domain elements inside highlighting domain
common_mistakes: Using <b> for UI button/menu names — use <uicontrol> instead
Using <b> for file paths — use <filepath> instead
Using <b> for code/API names — use <codeph> or <apiname> instead
Using <b> for command names — use <cmdname> instead
Using <b> for terms being defined — use <term> instead
Overusing <b> in technical docs — semantic elements are almost always better
correct_examples: ❌ WRONG: Click Save to save. ✅ CORRECT: Click Save to save.
❌ WRONG: Edit the /etc/hosts file. ✅ CORRECT: Edit the /etc/hosts file.

Acceptable use:

This step is critical — skipping it will corrupt the database.

text_content:
<i> applies italic highlighting to its content. Like <b>, it is a PRESENTATION element. Prefer semantic alternatives in DITA.

PREFER THESE SEMANTIC ALTERNATIVES:
- Term/glossary word → <term>conref</term>
- Variable name → <varname>hostname</varname>
- Book/document title → <cite>DITA 1.3 Specification</cite>
- Foreign word → <ph xml:lang="la">et cetera</ph>
usage_contexts: Italic emphasis when no semantic element applies
Foreign phrases where no other element fits
common_mistakes: Using <i> for terms being defined — use <term> instead
Using <i> for variable names — use <varname> instead
Using <i> for book/document titles — use <cite> instead
Using <i> for emphasis — consider if a semantic element fits first
correct_examples: ❌ WRONG: Set the timeout variable. ✅ CORRECT: Set the timeout variable.
❌ WRONG: See the DITA 1.3 Specification. ✅ CORRECT: See the DITA 1.3 Specification.

text_content:
<u> applies underline highlighting. It is a PRESENTATION element and is RARELY appropriate in DITA technical documentation.

CAUTION:
- Underline is commonly associated with hyperlinks in web output
- Using <u> can confuse readers into thinking text is clickable
- Almost always prefer semantic elements or other highlighting
usage_contexts: Rarely appropriate — underline conflicts with hyperlink styling
Legacy content migration where underline must be preserved
common_mistakes: Using <u> for emphasis — readers confuse underlined text with hyperlinks
Using <u> instead of <xref> for actual links
Overusing <u> in web-targeted output — underline = link in web conventions
correct_examples:
❌ AVOID:

This is important.

✅ BETTER:

This is important.

(or use a )

text_content:
<sup> renders content as superscript. Used for trademark symbols, mathematical exponents, footnote references, and ordinal indicators.

COMMON USES:
- Trademark: Product<sup>™</sup>
- Registered: Product<sup>®</sup>
- Exponents: x<sup>2</sup> + y<sup>2</sup>
- Ordinals: 1<sup>st</sup>, 2<sup>nd</sup>, 3<sup>rd</sup>
- Footnote refs: See note<sup>1</sup>
usage_contexts: Trademark and registered symbols
Mathematical exponents and powers
Footnote references
Ordinal number indicators (1st, 2nd)
common_mistakes: Using Unicode superscript characters instead of <sup> — not all fonts render them correctly
Using <sup> for chemical formulas — subscript <sub> is usually needed for chemical notation
correct_examples: Adobe Experience Manager® Guides provides DITA authoring.
The area is calculated as πr2.

text_content:
<sub> renders content as subscript. Used for chemical formulas, mathematical notation, and technical identifiers.

COMMON USES:
- Chemical formulas: H<sub>2</sub>O, CO<sub>2</sub>, NaCl
- Math subscripts: x<sub>1</sub>, x<sub>n</sub>
- Technical notation: V<sub>max</sub>, T<sub>amb</sub>
usage_contexts: Chemical formulas (H2O, CO2)
Mathematical subscript notation
Technical variable subscripts (Vmax, Tamb)
common_mistakes: Using Unicode subscript characters instead of <sub> — inconsistent rendering
Using <sub> for exponents — use <sup> for superscript/powers
correct_examples: Water (H2O) is essential for the cooling process.
Measure the temperature Tmax at the outlet.

text_content:
<tt> renders content in a monospace/teletype font. In DITA 1.3, <tt> is DEPRECATED in favor of semantic alternatives.

PREFER THESE ALTERNATIVES:
- Code/API → <codeph>String.valueOf()</codeph>
- File path → <filepath>/usr/local/bin</filepath>
- System output → <systemoutput>OK</systemoutput>
- User input → <userinput>ls -la</userinput>
- Command → <cmdname>grep</cmdname>
usage_contexts: DEPRECATED — use <codeph>, <filepath>, <systemoutput>, or <userinput> instead
Legacy content migration only
common_mistakes: Using <tt> in new content — it is deprecated, use <codeph> instead
Using <tt> for file paths — use <filepath> instead
Using <tt> for user input — use <userinput> instead
correct_examples:
❌ DEPRECATED: <tt>config.xml</tt>
✅ CORRECT: <codeph>config.xml</codeph> or <filepath>config.xml</filepath>

text_content:
describes the root cause of a problem in a topic. It is a child of and explains WHY the issue occurs. STRUCTURE: ```xml Build fails with memory error

The build process terminates with 'OutOfMemoryError'.

The JVM heap size is too small for the project size.

 Open build.properties. Increase -Xmx to 4096m. ``` Multiple elements are allowed when there are several possible causes.
usage_contexts: Explaining the root cause of a problem in troubleshooting topics
Multiple causes for the same symptom (use multiple <cause> elements)
AEM Guides troubleshooting topic type
common_mistakes: Putting <cause> directly in <troubleshooting> — must be inside <troublebody>
Confusing <cause> with <condition> — condition is the SYMPTOM, cause is the REASON
Using <cause> in concept/task topics — only valid in <troubleshooting>
correct_examples:
PDF output shows broken images.

Image paths use absolute URLs that are not accessible during PDF generation.

 Change image paths to relative paths.

text_content:
<condition> describes the SYMPTOM or situation that triggers troubleshooting. It answers 'What does the user see or experience?' and is a child of <troublebody>.

The <condition> comes BEFORE <cause> and <remedy> in the troublebody structure.
usage_contexts: Describing the symptom/situation in troubleshooting topics
What the user sees or experiences before investigating the cause
common_mistakes: Confusing <condition> with <cause> — condition is the SYMPTOM, cause is the REASON
Putting remedy steps in <condition> — condition only describes the problem
Skipping <condition> entirely — readers need to identify their problem first
correct_examples:
After upgrading AEM Guides to 4.4, the Web Editor fails to load and shows a blank white screen.

Browser cache contains stale JavaScript bundles.

 Clear browser cache and reload.

text_content:
<remedy> provides the SOLUTION to the problem described in <condition>/<cause>. It typically contains <steps> or <steps-unordered>.

STRUCTURE OPTIONS:
1. Simple remedy with steps:
   <remedy><steps>...</steps></remedy>

2. Remedy with responsible party:
   <remedy>
     <responsibleParty>System Administrator</responsibleParty>
     <steps>...</steps>
   </remedy>

Multiple <remedy> elements are allowed for alternative solutions.
usage_contexts: Providing fix/solution steps in troubleshooting topics
Multiple alternative remedies for the same problem
Assigning remedies to specific roles via <responsibleParty>
common_mistakes: Putting <steps> directly in <troublebody> — must be inside <remedy>
Using <taskbody> structure in troubleshooting — troubleshooting uses condition/cause/remedy, not prereq/context/steps
Forgetting to wrap steps in <remedy> — <steps> cannot be a direct child of <troublebody>
correct_examples:
<remedy>
  <responsibleParty>Content Author</responsibleParty>
  <steps>
    <step><cmd>Open the DITA map in the Web Editor.</cmd></step>
    <step><cmd>Right-click the broken topicref and select <uicontrol>Resolve Path</uicontrol>.</cmd></step>
  </steps>
</remedy>

text_content:
<enumerationdef> BINDS a set of subject definitions to a specific attribute, controlling what values authors can select for that attribute. Without <enumerationdef>, a subject scheme defines subjects but does NOT control any attribute dropdowns.

STRUCTURE:
```xml
<enumerationdef>
  <attributedef name="audience"/>  <!-- which attribute to control -->
  <subjectdef keyref="audienceValues"/>  <!-- which subjects to use -->
</enumerationdef>
```

Can also restrict to specific elements:
```xml
<enumerationdef>
  <elementdef name="note"/>  <!-- only control @type on <note> -->
  <attributedef name="type"/>
  <subjectdef keyref="noteTypes"/>
</enumerationdef>
```

AEM GUIDES: When the subject scheme map is referenced from the root map, enumerationdef causes the Web Editor to show a filtered dropdown instead of free-text entry for the specified attribute.
usage_contexts: Binding subject definitions to specific attributes for controlled values
Restricting attribute dropdowns in AEM Guides Web Editor
Controlling @audience, @platform, @product, @otherprops values
common_mistakes: Defining subjects without enumerationdef — subjects exist but don't control any attribute
Forgetting <attributedef> inside enumerationdef — must specify which attribute to control
Using href instead of keyref on the <subjectdef> child — keyref references the subject tree
Not referencing the subject scheme map from the root map — enumerationdef won't take effect
correct_examples:
<subjectScheme>
  <subjectdef keys="audiences">
    <subjectdef keys="admin" navtitle="Administrator"/>
    <subjectdef keys="dev" navtitle="Developer"/>
  </subjectdef>
  <enumerationdef>
    <attributedef name="audience"/>
    <subjectdef keyref="audiences"/>
  </enumerationdef>
</subjectScheme>

text_content:
<attributedef> names the attribute being controlled by an <enumerationdef>. It has one key attribute: name (required) — the attribute name to control.

EXAMPLE:
<attributedef name="audience"/> — controls the @audience attribute
<attributedef name="platform"/> — controls the @platform attribute
<attributedef name="otherprops"/> — controls the @otherprops attribute
usage_contexts: Specifying which attribute an enumerationdef controls
Used inside <enumerationdef> in subject scheme maps
common_mistakes: Omitting the name attribute — name is required to identify the controlled attribute
Using attributedef outside enumerationdef — only valid as a child of enumerationdef
Specifying a non-existent attribute name — must match a real DITA attribute
correct_examples:
<enumerationdef>
  <attributedef name="platform"/>
  <subjectdef keyref="platformValues"/>
</enumerationdef>

text_content:
<hasPart> establishes PART-OF (WHOLE-PART) relationships in a subject scheme. Child subjects are PARTS of the parent subject.

Example: 'Engine' is a part of 'Car'.

HREF is optional — keys on child <subjectdef> is the primary mechanism.

EXAMPLE:
```xml
<subjectScheme>
  <hasPart>
    <subjectdef keys="car" navtitle="Car">
      <subjectdef keys="engine" navtitle="Engine"/>
      <subjectdef keys="transmission" navtitle="Transmission"/>
      <subjectdef keys="chassis" navtitle="Chassis"/>
    </subjectdef>
  </hasPart>
</subjectScheme>
```

DIFFERENCE FROM hasKind:
- hasPart = PART-OF (Engine is a part of Car)
- hasKind = KIND-OF (Sedan is a kind of Car)
usage_contexts: Defining whole-part decomposition in subject scheme
Product component hierarchies
System architecture taxonomies
common_mistakes: Confusing hasPart with hasKind — hasPart is for components, hasKind is for subtypes
Using hasPart outside <subjectScheme> — only valid inside subject scheme maps
correct_examples:
<subjectScheme>
  <hasPart>
    <subjectdef keys="aem" navtitle="AEM">
      <subjectdef keys="sites" navtitle="AEM Sites"/>
      <subjectdef keys="assets" navtitle="AEM Assets"/>
      <subjectdef keys="guides" navtitle="AEM Guides"/>
    </subjectdef>
  </hasPart>
</subjectScheme>

text_content:
<hasRelated> establishes ASSOCIATIVE relationships between subjects in a subject scheme. Unlike hasKind/hasInstance/hasPart which are hierarchical, hasRelated indicates subjects that are RELATED but not in a parent-child taxonomy.

EXAMPLE:
```xml
<subjectScheme>
  <hasRelated>
    <subjectdef keys="dita" navtitle="DITA">
      <subjectdef keys="xml" navtitle="XML"/>
      <subjectdef keys="xslt" navtitle="XSLT"/>
    </subjectdef>
  </hasRelated>
</subjectScheme>
```
usage_contexts: Associating related subjects that don't have hierarchical relationship
Cross-referencing related technologies or concepts
common_mistakes: Using hasRelated for hierarchical relationships — use hasKind, hasInstance, or hasPart instead
Using hasRelated outside <subjectScheme>
correct_examples:
<subjectScheme>
  <hasRelated>
    <subjectdef keys="conref" navtitle="conref">
      <subjectdef keys="conkeyref" navtitle="conkeyref"/>
      <subjectdef keys="keyref" navtitle="keyref"/>
    </subjectdef>
  </hasRelated>
</subjectScheme>

text_content:
identifies a message number or error code in documentation. It is part of the software domain and is used to mark up identifiers for messages that a product generates. EXAMPLE:

If you see error ERR-2045, restart the service.
usage_contexts: Marking up error codes and message numbers
Software message identification for searchability
Error reference documentation
common_mistakes: Using <codeph> for message numbers — <msgnum> is more semantically correct
Using plain text for error codes — marking them up enables search and linking
correct_examples: Error AEM-4012 indicates the DITA map cannot be found.
See message GUIDES-WARN-001 in the server logs.

text_content:
contains the text of a message produced by an application. Unlike which is just the code, wraps the full message text and can contain variable elements like . EXAMPLE:

The system displays: File filename not found in directory.
usage_contexts: Documenting system/application messages with embedded variables
Error message text that includes variable parts
common_mistakes: Using <codeblock> for short messages — <msgph> is inline, use it for in-paragraph messages
Not using <varname> inside <msgph> for variable parts of the message
correct_examples: If you see Connection to host timed out after N seconds, check the network.

text_content:
<systemoutput> marks text that a computer or system generates in response to user input or as part of a process. Rendered in monospace by default.

DIFFERENCE FROM SIMILAR ELEMENTS:
- <systemoutput> = what the SYSTEM produces
- <userinput> = what the USER types
- <codeph> = generic inline code
- <screen> = block-level terminal session (both input and output)
usage_contexts: System-generated text, return values, status messages
Command-line output shown inline in a paragraph
common_mistakes: Using <codeph> when the text is specifically system output — <systemoutput> is more precise
Using <systemoutput> for user-typed commands — use <userinput> instead
Using <systemoutput> for block output — use <screen> for multi-line terminal sessions
correct_examples: The command returns BUILD SUCCESSFUL when complete.
If the status shows FAILED, check the error log.

text_content:
<userinput> marks text that a user types or enters. Rendered in monospace by default. Use it to distinguish what the user types from what the system displays.

DIFFERENCE FROM SIMILAR ELEMENTS:
- <userinput> = what the USER types (keyboard input)
- <systemoutput> = what the SYSTEM displays
- <codeblock> = block-level code listing
- <codeph> = generic inline code reference
usage_contexts: Text the user types at a command prompt
Values the user enters in a form field
Keyboard input in procedures
common_mistakes: Using <codeph> when describing what the user types — <userinput> is more precise
Using <userinput> for system-generated text — use <systemoutput> instead
Using <userinput> for UI button names — use <uicontrol> instead
correct_examples: At the prompt, type sudo systemctl restart aem and press Enter.
Enter admin in the username field.

text_content:
<hazardstatement> provides safety-related warnings following ANSI Z535 / ISO 3864 standards. It is more structured than <note type='danger'> and supports formal hazard communication.

TYPES: danger, warning, caution, notice

STRUCTURE:
```xml
<hazardstatement type="warning">
  <messagepanel>
    <typeofhazard>Electrical shock</typeofhazard>
    <consequence>Contact with live wires can cause severe injury or death.</consequence>
    <howtoavoid>Disconnect power before servicing.</howtoavoid>
  </messagepanel>
</hazardstatement>
```
usage_contexts: Safety-critical documentation (manufacturing, electrical, chemical)
ANSI Z535 compliant hazard communication
Regulated industry documentation
common_mistakes: Using <note type='danger'> instead of <hazardstatement> for ANSI-compliant safety warnings
Forgetting <messagepanel> — it is the required wrapper for hazard content
Omitting the type attribute — it determines the severity level
correct_examples:
<hazardstatement type="danger">
  <messagepanel>
    <typeofhazard>High voltage</typeofhazard>
    <consequence>Electrocution can cause death.</consequence>
    <howtoavoid>De-energize all circuits before opening the panel.</howtoavoid>
  </messagepanel>
</hazardstatement>

text_content:
<topicsubject> classifies topics in a DITAMAP by associating them with subjects defined in a subject scheme. It is used inside <topicSubjectTable> or as a child of <topicref> to assign classification metadata.

This enables subject-based filtering and faceted search in output.

EXAMPLE:
```xml
<topicref href="install-guide.dita">
  <topicsubject keyref="admin"/>
</topicref>
```
This classifies install-guide.dita as relevant to the 'admin' audience.
usage_contexts: Classifying topics by subject for faceted search
Associating topics with audience/platform/product subjects
Enabling subject-based filtering in output
common_mistakes: Using topicsubject without a subject scheme — the keyref must resolve to a subjectdef
Confusing topicsubject with the audience attribute — topicsubject is for classification, audience is for conditional processing
correct_examples:
<topicref href="api-reference.dita">
  <topicsubject keyref="developer"/>
</topicref>

text_content:
<subjectref> references a subject defined in a subject scheme from outside the scheme itself. Used in classification maps to associate content with subjects for filtering and organization.

EXAMPLE:
<subjectref keyref="linux"/> — references the 'linux' subject from a subject scheme
usage_contexts: Referencing subjects from outside the subject scheme
Classification maps and topic-subject associations
common_mistakes: Using subjectref without a corresponding subjectdef in the subject scheme
Confusing subjectref with subjectdef — subjectdef DEFINES subjects, subjectref REFERENCES them
correct_examples:
<topicref href="linux-setup.dita">
  <topicsubject>
    <subjectref keyref="linux"/>
    <subjectref keyref="admin"/>
  </topicsubject>
</topicref>

text_content: AEM Guides supports 7 output preset types: AEM Sites (publish topics as AEM pages), PDF (via Native PDF engine or DITA-OT), HTML5 (responsive web output), EPUB (e-book format), JSON (structured data for headless CMS), Custom (DITA-OT plugin-based), and Knowledge Base (publish to ServiceNow/Salesforce/Zendesk). Each preset is configured per DITA map from the Map Dashboard or Web Editor. Presets define transformation parameters, conditions (DITAVAL), baselines, and post-processing hooks.
common_mistakes: Confusing Native PDF preset with DITA-OT PDF preset — Native PDF uses AEM's built-in engine with CSS/JS templates; DITA-OT PDF uses Apache FOP or custom plugins
Not applying a baseline when publishing — output may include draft/unapproved content
Forgetting to set condition presets (DITAVAL) — output includes all profiled content instead of filtered
Using AEM Sites preset without configuring the site structure/template first
Publishing Knowledge Base without mapping DITA elements to the target platform's content model
correct_examples: Navigate to Map Dashboard > Output Presets tab > click + to add a new preset. Select preset type (e.g., 'PDF'), configure template, conditions, and baseline, then click Generate.
usage_contexts: Map Dashboard output tab
Web Editor quick generate
Bulk activation collections
CI/CD publishing pipelines

text_content: Native PDF in AEM Guides uses HTML/CSS-based templates to produce PDF output without DITA-OT. A template has 4 components: (1) Page Layouts — define header, footer, body regions for different page types (cover, TOC, chapter, index); (2) Stylesheets — CSS files controlling typography, spacing, colors, table styling; (3) Resources — images, fonts, JavaScript files; (4) Settings — page size, margins, bleed, crop marks. Templates are stored in /content/dam/dita-templates/pdf/ in AEM. You can use JavaScript for dynamic content (auto-numbering, conditional headers, barcodes via QR code libraries).
common_mistakes: Editing the default template directly instead of duplicating it first — updates may overwrite customizations
Using px units in CSS instead of mm/cm/in — PDF requires print-friendly units
Forgetting to map DITA elements to CSS classes in the stylesheet — elements render with default browser styling
Not testing with long content — page breaks, widows/orphans, table splitting behave differently in PDF vs HTML
Adding JavaScript that depends on browser APIs (DOM manipulation) — Native PDF renderer has limited JS support
correct_examples: Create a custom template: Duplicate default template > edit page-layout.html for header/footer > edit stylesheet.css for typography > add company logo to resources/ > configure in output preset.
usage_contexts: Corporate PDF branding
Multi-language PDF with RTL support
Regulatory document formatting
Custom TOC styling with leader dots

text_content: A baseline in AEM Guides is a snapshot of specific versions of topics and assets in a DITA map, used to freeze content for publishing. Two types: (1) Label-based baseline — selects topic versions by AEM label (e.g., 'Release 2.0 Approved'); (2) Date-based baseline — selects versions as of a specific date/time. Baselines are created from Map Dashboard > Baselines tab. When generating output, select a baseline to ensure only approved content is published. Dynamic baselines auto-update when new versions with matching labels are created.
common_mistakes: Publishing without a baseline — includes latest (possibly draft) versions of all topics
Using date-based baseline without considering timezone — AEM stores dates in UTC
Creating a label-based baseline but forgetting to label all topics in the map — unlabeled topics use latest version
Not understanding dynamic vs static baselines — dynamic auto-updates, static is frozen
Deleting topic versions that are referenced by a baseline — baseline becomes invalid
correct_examples: Map Dashboard > Baselines tab > Create Baseline > Name: 'v2.0-release' > Type: Label-based > Label: 'Release_2.0' > Save. Then in Output Presets, select this baseline before generating.
usage_contexts: Release management
Regulatory compliance publishing
Multi-version documentation
Rollback to previous published state

text_content: Condition presets in AEM Guides filter content during output generation based on DITA profiling attributes (audience, platform, product, otherprops, etc.). A condition preset maps attribute values to include/exclude/flag actions — equivalent to a DITAVAL file but managed through the AEM Guides UI. Created from Map Dashboard > Condition Presets tab or Web Editor. Multiple presets can exist per map (e.g., 'Internal Only', 'Customer Facing', 'Linux Admin'). When generating output, select a condition preset to filter content.
common_mistakes: Applying conditions at output time but forgetting to profile the source content — all content passes through
Confusing condition presets with baselines — conditions filter by attributes, baselines filter by version
Creating overlapping conditions that exclude all content in a section — output has empty topics
Not testing condition combinations — some combinations produce invalid DITA (e.g., all steps excluded from a task)
Using props attributes without defining them in subject scheme — no validation in Web Editor
correct_examples: Map Dashboard > Condition Presets > New > Name: 'Linux Admin' > Set audience=admin to Include, platform=linux to Include, platform=windows to Exclude > Save.
usage_contexts: Multi-audience publishing
Platform-specific documentation
Internal vs external content filtering
Product variant documentation

text_content: The AEM Guides Web Editor is a browser-based DITA authoring environment with features: (1) Author view — WYSIWYG-like editing with element insertion toolbar; (2) Source view — raw XML editing; (3) Preview — rendered output preview; (4) Split view — author + preview side by side. Key features: Smart tag suggestions, content snippets, find/replace with regex, track changes, inline review comments, content reuse panel (conref/keyref browser), DITA map editor with drag-drop reordering, quick generate output, version compare, keyboard shortcuts.
common_mistakes: Editing in Source view without understanding DITA validation — easy to create invalid XML
Not using content snippets for repeated patterns — leads to copy-paste inconsistency
Forgetting to check in/unlock files after editing — blocks other authors
Using browser back button instead of Close — may lose unsaved changes
Not configuring custom element templates — authors insert bare elements without required children
correct_examples: Open topic in Web Editor > Author view > Place cursor > Insert Element (Alt+Enter) > Select 'note' > Type content > Save (Ctrl+S) > Check in.
usage_contexts: Daily DITA authoring
Content review and commenting
Quick output generation
Version comparison and merge

text_content: AEM Guides provides a built-in review workflow: (1) Author creates a review task from Map Dashboard or Web Editor, selecting topics and reviewers; (2) Reviewers receive email notification and open the Review UI; (3) Reviewers add inline comments, highlight text, suggest changes, and mark topics as reviewed; (4) Author views all comments in the Review Dashboard, accepts/rejects suggestions, and resolves comments; (5) Review task is closed when all topics are reviewed. Reviews support versioning — each review round creates a snapshot.
common_mistakes: Sending topics for review without checking them in — reviewers see stale versions
Not setting a review deadline — tasks remain open indefinitely
Reviewers editing topics directly instead of adding comments — bypasses the review workflow
Not closing review tasks after completion — clutters the review dashboard
Creating one review task with too many topics — reviewers get overwhelmed
correct_examples: Map Dashboard > Review > Create Review Task > Select topics > Add reviewers (email) > Set deadline > Add instructions > Send.
usage_contexts: SME review of technical content
Legal/compliance review
Peer review before publishing
Translation review

text_content: AEM Guides translation workflow: (1) Select topics/map from Map Dashboard > Translation tab; (2) Create translation project — choose target languages, translation provider, and scope; (3) AEM creates language copies; (4) Send to translation provider via Translation Integration Framework connectors; (5) Receive translated content; (6) Review translated topics; (7) Approve and publish. Supports incremental translation — only sends modified topics. XLIFF format for translation memory compatibility.
common_mistakes: Not setting xml:lang attribute on root topic element — translation tools cannot identify source language
Translating conref source files separately from consuming files — breaks content references
Forgetting to translate key definitions — keyref values remain in source language
Not using the AEM translation scope filter — sends unchanged topics for re-translation (cost waste)
Publishing translated content without applying translated condition presets
correct_examples: Map Dashboard > Translation > Create Project > Target: fr, de, ja > Provider: Human > Scope: Modified only > Create.
usage_contexts: Multi-language documentation
Localization pipeline
Incremental translation updates
Machine translation with post-editing

text_content: AEM Guides version management: Every save can create a new version (configurable). Features: (1) Version History — view all versions with timestamps, authors, comments; (2) Version Compare — side-by-side diff of two versions; (3) Revert — restore a previous version; (4) Labels — tag versions for baseline reference; (5) Purge — admin removes old versions. Labels are used by baselines to select specific versions for publishing.
common_mistakes: Not adding version comments — version history becomes unhelpful
Auto-versioning every save without purge policy — creates hundreds of versions per topic
Reverting a version without checking who else is editing — overwrites concurrent changes
Labeling versions inconsistently — baseline lookup fails
Deleting versions referenced by published baselines — breaks audit trail
correct_examples: Web Editor > Open topic > Make changes > Save > Version History > Add comment > Save as new version. Apply label 'Sprint-42-Approved'.
usage_contexts: Content lifecycle management
Audit trail compliance
Baseline-driven publishing
Collaborative editing conflict resolution

text_content: The Map Dashboard in AEM Guides is the central hub for managing a DITA map lifecycle. Tabs: (1) Output Presets — configure and generate output; (2) Baselines — create version snapshots; (3) Condition Presets — define filtering rules; (4) Reports — DITA map report, content reuse, conversion status, reverted files; (5) Translation — manage translation projects; (6) Bulk Activation — manage map collections for batch publishing.
common_mistakes: Generating output from Web Editor quick-generate instead of Map Dashboard — misses baseline and condition configuration
Not checking the DITA map report before publishing — broken links slip into output
Confusing Map Dashboard with Map Editor — Dashboard is for management, Editor is for authoring
Not setting up map collections for bulk activation — publishing maps one-by-one
correct_examples: Assets UI > Navigate to .ditamap > Click map name > Map Dashboard opens > Output Presets tab > Select preset > Set baseline > Set conditions > Generate.
usage_contexts: Publishing workflow
Release management
Content quality reports
Multi-language publishing

text_content: AEM Guides can publish DITA topics as AEM Content Fragments for headless CMS delivery via GraphQL API. To publish: Web Editor > Quick Generate > Content Fragment preset > Map DITA elements to CF model fields > Generate. Also supports Experience Fragment publishing for reusable page components.
common_mistakes: Not creating the Content Fragment Model before publishing — generation fails
Mapping complex DITA structures to simple text fields — content is flattened/lost
Publishing without resolving conrefs first — fragment contains unresolved references
Forgetting to publish the CF to the delivery tier — not available via GraphQL
correct_examples: Web Editor > Open topic > Quick Generate > Content Fragment > Select CF Model > Map fields > Generate > Publish to delivery.
usage_contexts: Headless CMS delivery
Multi-channel content
API-driven documentation portals
Mobile app content feeds

text_content: Bulk Activation in AEM Guides publishes multiple DITA maps at once via Map Collections. Steps: (1) Create Map Collection; (2) Add DITA maps; (3) Configure preset, baseline, conditions per map; (4) Activate entire collection. Supports scheduled and incremental activation.
common_mistakes: Adding maps without configuring presets — bulk activation skips unconfigured maps silently
Not using incremental mode for large collections — full publish takes hours unnecessarily
Deleting a map without removing it from collections — activation fails
Scheduling activation without verifying baselines — publishes stale content
correct_examples: Bulk Activation console > Create Collection > Add maps > Configure each: preset, baseline, conditions > Activate All.
usage_contexts: Product release publishing
Multi-map documentation sets
Scheduled off-hours publishing
CI/CD documentation pipeline

text_content: AEM Guides supports DITA content reuse through: (1) Conref — direct content reference by file path + element ID; (2) Conkeyref — content reference via key + element ID; (3) Keyref — key-based reference for variables; (4) Content Snippets — pre-built reusable XML fragments. The Web Editor shows a Content Reuse panel listing all reusable elements across the repository.
common_mistakes: Using conref with absolute paths instead of keyref — breaks when files are moved
Creating circular conref chains — causes infinite loop during publishing
Not validating conref targets exist — broken references show as empty in output
Mixing conref and conkeyref in the same element — only one mechanism per element
Reusing content across different topic types without checking content model compatibility
correct_examples: For variables: with keydef. For content blocks:
usage_contexts: Product name/version variables
Legal disclaimers and warnings
Common procedure steps
Standard UI labels

text_content: A DITAVAL file (.ditaval) defines conditional processing rules for filtering and flagging DITA content during output generation. Structure: <val> root containing <prop> elements specifying action (include/exclude/flag) for each profiling attribute value. Also supports <revprop> for revision flagging and <style-conflict> for resolving conflicting flags. In AEM Guides, DITAVAL rules are managed as Condition Presets in the Map Dashboard UI.
common_mistakes: Forgetting the default action — unmatched content is included by default
Using action='exclude' on required DITA elements (title, shortdesc) — creates invalid output
Not testing all condition combinations — some produce empty topics or broken cross-references
Applying DITAVAL to maps with conrefs without profiling sources consistently
Confusing action='flag' with action='exclude' — flag adds visual markers, exclude removes content
correct_examples: <val><prop action='exclude' att='audience' val='internal'/><prop action='include' att='audience' val='external'/></val>
usage_contexts: Multi-audience publishing
Platform filtering
Revision flagging
Product variant documentation

text_content: AEM Guides Map Dashboard reports: (1) DITA Map Report — broken links, missing images, metadata completeness; (2) Content Reuse Report — conref/conkeyref usage across repository; (3) Conversion Status Report — Word/InDesign to DITA jobs; (4) Reverted File Version History — files reverted to previous versions. Reports accessible from Map Dashboard > Reports tab.
common_mistakes: Not running the DITA map report before publishing — broken links appear in output
Ignoring content reuse report — duplicate content proliferates
Not filtering reports by status — overwhelming list
Relying only on reports without Web Editor validation — may miss DITA errors
correct_examples: Map Dashboard > Reports > Generate DITA Map Report > Filter: 'Broken Links' > Fix issues > Re-generate > Publish.
usage_contexts: Pre-publish quality gate
Content health monitoring
Reuse optimization
Migration progress tracking

text_content: AEM Guides Extension Framework (JUI Framework) customizes the Web Editor UI without modifying core code. Components: (1) Custom Panels; (2) Custom Toolbar Buttons; (3) Custom Widgets (dropdowns, labels, text areas); (4) Event Handlers; (5) Custom Review App. Built with React/JavaScript and deployed as AEM clientlibs.
common_mistakes: Modifying core Web Editor files instead of using extensions — updates overwrite customizations
Not testing extensions after upgrades — framework APIs may change
Creating heavy extensions that slow down the Web Editor
Not using the provided component library — custom HTML may break with theme changes
correct_examples: Create extension: apps/myproject/clientlibs/guides-extension/ > js/customPanel.js > register in extension.json > Deploy as clientlib.
usage_contexts: Custom authoring panels
Terminology validation
Automated quality checks
External system integration
